text: <par>There are two phonosemantically similar roots <xref ref="814">#814</xref> and <xref ref="2012">#2012</xref>.</par><par>For now, all Qiangic forms for HORN are assigned to these latter roots, rather than to the present root; all Himalayish forms are assigned to 814, while all Lolo-Burmese forms are deemed to descend from the present root.</par>

<par>Most reflexes of this etymon (possibly including WB <latinform>muik</latinform>) have a final velar stop, but other Burmese forms point to an allofam with final velar nasal. Several Himalayish languages of Nepal have borrowed an accidentally similar Indo-Aryan root with the meaning 'fist': Kanauri <latinform>muṭhu</latinform>, <latinform>mŭṭṭhu</latinform>, Chantyal <latinform>murki</latinform>, Kham <latinform>mutka</latinform> < cf. Skt. <emph>muṣṭi-</emph>, Pali <emph>muṭṭhi-</emph>.</par>
<par>This etymon was reconstructed as <latinform>b-yet ⪤ m-yet</latinform> in VSTB:211, but was inadvertently omitted from HPTB.</par>

善 OC *d̑i̯ɑn, GSR #205a-c 'good, approve, make good'; B & S 2011: *ɡenʔ {[ɡ]e[n]ʔ}; Mand. shàn.
繕 OC *d̑i̯ɑn, GSR #205f 'repair, put in order'; Mand. shàn.

<par>This root shows an unusual alternation of initial consonants, with both the dental and the velar onsets being well attested. Some languages have lost any direct trace of either of these initial elements, and show forms beginning with <latinform>r-</latinform> or <latinform>ʐ</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root appears mostly in Himalayish, with a possible cognate from Qiangic. The second syllable of Karbi <latinform>mak-phu</latinform> 'mons Veneris' seems unrelated. In view of the gloss it probably means something like 'swelling; protuberance'. It bears a resemblance to other reflexes of the well-attested root <reconstruction>*bwam ⪤ *pwam</reconstruction> PLUMP / SWOLLEN (STC #172; HPTB pp. 249, 252, 341, 518).</par>
<par>Mpi seems to have developed a doublet from this complex initial group.</par>
<par>Perhaps derived from PKC <latinform>*yaa</latinform> HUNDRED (VanBik 2009:281). See <xref ref="2258">#2258</xref>.</par>
<par>"The first morpheme is 'tooth'; the second morpheme can be identified with the classifier for long slender objects, PT *buŋ..." Sun 1993a:92.</par>

<par>Two forms with final velar stop (Bokar Lhoba <latinform>gum pɯk</latinform> and Tsangla Motuo <latinform>ʔa pak taŋ</latinform> 'mouth') seem at first glance to be related to this root. However, Bokar <latinform>nap pang</latinform> and Tsangla <latinform>no waŋ</latinform> 'mouth' suggest that a separate root is involved.</par><par>The resemblance of these stop-finalled forms to Thai (cf. Siamese <latinform>pàak</latinform> 'mouth') seems quite fortuitous.</par><par>So far the nasal prefix has only been found in Qiangic.</par>

<par>This etymon has a very likely connection with <xref ref="663">#663</xref>, illustrating the widespread alternation between dental stop and liquid in TB. See Matisoff 2013 ("The dinguist's dilemma").</par>

寔 OC *d̂i̯ək, GSR #866s 'this; this is; really'; Schuessler 2007:464 *dək; B & S 2011: (*dek); Mand. shí.
實 OC *d̂i̯ĕt, GSR #398a-c 'solid; true; really'; Schuessler 2007:464 *dit; Mand. shí.
直 OC *di̯ək, GSR #919a 'straight; right'; Schuessler 2007:616 *drək; B & S 2011: *N-t‹r›ək; Mand. zhí.正 OC *t̂i̯ĕng, GSR #833j 'first (lunar month)'; B & S 2011: *C.teŋ; Mand. zhēng.
正 OC *̵t̑i̯ĕng, GSR #833j 'straight, correct'; B & S 2011: *teŋ-s; Mand. zhèng.
政 OC *̵t̑i̯ĕng, GSR #833r-s 'government; rule, law; manage'; Schuessler 2007: *teŋh; B & S 2011: *teŋ-s ; Mand. zhèng.Comment: Schuessler 2007:464 considers 實 shí with the meaning "solid, true, really" to be a phonetic variant of 寔 shí in OC. He treats as a separate etymon 實 shí, when it means "fruit" (ibid.).

<par>This root appears in several TB subgroups, including Lolo-Burmese and Karenic, but is certainly an areal word; cf. Thai <latinform>cháaŋ</latinform> and Mandarin <hanform>象</hanform> <latinform>xiàng</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root seems to be confined to the NE Indian linguistic area.</par>

<par>This well-attested root occurs in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, and Lolo-Burmese, and probably in Karenic and Qiangic as well. It is reconstructed in STC #135, which only lists the WT and Lushai (Mizo) forms. The Jingpho forms <latinform>pràt</latinform>, <latinform>šəpràt</latinform> 'bear, give birth' do not belong in this set, since <latinform>-ŋ ⪤ -t</latinform> is not an established pattern of variation in TB word-families.<footnote>As a longshot, we might relate these Jingpho forms to <xref ref="1787">#1787</xref>, below.</footnote></par>
<par>The Himalayish forms with high vowels (Tamangic <latinform>-u/-i</latinform>, Pattani <latinform>-i</latinform>) are tentatively assigned to this etymon.</par>
<par>This promising root requires further confirmation.</par>

<par><hanform>梯</hanform> OC <latinform>*t’i̯ər</latinform>, GSR #591l 'wooden steps, staircase'; Schuessler 2007:493 <latinform>*thî ~ thə̂i</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*l̥ˁəj {l̥ˁ[ə]j}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>tī</latinform>.</par><par>The Chinese and Chepang comparisons are due to Bodman 1980.</par>
<par>This promising root requires further confirmation.</par>

<par>For now, we consider this etymon as distinct from <xref ref="1906">#1906</xref>, although several reflexes have <latinform>-i</latinform> vocalism; such <latinform>-i</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>-u</latinform> variation has so far only been solidly attested in closed syllables. The Qiangic form with <latinform>-i-</latinform> shows the expected "brightening".</par><par>The Apatani forms might also come from <xref ref="1819">#1819</xref>.</par>
<par><hanform>逗</hanform> S625 C141</par>

<par>This etymon has solid reflexes in the NE Indian Areal Group (Garo, Karbi) and Himalayish (Lepcha, Manchati). Several Chinese comparanda suggest themselves: <hanform>雄</hanform> OC <latinform>*gi̯ŭng</latinform>, GSR #887l 'male'; Mand. <latinform>xióng</latinform>, and/or <hanform>犅</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*kɑ̂ng</reconstruction>, GSR #697f-g, 'bull'; Mand. <latinform>gāng</latinform>. A less plausible comparison is with <hanform>公</hanform> OC <latinform>*kung</latinform>, GSR #1173a-f 'father; prince'; Mand <latinform>gōng</latinform> (although Karlgren notes that 'some of these forms seem to suggest a phallic interpretation'). STC (n. 488, p. 190) suggests comparing this last Chinese morpheme to TB forms like Rawang <latinform>əkhaŋ</latinform> 'grandfather'; WB <latinform>pha’-khaŋ</latinform> 'father', <latinform>mi’-khaŋ</latinform> 'mother', <latinform>khaŋ-pwân</latinform> 'spouse', and <latinform>khaŋ-bhya</latinform> 'sir; madam'. It seems unlikely, however, that this group of forms has anything specifically to do with maleness; it seems rather to have been an honorific appellation for an elder or respected relative of either sex.</par>

雄 OC *gi̯ŭng, GSR #887l 'male'; Li 1971: *gwjəng; Baxter 1992 #1348: *wjɨng; Mand. xióng.This proposed cognate is doubtful. The OC labialized initial plus schwa vocalism would normally correspond to a rounded vowel in PTB.
[ZJH]

犅 OC *kɑ̂ng, GSR #697f-g ‘bull’; Li 1971: *kang; Baxter 1992: *kang; Mand. gāng.The proposed cognacy works phonetically, but one must also note a competing etymology with equally persuasive semantics that relates this Chinese word to WT glang ‘cow, elephant’. (Gong 1995 and 2001 instead relate WT glang to OC 'elephant' 象 *gljangx > *ljangx.)Schuessler (2007:251) suggests that 犅 may come from earlier *klaŋ, and posits a relationship with Mru klaŋ 'male' and Lushai (Mizo) tlaŋ 'male', as well as with WT glang. He proposes a PTB root *laŋ with animal prefix *s- or *k-.See the previous etymon #1638.Note the parallel etymology with a homophonous member of the same phonetic series 岡 ‘ridge’ (Mand. gāng), which has been compared to WT sgang ‘hill, spur’ and WB khang ‘strip of high ground’ (see for example Coblin 1986:94-2).See also HPTB:266, 303. [JAM]
[ZJH]

<par>Contra STC #142, Lahu <latinform>bî </latinform> ‘full’ is not from this etymon, but rather from <xref ref="134">#134</xref>.</par><par>As discussed in Matisoff 1988 ("Straight, flat, full"), this root is allofamically related both to <xref ref="707">#707</xref> and <xref ref="2160">#2160</xref>, the common semantic core being "a complete or ideal realization of a quality appropriate to a particular dimensionality in space".</par>

贏 OC *di̯ĕng, GSR #816f 'surplus, profit; win'; Schuessler 2007:575 *leŋ; B & S 2011: *ɢeŋ {[ɢ]eŋ}; Mand. yíng.
盈 OC *di̯ĕng, GSR #815a-b 'fill; full; surplus'; Schuessler 2007:575 *leŋ; B & S 2011: (*leŋ); Mand. yíng.
孕 OC *di̯əng, GSR #945j 'pregnant'; Schuessler 2007:598 *ləŋh; B & S 2011: *liŋ-s {l[i]ŋ-s}; Mand. yùn.
<par>This root bears a strong resemblance to <xref ref="5647">#5647</xref>, and should perhaps be combined with it in the future.</par>

洗 OC *siən/siər, GSR #478j ‘wash'; Schuessler 2007:525 *sə̂iʔ/sî(n)ʔ/sə̂nʔ; B & S 2011: *sˁərʔ {[s]ˁərʔ}; Mand. xǐ.
洒 OC *siən/siər, GSR #594g-h 'wash'; Schuessler 2007:525 sə̂iʔ/sî(n)ʔ/sə̂nʔ; B & S 2011: *sˁərʔ {[s]ˁərʔ}; Mand. xǐ.
<par>Cf. the rather similar etymon <xref ref="6704">#6704</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>乾</hanform> OC <latinform>*g’i̯ɑn</latinform>, GSR #140c 'heavenly'; Schuessler 2007:425 <latinform>*gran</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*ɡran</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>qián</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> In Chinese philosophy, <hanform>乾</hanform> <latinform>qián</latinform> refers to "heaven, the male principle", the first of the <hanform>八</hanform><hanform>卦</hanform> <latinform>bā</latinform><latinform>guà</latinform>, i.e. the eight divinatory trigrams of the Book of Changes.</par>

<par>This allofamic reconstruction is based on the rGyalrongic reflexes, which vary between <latinform>rɲe</latinform> and <latinform>rme</latinform>. Still other rGyalrongic forms with back vowels fit better with <xref ref="363">#363</xref>.</par>
<par>This root is solidly attested in the NE Indian Areal Group (Chin [Lai], Naga [Lotha, Phom] and Meithei), but so far it has not been discovered anywhere else.</par>

<par>This root, attested in Qiangic and Naxi, usually refers to a bad smell, although certain forms are glossed with the opposite meaning of 'fragrant'.</par><par>For phonosemantically similar roots, see <xref ref="1421">#1421</xref> and <xref ref="810">#810</xref>.</par>

<par>Languages like Ao and Zeme have a sibilant fricative or affricate initial, although this may be chalked up to a widespread tendency to develop frication of <latinform>t</latinform> before <latinform>u</latinform>, as in Japanese.</par><par>Vowel length is reconstructed because of the WB rhyme <latinform>-uik</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>篤</hanform> OC <latinform>*tôk</latinform>, GSR #1019g 'firm, solid, thick'; Schuessler 2007:217 <latinform>*tûk</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>tˁuk</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>dǔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>Recommend untagging anomalous Mongsen Ao form. There is no other support for a PTB *t- > Mongsen s- development, and the source is not completely reliable.</par>
<par>This root is of restricted distribution, occurring chiefly in Bodic and rGyalrongic.</par>
<par>The basic meaning of this root is apparently STICK / BRANCH, with a semantic extension into TORCH, typically a pine branch used to light one's way at night.</par>

<par>This etymon has reflexes ranging from an affricate <latinform>ts-</latinform> (Lotha), to a simple dental stop <latinform>t- or th-</latinform>, to a dental sibilant (Mizo, Nocte), to an <latinform>h-</latinform> (Meithei, Moyon, Maring). [For a similar range of reflexes, cf. <xref ref="2742">#2742</xref>. </par><par>We can assume a development in Meithei from <latinform>*ts-</latinform> to <latinform>*s-</latinform> to <latinform>h-</latinform>; the Moyon and Maring forms might well be borrowings from Meithei.</par><par>VanBik reconstructs a binome <latinform>*tak-shaa</latinform> for Proto-Kuki-Chin, where the second element is the general etymon PTB <latinform>*sya</latinform> MEAT / FLESH / ANIMAL.</par>
<par>This root has been uncovered in both Qiangic and Loloish. The nasal prefix is reflected by the voiced Lahu initial.</par>
<par>This etymon is very possibly related to <xref ref="163">#163</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is evidently hypocoristic in origin (see also <xref ref="3459">#3459</xref>, above), and in fact is practically identical to the Indo-European root <emph>*mā-</emph> 'mother; breast' (> e.g. Latin <emph>mamma</emph>), of which the <emph>American Heritage Dictionary</emph> (1981:1527) says "An imitative root derived from the child's cry for the breast (a linguistic universal found in many of the world's languages, often in reduplicated form)." The final nasal in this etymon might have arisen through the reduction of an earlier reduplicated form <reconstruction>*ma-ma</reconstruction> (as in Ao, Sangtam, and Bunan).</par><par>There is a phonologically similar but apparently unrelated root <xref ref="3451">#3451</xref>, below.</par>
<par>This root is to be distinguished from the phonologically similar <xref ref="710">#710</xref> and <xref ref="711">#711</xref>.</par>
<par>This root has also acquired both the <latinform>-n</latinform> and <latinform>-t</latinform> suffixes; see <xref ref="2143">#2143</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>薄</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’ɑ̂k</latinform>, GSR #771p 'thin'; Schuessler 2007:170 <latinform>*bâk</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*bˁak {(Cə.)[b]ˁak}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bó</latinform>.</par>
<par>For detailed discussion of the numeral THREE, see Matisoff 1995 ("Numerals"), pp. 181-183.</par>

三 OC *səm, GSR #648a-c 'three'; Coblin 86:149 ST *gsum > OC *səm; Schuessler 2007:449 *sə̂m; B & S 2011: *srum {sr[u]m} (sr- > s-; infl. by 四 s- ?); Mand. sān.
參 OC *śi̯əm, GSR #647a-b 'three stars in Orion belt'; Schuessler 2007:449 *srəm; B & S 2011: *srum {sr[u]m}; Mand. shēn.
參 OC *ts’əm, GSR #647a-b 'unit of three; threefold'; Schuessler 2007:449 *tshə̂m < *k-sə̂m; B & S 2011: *N-sˁrum {N-sˁr[u]m}; Mand. cān.

<par>This etymon appears similar to <xref ref="3559">#3559</xref>, as suggested by the WB compound WB <latinform>lañ-ceʼ</latinform> ‘larynx’ (lit. ‘neck seed’).</par><par>On the other hand, several languages have distinct reflexes of these two roots, e.g. Chokri <latinform>tsa</latinform> 'seed' vs. <latinform>(u) ru¹¹ che³⁵</latinform> ‘windpipe/trachea’, and nor Darang <latinform>tɑ³¹ piɑi⁵⁵</latinform> ‘seed’ vs. <latinform>shig-ceig</latinform> ‘windpipe’, so that it seems best to keep them separate.</par>

<par>This root is attested in Jingpho, Kanauri, a few languages of Nepal, and especially in Tibetan. Cf. WT <latinform>ḥdon-pa</latinform>, pf. <latinform>bton</latinform>, fut. <latinform>gdon</latinform>, imp. <latinform>thon</latinform> 'cause to go out / cause to come forth'. Some reflexes have final <latinform>-ŋ</latinform> instead of <latinform>-n</latinform>. See STC p. 125.</par>

<par>Although this root is of quite limited distribution, the exact but unusual rhyme correspondence between Thulung and Nungic inspires confidence that this is a genuine etymon.</par><par>The first syllable of Rengma <latinform>te ro ha</latinform> 'feather' looks related but it might mean 'bird' (cf. <latinform>tegü</latinform> or <latinform>tegvu</latinform> 'bird'). Cf. <xref ref="1604">#1604</xref> (STC pp. 149, 192), attested otherwise only in Bodo-Garo and Karenic.</par>

<par>A possible Chinese comparandum is <hanform>屁</hanform> Mand. <emph>pì</emph> (cf. Mand. <hanform>放屁</hanform> <emph>fàngpì</emph> 'to fart' and <hanform>辟谷</hanform> <emph>pìgǔ</emph> 'buttocks'), although it is not clear whether it is attested in Old Chinese.</par>

<par><hanform>髀</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯ĕg</latinform> ~ <latinform>*bieg</latinform>, GSR #874f 'thighbone, haunch'; Schuessler 2007:164 <latinform>*peʔ</latinform> ~ <latinform>*bêʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*beʔ</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>bì</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root seems quite distinct from <xref ref="404">#404</xref>.</par>

<par>This looks like a valid Proto-Tani root, but it is possible that there is a connection with <xref ref="6680">#6680</xref>. Cf. English expressions like 'he got clean away', where the meaning is "action performed with a complete result".</par>

<par><hanform>妣</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯ər</latinform>, GSR #566a-f 'mother (deceased), ancestress'; Coblin 86:88 ST <latinform>*pyid </latinform> > OC <latinform>*pjidx</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:162 <latinform>*piʔ</latinform>~<latinform>*pih</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pijʔ</latinform>~<latinform>*pijʔ-s </latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bǐ</latinform>~<latinform>bì</latinform>.</par>

<par>The initial consonant reflexes of this root vary between affricates, dental stop, and <latinform>s-</latinform>, in a manner similar to <xref ref="2742">#2742</xref>.</par><par>Written Tibetan attests both the <latinform>b-</latinform> and <latinform>g-</latinform> prefixes.</par><par>Mizo has a doublet <latinform>dai-lêng</latinform> 'shit' and <latinform>tǔi-êk</latinform> 'rust', motivating the reconstruction of medial <reconstruction>*-w-</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>A number of languages (e.g., Burmese, Mizo) have developed simplex/causative pairs from this etymon. </par>

落 OC *glɑ̂k, GSR ＃766q' 'fall, drop; perish'; Schuessler 2007:371 *râk; B & S 2011: *kə.rˁak; Mand. luò.
露 OC *glɑ̂g GSR #766t' 'dew'; Schuessler 2007:366 *râkh; B & S 2011: *p.rˁak-s; Mand. lù.
下 OC *gʼɑ̊, GSR #35a 'down; descend'; Schuessler 2007:527 *grâʔ 'down', *grâh 'descend'; B & S 2011: *ɡˁraʔ 'down', *m-ɡˁraʔ-s 'descend'; Mand. xià.Schuessler (2007:371) attributes OC initial *r- in 落 *râk to Austroasiatic influence, where OC initial *l- might otherwise be expected based on comparison with TB *(C‑)l- for this etymon. In this regard, however, Matisoff identifies "...several etyma where PTB *(C-)l- seems to correspond to Baxter’s *(C)-r-, including ‘fall’, ‘good’, ‘neck’, ‘salty’, ‘strength / arm’, and ‘young man / husband’"(HPTB:53). We reiterate here Matisoff's (ibid.) claim that the relationship between TB *l-/*r- and OC *l-/*r- is complex and has yet to be fully clarified. Apparent irregularities in correspondences between TB/OC *l-/*r- may therefore not always be attributable to loans to or borrowings into TB/OC.陊 OC *d’iɑ, GSR #3o-p 'fall down, collapse'; Schuessler 2007:619 *d-laiʔ ; B & S 2011: (*t-lajʔ); Mand. duò.
阤 OC *d’iɑ, GSR #4z 'fall down, collapse'; Schuessler 2007:619 *d-laiʔ; B & S 2011: (*t-lajʔ); Mand. tuó ~ zhì.
<par>Mikir and Khoirao have a dental stop instead of the lateral. Kham shows preemption by the <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix.</par>
<par>This root has so far been identified only in Himalayan languages.</par>

<par>The basic meaning of this etymon seems to be SEED, with extensions into SMALL ROUND OBJECT, and thence to TESTICLE.</par><par>This morpheme frequently occurs as the last syllable in words for EYE, as does the semantically similar root <xref ref="1019">#1019</xref>.</par><par><xref ref="3559">#3559</xref> is quite distinct from <xref ref="1019">#1019</xref>, as witness the WB forms <latinform>sî</latinform> 'fruit' vs. <latinform>ə-ceʼ</latinform> 'seed'. </par>

<par>This mythical animal has been associated with WATER, RAINBOW, and THUNDER. Some forms which look as if they might descend from this root should actually be assigned to <xref ref="2465">#2465</xref>.</par>

龍 OC *li̯ung, GSR #1193a-e 'dragon'; Gong 2001:24 *m-bruŋ / *m-bruk; B & S 2011: *mə-roŋ {[mə]-roŋ}; Mand. lóng.
隆 OC *gli̯ông, GSR #1015f 'thunder, sound of thunder'; Schuessler 2007:363 *ruŋ; B & S 2011: *ruŋ {[r]uŋ}; Mand. lóng.
雹 OC *b’ộk, GSR #1113n 'hail'; B & S 2011: *C.bˁruk {C.[b]ˁruk}; Mand. báo.Gong 2001:24 and HPTB #585 compare the current etymon, PTB *m-bru(ŋ/k) 'dragon; rain', with Chinese 龍 lóng 'dragon'. Both these etyma may be allofamic with 雹 báo 'hail', as suggested by the latter's reconstruction as *C.bˁruk in B & S 2011.[SPB]

<par>This root bears a strong resemblance to <xref ref="1794">#1794</xref>. For now, we are assigning reflexes with open syllables or final <latinform>-t</latinform> to the present etymon, and those with final velars to <xref ref="1794">#1794</xref>.</par><par>If these two roots were to be combined, it would necessitate a reconstruction like <latinform>səw-t ⪤ səwk</latinform>.</par>
<par><hanform>嗽</hanform> Cf. #1794 root variation?</par>
<par>For a phonosemantically similar root, see <xref ref="310">#310</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed in STC #382, which cites the WT, Jingpho, Bodo, and Dimasa forms. The Bai and Lolo-Burmese forms look cognate, but the relationship of the Jinuo form to this set is uncertain. The semantic range of this etymon, which extends from the notion of birth to that of sprouting, greenness, freshness, is parallelled by another root <xref ref="71">#71</xref> (q.v.).</par>

拍 *mə-pʰˤrak; pāi slap, pat, swat, beat, strike
伐 *m-pat; fá attack, strike
<par>This root is quite distinct from <xref ref="819">#819</xref>.</par>
<par>This stronghold of this etymon is Lolo-Burmese, where several forms overtly carry the nasal prefix, which is also indirectly attested by the voiced initials in languages like Lahu.</par>

<par>This etymon has so far been uncovered almost exclusively in the NE Indian Areal Group, where it is widely attested. The Lailenpi and Lotha forms point to a prototype with nasal prefix, <reconstruction>*N-yaŋ</reconstruction>. Many of the daughter languages have developed initial <latinform>z-</latinform> in this etymon. In several languages, the meaning of this word extends to 'stinger of bee', which perhaps is the original meaning. For the stinging semantics, cf. vulgar English <emph>prick</emph>.</par><par>An excellent extra-NE Indian candidate for cognacy is Jingpho <latinform>rāŋ</latinform> 'to be concupiscent; to be driven by sexual desires; to burn with lust' (Hanson 1906/1954:564), recorded as <latinform>ʒaŋ³³</latinform> in Dai, et al. 1983:684, where it is glossed <hanform>性冲动</hanform> 'sexual impulse'. See the compound <latinform>ne³¹ ʒaŋ³³</latinform> 'sexually potent male', under <xref ref="545">#545</xref>, above.</par>
<par>This allofam has so far only been uncovered in a few TB languages of Nepal.</par>

<par><hanform>覯</hanform> OC <latinform>*ku</latinform>, GSR #109j 'come across, meet with'; Coblin 86:72 ST <latinform>*kuk</latinform> > OC <latinform>*kugh</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:258 <latinform>*kôh</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kˁo-s {kˁ(r)o-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>gòu</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is quite widespread, and covers a broad semantic range, from BREAST to SUCK to DRINK (any liquid). By a relatively recent extension of meaning, this root is also used for SMOKE (tobacco), and thence for TOBACCO itself (as in Lahu).<footnote>The extension of 'drink/suck' to 'smoke (tobacco)' is common in the world's languages. Cf. e.g. Japanese <emph>nomu</emph> 'drink', <emph>tabako wo nomu</emph> 'smoke a cigarette'.</footnote></par>

<par>There is a promising Chinese comparandum <hanform>欶</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>shuò</latinform> proposed by Coblin (1986:144). In Matisoff 1970 (#57) I suggested that WB <latinform>sok</latinform> (also transcribable as <latinform>sauk</latinform>) 'drink, smoke' and Lahu <latinform>šú</latinform> 'tobacco' were cognate. I still believe that to be correct, even though in Matisoff 1988:1192 (<emph>The Dictionary of Lahu</emph>) I entertained an alternative comparison with WB <latinform>hrup</latinform> 'snuff up; sniff; sip; sup'. It now looks as if WB <latinform>hrup</latinform> might be related rather to Chinese <hanform>呷</hanform> (OC <reconstruction>*hrap</reconstruction>, Mand. <latinform>xiā</latinform>) 'to drink in with a sucking movement', cited in Coblin 1986:43. This seems preferable semantically and phonologically to Coblin's comparison of the Chinese form to WB <latinform>hap</latinform> 'bite at' < PTB <reconstruction>*hap</reconstruction> [STC #89].</par>

欶 OC *sŭk, GSR #1222o 'suck'; Li 1971: *sruk; Baxter 1992: *srok; Mand. shuò.Gong 1995 #279 reconstructed *rsuk. In Li’s system another possibility is *sthruk, by analogy with 束 ‘bundle’, Mand. shù which is reconstructed *sthjuk to account for the presence in this series of 誎 / 促 ‘urge on', Mand. cù < *tshjuk. Schuessler 1987:567 reconstructed 束 shù ‘bundle’ as *?-juk to indicate that the initial is uncertain, and in 2007:473 suggests a pre-Old Chinese form *C-sok.This comparison is made in Coblin 1986:144. The vowel correspondence is problematic. OC *-uk (Li)/*-ok (Baxter) normally corresponds to PTB *-uk, as in 曲 'bend/crooked', PTB *guk~*kuk, OC *khjuk (Li)/*kh(r)jok (Baxter), Mand qū, qǔ.
[ZJH]

呷 OC *χap (not in GSR #629), 'to drink with a sucking movement'; Li 1971: *hrap; Baxter 1992: *xrap; Mand. xiā.The Middle Chinese vocalism dictates an Old Chinese reconstruction with medial *-r-, but Schuessler 2007:526 has *hap, arguing that the MC vocalism may be due to "sound symbolism or archaistic colloquialism". This enables him to make a comparison with PTB *hap [STC #89] as well as with Austroasiatic forms of similar shape.Matisoff's proposal that 呷 xiā is instead cognate to WB hrup 'snuff up; sniff; sip; sup' provides a better match for Chinese *-r-, but the vocalism is problematic. We would expect a Burmese cognate in -ap < PTB *-ap. Of course, given the likelihood of sound symbolism in words with these semantics, irregular correspondences are to be expected. It is therefore not an easy matter to decide if Chinese 呷 xiā is better compared to PTB *hap or WB hrup, or if the similarity of phonological shape is not due to cognacy at all.
[ZJH]

<par>The velar suffix is reconstructed on the basis of the Jingpho final glottal stop, as well as the WB creaky tone. The dental suffix is reconstructed courtesy of the WT and Padam-Mishing [Abor-Miri] forms.</par>

<par>Three different prefixes are attested with this root: Written Tibetan has <latinform>s-</latinform>, but some modern dialects have <latinform>r-</latinform>. The voiced initial in the Lahu form reflexes a nasal prefix at the PLB level.</par><par>The Written Tibetan doublet <latinform>skam-po</latinform> 'dry' and <latinform>skom-pa</latinform> 'thirst' strongly supports the reconstruction of <reconstruction>*-wa-</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>This root bears some resemblance to <xref ref="2347">#2347</xref>, the common core of meaning perhaps residing in the dry sound made by fallen leaves.</par>

顑 OC *χəm~*k’əm, GSR #671m 'emaciated, gaunt'; Coblin 86:148-9 ST *khəm > OC *khəmx; Schuessler 2007:332 *hə̂ms~*khə̂mʔ; Mand. kǎn.
歁 OC (*k’əm), (not in GSR #658) 'famished'; Schuessler 2007:332 *khə̂mʔ; Mand. kǎn.
<par>TJLB #257, #132;  Also *g<latinform>ʼi̯ăn </latinform>(impt), <latinform>gʼi̯at</latinform> stubborn <latinform>gʼi̯wət.</latinform>?</par>

<par><hanform>拳</hanform> OC <latinform>*gʼi̯wɑn</latinform>, GSR #226g 'force, forceful; fist; strength'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*N-kron {N-kro[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>quán</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymon bears a strong resemblance to <xref ref="1840">#1840</xref>, and should probably be recognized as an allofamic version of it.</par>
<par>This root is well-attested in Himalayish, but has so far not been identified elsewhere.</par>

<par><hanform>甛</hanform>/<hanform>甜</hanform> OC (<latinform>*d’i̯ɑm</latinform>), (not in GSR #606) ‘sweet'; Coblin 86:145 ST <latinform>*ƚiim</latinform> > OC <latinform>*diəm</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:496 <latinform>*lêm ~ lîm</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>tián</latinform>.</par>

<par>Lahu <latinform>co</latinform> 'one's life; period of time' looks superficially like the present root, but is actually a borrowing from Tai (cf. Shan <latinform>soo</latinform>, Siamese <latinform>chûa</latinform>, White Tai <latinform>čoo</latinform>. See DL:478.</par>

<par>This Karenic etymon seems to be a loan from Mon-Khmer. Cf. PMK <latinform>*ʔseh</latinform> > Old Mon <latinform>kṣeḥ</latinform>, Mod Mon <latinform>cheḥ</latinform>, Old Khmer <latinform>seḥ</latinform>, Mod Khmer <latinform>seh</latinform>. See Shorto 2006:#2089.</par>
<par>It is possible that some of the open syllable forms listed here belong rather under <xref ref="3596">#3596</xref>.</par>

<par>The basic meaning of this etymon seems to be 'break out'. It is reconstructed in STC #254 as <reconstruction>*be ~ *pe</reconstruction> 'broken; break', though the present reconstruction with <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction> seems preferable.</par><par>The Mizo form with final glottal stop suggests a secondary allofam with the <latinform>-s</latinform> suffix.</par>
<par>Further confirmation of this root would be welcome.</par>

<par><hanform>辛</hanform> OC <latinform>*si̯ĕn</latinform>, GSR #382a-f 'pungent, bitter; painful'; Coblin 86:44 ST <latinform>*msyin</latinform> > OC <latinform>*sjin</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:538 <latinform>*sin</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*sin {[s]i[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xīn</latinform>.</par>
<par>noun or verb?</par>

掌 OC *t̑i̯ɑng GSR #725j 'palm, sole'; Li 1971: (*tjangx); Baxter 1992 #231: *tjangʔ (#231); B & S 2011: *[k.t]aŋʔI do not understand why Baxter & Sagart (2011) reconstruct a cluster *k.t- instead of plain *t- here. Their notation with a period (as opposed to a hyphen as in *k-t-) indicates that they have no evidence for a morphological prefix *k-, but I don’t see any motivation for reconstructing a purely phonological pre-initial element *k.; Mand. zhǎng.
[ZJH]

<par>The Jingpho forms with final <latinform>-k</latinform> are loans from Burmese, since PTB <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction> regularly becomes Jingpho <latinform>-ʔ</latinform>. Jingpho <latinform>byáʔ</latinform> is apparently the regular cognate to the Burmese forms.</par>

犛 OC *li̯əg, GSR #979j 'ox'; Schuessler 2007:348; Mand. lí, máo.
needs revision ≠ glang ox

籠 OC *lung, GSR #1193l 'cage, coop'; Schuessler 2007:363 *rôŋ; B & S 2011: *k.rˁoŋ; Mand. lóng.
籠 OC *lung, GSR #1193l 'basket; cage'; Schuessler 2007:363 *rôŋʔ; B & S 2011: *k.rˁoŋʔ; Mand. lǒng.

<par>This root is distinct from <xref ref="1852">#1852</xref>. Written Tibetan preserves both etyma: <latinform>lba-ba</latinform> from the present root and <latinform>ḥbur</latinform> from <xref ref="1852">#1852</xref>.</par>
<par>perhaps<latinform>⪤ *sak</latinform> ROUGH/SCALY.</par>

<par>The Jingpho binome means literally "hard-liver" (cf. <latinform>məsìn</latinform> 'liver', which is opposed to <latinform>sìn-wóp</latinform> 'lungs', lit. "soft/spongy liver"). This metaphorical antonymy is discussed at length in <plainlatinform>VSTB:198-9</plainlatinform>.</par>
<par>This root is much less widespread than the resemblant <xref ref="540">#540</xref> and seems distinct from it.</par>

<par>As is frequently the case with pronouns, there is quite a bit of variation with this etymon. In particular, the Loloish languages have multiple reflexes for YOU (sg.), YOU (du.) and YOU (pl.). A number of languages have an open-syllable allofam, but most reflexes have the final velar nasal.</par><par>See e.g. <latinform>nɔ̀-hɨ</latinform>, <latinform>nì-hɨ</latinform>, <latinform>nɨ̀-hɨ</latinform> 'you (pl.)' (<plainlatinform>DL</plainlatinform> 792). For an analysis of pronominal paradigms in Loloish, see Matisoff 1993 ("Sangkong").</par>

汝 OC *ńi̯o, GSR #94j-k 'you (sg.)'; B & S 2011: *naʔ; Mand. rǔ.
若 OC *ńi̯ɑk, GSR #777a 'you (sg.)'; B & S 2011: *nak; Mand. ruò.
爾 OC *ńiɑ, GSR #359a 'your' ; B & S 2011: *neʔ {ne(j)ʔ}; Mand. ěr.
而 OC *ńi̯əg, GSR #982a-b 'your'; Schuessler 2007:226 *nə; B & S 2011: *nə; Mand. ér.
乃 OC *nəg, GSR #945a 'your' ; B & S 2011: *nˁəʔ {nˁə(ŋ)ʔ}; Mand. nǎi.

<par>There is a phonologically similar Tai root (cf. Tai-Nuea <latinform>pe⁴</latinform>, Siamese <latinform>phɛɛ</latinform>). This looks like a Southeast Asian areal word. See also <xref ref="5781">#5781</xref>.</par>

迆 OC *diɑ, GSR #4r 'slant; deflect'; Schuessler 2007:565 *lai; Mand. yí.
迤 OC *diɑ, GSR #4i' 'turn aside (as of a river's course)''; Schuessler 2007:565 *lai; Mand. yí.
阤 OC *d’iɑ, GSR #4z 'slope'; Schuessler 2007:565 *d-laiʔ; Mand. zhì.

HPTB p.332 (first table note a):
"STC reconstructs *bwat, and JAM 2000a:#7 reconstructs *bʷat, but the Proto-Loloish high-stopped tone points to a voiceless prefix at the PLB level, undoubtedly *s- (*s-watᴴ), a reduction of the morpheme *siŋ ⪤ *sik 'tree' that is the first element of the compound meaning 'flower' in several languages (e.g. Nung, Lahu)."

匈/胷/胸 OC *χi̯ung, GSR #1183d-f 'breast(bone); chest'; Schuessler 2007:541 *hoŋ; B & S 2011: (*qʰoŋ {qʰ(r)oŋ}); Mand. xiōng.
膺 OC *ʔi̯əng, GSR #890e 'breast, chest; breastplate; resist, oppose'; Coblin 86:85 ST *ʔƚjəng > PC *ʔljəng > OC *ʔjəng; Schuessler 2007:574-5 *ʔəŋ; B & S 2011: *qəŋ {[q](r)əŋ}; Mand. yīng.
應 OC *ʔi̯əng, GSR #890d 'to face, respond; correspond, agree'; Schuessler 2007:575 *ʔəŋh; B & S 2011: (*qəŋ-s); Mand. yìng.
應 OC *ʔi̯əng, GSR #890d 'ought to, must; rightfully'; Schuessler 2007:575 *ʔəŋ; B & S 2011: (*qəŋ); Mand. yīng.
<par>Several forms seem to reflect a variant with a final velar nasal: WB <latinform>lañ-tuing</latinform>, <latinform>jak-tuing</latinform> and Sulong <latinform>kə³³tuoŋ³³</latinform>.</par>

脰 OC *d’u, GSR #118d 'neck'; B & S 2011: *kə.dˁok-s; Mand. dòu.
髑 OC *dʼu̯k, GSR #1224m 'skull'; B & S 2011: *dˁok {[d]ˁok}; Mand. dú.

<par>This etymon resembles <xref ref="1860">#1860</xref>, but must be kept distinct, as witness the two Jingpho forms <latinform>myéʔ</latinform> from the present root, and <latinform>mat</latinform> from <xref ref="1860">#1860</xref>.</par>

爚 OC *di̯ok, GSR #1119f 'shine; melt'; Schuessler 2007:477 *liauk; B & S 2011: (*rewk); Mand. yuè.
濯 OC *d’ŏk, GSR #1124h 'brilliant, fine; sleek, glossy; moisten, wash'; Schuessler 2007:632 *d-liauk; B & S 2011: *lˁrewk; Mand. zhuó.
曜 OC *di̯og, GSR #1124i 'shine; brightness'; Schuessler 2007:477 *liaukh; B & S 2011: *lewk-s; Mand. yào.
的 OC *tiok, GSR #1120h 'bright, brilliant; mark in a target'; Schuessler 2007:631 *tiâuk; B & S 2011: *t-lˁewk {[t-l]ˁewk}; Mand. dì.
灼 OC *t̂i̯ok, GSR #1120f 'burn; brilliant; illuminate'; Schuessler 2007:631 *tiauk; B & S 2011: (*t-qewk); Mand. zhuó.
爍 OC *śli̯ok, GSR #1125n 'melt, fuse; shine'; Schuessler 2007:477 *lhiauk; B & S 2011: *r̥ewk (dial. r̥- › x-, palatalizing); Mand. shuò.The following two forms are graphic variants of 燿 yào, with essentially the same meaning:燿 OC *di̯og, GSR #1124j 'shine, gleam'; Schuessler 2007:477 *liaukh; B & S 2011: (*lewk-s); Mand. yào.
耀 OC *di̯og, GSR #1124k 'shine'; Schuessler 2007:477 *liaukh; B & S 2011: (*lewk-s); Mand. yào.
<par>The Tibetan - Chinese comparison is due to Gong 2001, set 31.</par>

<par>If WT <latinform>rkaŋ</latinform> is indeed descended from this root, we must assume a radical simplification of the complex <latinform>*rkly-</latinform> sequence to <latinform>rk-</latinform>.</par>

牼 OC *k’ĕng ~ *g’ĕng, GSR #831q-r 'shank bone (of ox)'; Schuessler 2007:335 *khrêŋ; B & S 2011: *m-kʰˁ‹r›eŋ; Mand. kēng.
脛 OC *g’ieng, GSR #831k 'leg, shank'; Schuessler 2007:541 *gêŋh; B & S 2011: *m-kʰˁeŋ-s; Mand. jìng ~ xìng.
莖 OC *g’ĕng, GSR #831u 'stalk; hilt of sword'; Schuessler 2007:335 *grêŋ; B & S 2011: *m-k-lˁ‹r›eŋ; Mand. jīng ~ héng.

<par>We recognize three quite similar etyma in this semantic area: the present root <xref ref="5639">#5639 </xref>, <xref ref="225">#225</xref>, and <xref ref="3322">#3322</xref>. In the case of phonologically eroded languages (e.g. Loloish, Qiangic, Baic), it is sometimes difficult to decide among them. Several Loloish languages have distinct reflexes for at least two of them, e.g. Lahu <latinform>tɔ̀</latinform> 'lower back / loins / lumbar region' < <xref ref="225">#225 </xref> vs. <latinform>ɔ̀-mɛ̄-tu</latinform> 'tail' < <xref ref="5639">#5639 </xref>; Written Burmese <latinform>taŋ-pâ</latinform> 'buttock' < <xref ref="225">#225 </xref> vs. <latinform>khâ-tuiŋ</latinform> 'lower back / loins / lumbar region' < <xref ref="3322">#3322 </xref>. Two Southern Loloish binomes are particularly problematic (Akha <latinform>dɔ̀-dàŋ</latinform> 'buttocks' and Sangkong <latinform>toŋ³¹ taŋ⁵⁵</latinform> 'buttocks'), where the first syllables are from <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>, while the second syllables are of uncertain origin despite their superficial resemblance to <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon is well-attested in Karenic and apparently also in Lolo-Burmese (see note above under <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref>), with scattered but good-looking cognates in the NE Indian Areal Group (Meithei), Himalayish, and Asakian (Jingpho-Asakian).</par>

<par>In Chart L (Sak-Luish Group) of Luce 1985, under the confusing gloss "Penis/Testicles", two forms are given from Bawtala Sak, one from Dodem Sak, two from Ganan, and two from Kadu (Kantu). Where two forms are given from the same language, separated by a semicolon, I have assumed that the first one means 'penis' and the second one 'testicles', and have reglossed them accordingly. Under this interpretation, some syllables are assigned to the present root; while others, phonologically quite similar, are taken to mean 'testicle', and are assigned to <xref ref="300">#300</xref>, above [q.v.].</par><par>Rather arbitrarily, I assign reflexes with stop initials to this root and those with fricated or palatalized initials to <xref ref="1601">#1601</xref>.</par><par>Like the previous etymology <xref ref="3420">#3420</xref>, some reflexes of this root have a final <latinform>-k</latinform>.</par><par>The resemblance to English <emph>dick</emph> appears entirely accidental.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root requires further confirmation.</par>

<par>Suffixal <latinform>*-n</latinform> is set up on the basis of the Chinese comparandum <hanform>猿</hanform> OC <latinform>gi̯wɑ̆n</latinform> GSR #256c, Mand. <latinform>yuán</latinform> 'ape'.</par>

猿 OC *gi̯wɑ̆n, GSR #256c 'ape'; Schuessler 2007:593-4 *wan; Mand. yuán.
猨 OC *gi̯wɑ̆n, GSR #255d 'ape'; B & S 2011: *ɢʷan {[ɢ]ʷa[n]}; Mand. yuán.

<par><hanform>船</hanform> OC <latinform>*di̯wɑn</latinform>; GSR #229e 'boat'; Schuessler 2007:195-6 <latinform>*m-lon</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*Cə.lon {Cə.lo[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>chuán</latinform>.</par>
<par>This disyllabic Kiranti root has so far not been discovered elsewhere.</par>

<par>The unusual <latinform>*-y</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*-ŋ</latinform> alternation in coda may also be interpreted as an instance of a nasal suffix (<latinform>*s-ley-N ⪤ *s-rey-N</latinform>), although such a suffix is usually reconstructed for the dental point of articulation.</par><par>The first syllable of the Kuki-Chin binomes is obviously from a general root for rodents, <xref ref="2796">#2796</xref>.</par>

狌 OC *si̯ĕng, GSR #812t; Mand. shēng.
鼪 OC *si̯ĕng, GSR #812u; Mand. shēng.

<par>A root <reconstruction>*laːy</reconstruction> is set up in STC #287 with the meaning MIDDLE/CENTER, on the basis of WB <latinform>ălai</latinform> and Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>lai</latinform>.<footnote>This root, widespread in Chin languages, is also used in an ethno-geographical sense. The glossonym <emph>Lai</emph> is used broadly for a group of languages in the <emph>central</emph> subdivision of the Chin area in Burma, and more narrowly for the language of Hakha, its most important town.</footnote> Elsewhere, STC presents two additional roots: <reconstruction>*s-tay</reconstruction> 'navel' (STC #299), based on WT <latinform>lte-ba</latinform>, Jingpho <latinform>dài ~ šədāi</latinform>, Garo <latinform>ste</latinform>; and <reconstruction>*tay</reconstruction> 'self' (STC #284), based on Jingpho <latinform>dāi</latinform> (also <latinform>dāidāi</latinform>) and Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>tei</latinform>. Yet Benedict himself implies by a cross-reference (p. 65) that these latter two roots are really one and the same. I wish to go a step further to claim that all three STC roots (#284, #287, #299) are co-allofamic.</par><par>The interchange of <latinform>l-</latinform> with dental stops occurs in several other TB roots, including TONGUE and ARROW (and this phenomenon is known from other language families as well, notably Indo-European, where by coincidence TONGUE is also one of the best examples (cf. Latin <emph>lingua</emph> vs. pGmc <emph>*tungōn-</emph>), alongside TEAR (cf. Latin <emph>lacrima</emph> vs. pGmc <emph>*tahr- ⪤ *tagr-</emph>), BROTHER-IN-LAW (cf. Latin <emph>lēvir</emph> vs. Lithuanian <emph>dieverìs</emph>), etc.<footnote>See Matisoff 2013 ("The dinguist's dilemma"). See also the discussion in HPTB, pp. 50-53.</footnote></par><par>There is strong evidence for both a nasal and a sibilant prefix with this root (occasionally with both together, as in the Ashö Sandoway form <latinform>ă`hmlai¹</latinform> 'navel' < <reconstruction>*s-m-laːy</reconstruction>). However, the appearance of a cluster like <latinform>tl-</latinform> in TB words for SELF or NAVEL does not by itself indicate the co-allofamy of the stop and lateral prototypes, since <latinform>tl-</latinform> is frequently the reflex of <reconstruction>*sl-</reconstruction> (especially in Kuki-Chin and other languages of the NE Indian Areal Group), so that these forms could be referred directly to <reconstruction>*s-laːy</reconstruction>. See the Lakher (Mara), Miju, Bantawa, and Kham forms below.</par>

<par>In forms like Ao <latinform>²tɯ ²pɯ ²la</latinform>, Rengma <latinform>¹a ²bvɯ ³li</latinform>, it might be plausible to treat the second syllables like dimidiations (syllabizations) of the labial prefix <reconstruction>*bV- ⪤ *mV-</reconstruction>, since in that position many other languages have elements with unstressed vowels (transcribed <latinform>pə-</latinform>, <latinform>mə-</latinform>, <latinform>pü-</latinform>, etc.). On the other hand, when the quality of the vowel in such a syllable is clearly [<latinform>u</latinform>], it seems preferable to analyze this element as descending from <xref ref="164">#164</xref> or <xref ref="2111">#2111</xref>. This is especially clear in Himalayish: cf. the Bantawa and Kham reflexes.</par><par>Both the lateral- and stop-initialled variants of this etymon are widely attested in TB. In the following sections, the reflexes are divided between the two allofams.</par><par>The semantic scope of this etymon is already quite broad, but I would like to claim that there is also a genuine phonosemantic connection with a root meaning BELT / ZONE / WAIST (see below).</par><par>There is an interesting Dumi compound <latinform>saː◦khil◦tɨm</latinform> 'navel' composed of <latinform>saː◦khil</latinform> 'viscera, guts' < <latinform>khil</latinform> 'feces', plus <latinform>tɨm</latinform> 'head, leader': i.e. the navel is viewed in Dumi as the leader of the intestines. See van Driem 1993:415.</par>
<par>See HPTB <reconstruction>*laːy ⪤ *m/s-taːy</reconstruction>, pp. 52, 210.</par>
<par>As roots for RAINBOW go, this is not the worst attested. It seems to be especially common in Loloish.</par>

The WT forms <latinform>so-rnyil</latinform> and <latinform>snyil</latinform> illustrate this prefixization process.</par>
<par>Lahu has a doublet here: <latinform>ga</latinform> from <latinform>*m-ga³</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>kà</latinform> from <latinform>*gra¹</latinform>.</par>

<par>Tibetan has evidence for both the <latinform>-l</latinform> and <latinform>-n</latinform> finals.</par><par>This root is quite possibly related to <xref ref="5531">#5531</xref>; this open-syllable variant is also attested directly in Tibetan.</par><par>Written Burmese <latinform>rây</latinform> 'brave' is a possible doublet of <latinform>ran</latinform> 'quarrel'.</par>
<par>Cf. comparanda for <xref ref="5570">#5570</xref> and for <xref ref="5531">#5531</xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon is very likely allofamic with <xref ref="2722">#2722</xref>, illustrating the widespread alternation between dental stop and liquid in TB. See Matisoff 2013 ("The dinguist's dilemma").</par>

<par><hanform>良</hanform> OC <latinform>*li̯ɑng</latinform>, GSR #735a 'good'; Schuessler 2007: (<latinform>*raŋ</latinform>); B & S 2011: <latinform>*raŋ {[r]aŋ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>liáng</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment</latinform>: For <hanform>良</hanform> <latinform>liáng</latinform>, recently reconstructed as OC <latinform>*raŋ</latinform>, to be a valid comparandum, we would ideally wish to see an initial <latinform>*r-</latinform> in the PTB form here. One possible recourse is to assume an alternation <latinform>*l(y)a(k/ŋ)</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*r(y)a(k/ŋ)</latinform> at the PST stage.</par>

<par>This root is quite similar to <xref ref="778">#778</xref>, although the difference in point of articulation of the final leads us to keep them separate.</par><par>The Kham form with nasalized vowel could plausibly be assigned to either root.</par>

<par>This etymon bears a resemblance to <xref ref="5442">#5442</xref>, so that it is difficult to assign reflexes in eroded subgroups like Loloish to one or the other. Further microlinguistic work is necessary.</par>

<par>The variable vocalism in this root was perhaps the reason why Benedict considered it to be a loan from Austro-Tai <latinform>*(m)baḷi</latinform>. However, several other roots show <latinform>*-ey</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*-ay</latinform> variation, including NEAR; TONGUE; RICE / PADDY; PASS / EXCEED; BRIDGE; and BAMBOO STRIP. See GSTC #s 55, 56, 57, 58, 130, and 133.</par><par>Cf. the confusing note in Benedict (1975a:82), where he suggests that two separate borrowings from Austro-Tai are involved, both of which overlap with a native root. See also STC #283 and note 205, p. 64.</par><par>Several different prefixes are attested with this root, with their relative antiquity yet to be determined.</par>

<par>This rather rare but valid-looking root often occurs as the second syllable of compounds where the first syllable descends from <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>. There seems to be a connection with the notion of 'fist'.</par>
<par>This root seems to show vowel gradation. For a similar pattern of variation, see <xref ref="1417">#1417</xref> and <xref ref="1447">#1447</xref>.</par>
<par><hanform>勃</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’wət</latinform>, GSR #491b 'powdery (soil)'; Schuessler 2007:171 <latinform>*bə̂t</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bó</latinform>.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root resembles PTB <latinform>*ra</latinform> GOAT, but appears to be an independent morpheme.</par>
<par>This root is quite promising, but further confirmation would be welcome.</par>

<par>This root very frequently occurs in compounds, either with <xref ref="681">#681</xref> or <xref ref="803">#803</xref>. See e.g. Lipho <latinform>pha²¹me̱³³</latinform> (with <xref ref="681">#681</xref>) vs. Nusu (Central) <latinform>pʰɹɑʴ⁵⁵ n̥ɑ³⁵</latinform> (with <xref ref="803">#803</xref>). To complicate matters further, the EYE morpheme may occur in either order with <xref ref="1186">#1186</xref>. See e.g. Lahu <latinform>mɛ̂ʔ-phû</latinform> vs. Lisu <latinform>hpi⁵-myá³</latinform>.</par>

<par>This binome is found in Written Tibetan <latinform>ri dwags</latinform>, with the extremely rare initial <latinform>dw-</latinform> in the second syllable. This binome has been widely borrowed in languages influenced by Tibetan (Damu, Menba, Tamangic, Qiangic, rGyalrongic).</par><par>For the first syllable, see <xref ref="6076">#6076</xref>.</par>

<par>Besides Lolo-Burmese, where it is widely attested (even in Jinuo), this etymon has solid reflexes in Qiangic (Shixing) and Bai. The suggested NE Indian Areal Group cognate (in Mao) is uncertain; this form might better be assigned to <xref ref="1632">#1632</xref> (above), like Angami <latinform>kru</latinform>. This root was reconstructed for PLB in Matisoff 1978b:6 ("Mpi and Lolo-Burmese microlinguistics"); cf. also Matisoff 1988a:917-8. This is a good example of the development of *labiovelar initials into Lahu labials, as also in DOG (PTB <reconstruction>*kʷəy</reconstruction> > Lahu <latinform>phɨ̂</latinform>); see also STC n. 83 (p. 26). Note the doublets in Hani (Khatu) and Naxi (Lijiang); the Naxi forms clearly show alternative labial and velar reflexes of the complex *labiovelar initial. Compounds for WOMB/PLACENTA typically have the structure CHILD + NEST. Compounds for SCROTUM have the structure TESTICLE + NEST.</par>

窠 ‘burrow, nest’; GSR: not in #351; Li 1971: (*khwar); Baxter 1992: (*kʷhaj); Mand. kē.This character is not in GSR and is not reconstructed by either Li or Baxter. Based on its phonetic element and its Middle Chinese reading, however, its OC reconstruction is not in doubt. The word occurs in the Shuowen Jiezi, attesting to its existence in the first century AD. The Shuowen entry reads 空也。從穴。果聲。一曰鳥巢也。在樹曰巢。在穴曰窠。 ("Empty. [The character is formed] from [semantic element] 穴 and 果 is the phonetic. Another meaning is bird’s nest. When in a tree it is called cháo, in a cave it is called kē.") The element 果 depicts an object in a tree and is part of the pictograph in 巢, Mand. cháo ‘nest’. In 窠 it may be serving as a semantic as well as a phonetic element.The connection with PTB was proposed by Handel (1998). It establishes a correspondence between PTB labiovelar initials and Chinese labiovelar initials. The vowel correspondence, however, is problematic, since PTB final *əy usually corresponds to OC *-id (Li)/*-əj (Baxter); see the examples under the discussion for #300.
[ZJH]

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par><par>Darang has an apparent doublet <latinform>kʰɑ³¹ lo⁵³</latinform> 'palate' and <latinform>ɕɯ³¹tɯ³¹gɹu⁵³</latinform> 'throat'.</par>

This root is solidly established for rGyalrongic.
It is sometimes difficult to distinguish reflexes of this etymon from those of #1235 in Qiangic proper.

<par>This etymon is apparently distinct from <xref ref="5695">#5695</xref>. An <latinform>*m-</latinform> prefix is attested in Kaman [Miju] <latinform>mphlɑt³⁵</latinform>, while an <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix is attested in Daofu <latinform>sphro</latinform>. The Burmish and Dulong forms support the variant with a nasal final. (This etymology is due to Liberty Lidz.)</par>

<par>This is by far the most widely attested etymon for PENIS in TB, occurring in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese (including Jinuo), and Qiangic. (See STC #262, which cites reflexes from the first three of these groups.) The nasal prefix is well-established, appearing e.g. in WT and other Himalayish languages. This prefix has caused nasalization of the root-initial lateral in several languages (e.g. Meithei and Jingpho), or even driven out or "preempted" the root-initial entirely (as in Chang Naga or Lahu). Those forms which have developed a secondary nasal root-initial are presented separately below as set <xref ref="545">#545</xref>. A couple of Loloish languages (Lalo, Lisu) have reflexes in <latinform>h-</latinform>. It is possible that these point to an alternant with sibilant prefix, <reconstruction>*s-ley</reconstruction> (as is reflected more directly in Phunoi <latinform>hle¹¹</latinform> and Lepcha <latinform>săli</latinform>). Several Loloish languages (Lalo, Lipho, Lolopho, Nesu, Sani/Nyi) have words for PENIS and TESTICLE with initial <latinform>d-</latinform>. Since it is true that there is much interplay between <latinform>l-</latinform> and <latinform>d-</latinform> in ST,<footnote>See the note under <xref ref="518">#518</xref>, above.</footnote> and it is also the case that palatalized labial initials are sometimes dentalized in Loloish languages like Sani,<footnote>E.g. PTB/PLB <reconstruction>*my-</reconstruction> > Sani <latinform>n-</latinform> (e.g. <xref ref="33">#33</xref> > Sa. <latinform>ne⁴⁴</latinform>); <reconstruction>*by-</reconstruction> > Sani <latinform>dl-</latinform> (e.g. <xref ref="2187">#2187 </xref> <reconstruction>*bya</reconstruction> BEE > Sa. <latinform>dla-ma</latinform>); <reconstruction>*py-</reconstruction> > Sa. <latinform>tl-</latinform> (e.g. <xref ref="2588">#2588</xref> > Sa. <latinform>tlö</latinform>); see STC, n. 93, p. 29.</footnote> it might seem reasonable to assign these reflexes in <latinform>d-</latinform> to <reconstruction>*m-ley</reconstruction> if we posit an intermediate palatalized prototype like <reconstruction>*myey</reconstruction>. However, Lalo has forms with both <latinform>d-</latinform> and <latinform>h-</latinform> (<latinform>de³³se²¹</latinform> 'testicle', <latinform>he²¹</latinform> 'penis'), which suggests that two separate etyma are involved. I am therefore assigning the reflexes in <latinform>d-</latinform> to <xref ref="641">#641</xref>, below.</par>

<par>Several languages have forms that resemble reflexes of this etymon, but which are really loans from Indo-Aryan (cf. Skt. <emph>lingam</emph>): WB <latinform><emph>lin</emph>-khu’</latinform> 'scrotum', Kanauri <latinform>liṅ</latinform> 'penis (polite)'.</par>

<par>The meaning of the first syllable in this reconstructed form is unknown.</par><par>Another Karenic form for SWEAT, Pa-O <latinform>tə́ʔ-so</latinform>, has been assigned to <xref ref="60">#60</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is quite distinct from <xref ref="363">#363</xref>. Compare, e.g. WB <latinform>mwê</latinform> < <latinform>*s-mul</latinform> vs. <latinform>hmaŋ</latinform> < <latinform>*s-ma-ŋ</latinform>.</par>
<par>The stronghold of this root is Tani, but it also appears as a productive body part prefix in Angami, and has developed the meaning CORPSE / DEAD BODY in Tangut.</par>

<par>This etymon requires further review. It is perhaps distinct from etymon 418 *tsik JOINT (although the reflexes look similar) and occurs mostly as the second element of compounds in Loloish languages.</par>
<par>This etymon strongly resembles <xref ref="56">#56</xref>. The Lahu form could descend from either prototype.</par>

<par>This root resembles <xref ref="598">#598</xref>, and Phunoi seems to show variation between an open and a stopped reflex. We tentatively include the Phunoi forms under this open syllable etymon because Phunoi does preserve PTB <reconstruction>*-p</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>The Tibetan-Chinese comparison is due to Gong 2001:28.</par>

曰 OC *gi̯wɑ̆t, GSR #304a-d 'say'; Schuessler 2007:595 *wat; B & S 2011: *ɢʷat {[ɢ]ʷat}; Mand. yuē.
話 OC *g’wɑd, GSR #302o 'speak, word'; Schuessler 2007:283 *gwrâ(t)s or *grô(t)s; B & S 2011: *gʷˤrat-s {[g]ʷˤrat-s}; Mand. huà.

<par><hanform>卜</hanform> OC <latinform>*puk</latinform>, GSR #1210a-d 'divine using pyromancy (i.e. by interpreting cracks produced in a tortoise shell or ox bone exposed to intense heat)'; Schuessler 2007:172 <latinform>*pôk</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pˁok</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bǔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymon has so far been found only in a few Kuki-Chin languages.</par>

<par>This root seems to be fairly widely distributed across subgroups, although with scant attestation in each. There is some slight evidence for an <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix (Achang [Xiandao] <latinform>l̥ɿ⁵⁵ɲ̊ɔʔ⁵⁵<emph>l̥aŋ³¹</emph>kam⁵⁵</latinform>).</par><par>At first glance, Bodo <latinform>zay klóŋ</latinform> 'rainbow' seems to reflect a velar prefix with this root, but it is at least equally probable that the second syllable actually means 'bridge' (cf. Bodo <latinform>sayklóŋ</latinform>), the semantic association residing in the arched appearance of the rainbow.</par>
<par>The independence of this root from <xref ref="5621">#5621</xref> is demonstrated by the Darang binome <latinform>grõ-khru</latinform>.</par>

<par>Interestingly, this root can either mean RICE or THAT WHICH IS EATEN WITH RICE. Cf. the basic distinction made in Siamese between <latinform>khâaw</latinform> 'rice' amd <latinform>kàp-khâaw</latinform> 'accompaniment to rice'.</par><par>For a phonosemantically similar root, see <xref ref="3531">#3531</xref>.</par>
<par>This root is best attested in Lolo-Burmese, but (contra STC pp. 203, 213) it is also to be found elsewhere, e.g. Shixing / Xumi, Baic, and Karenic.</par>
<par><hanform>裕</hanform> OC <latinform>*gi̯ug</latinform>, GSR #1202h 'indulgent, abundant, opulent'; Schuessler 2007:589 <latinform>*lokh</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yù</latinform>.</par>

<par>The nasal prefix is directly attested in Namuyi. Several other languages (e.g., Proto-Northern Naga, rGyalrongic) seem to have undergone preemption of the initial dental by the nasal prefix.</par><par>The Karenic cognates display the homorganic final nasal.</par>
<par>Scour residue for reflexes.</par>

<par>This is definitely an areal word, found in Mon-Khmer and Tai as well as in TB and perhaps Chinese. The meaning ranges from 'small weight or coin' to 'bean, pea'. Shorto 2006 (#1489, p. 400) reconstructs Proto-Mon-Khmer <latinform>*ɓa(a)y</latinform> 'bean; small weight or coin' (cf. Mon <latinform>boa</latinform> 'bean, pea, 1/16 tical'); also Thai <latinform>pʰay</latinform> '1/32 tical'.</par><par>Shorto also suggests a connection with Amoy/Hokkien <latinform>pè</latinform> or <latinform>poè</latinform> <hanform>貝</hanform> (cf. Mand. <latinform>bèi</latinform>).</par><par>The present PTB reconstruction is a revision of STC #253, where the root is reconstructed <latinform>*be</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root shows variation between a palatalized velar and a palatalized dental affricate initial. Note the variant Jingpho forms.</par><par>In this case, both Written Burmese and Mizo unambiguously reflect a long vowel (<reconstruction>*-in</reconstruction> > WB <latinform>-añ</latinform>, while <reconstruction>*-iːn</reconstruction> > WB <latinform>-in</latinform>). See HPTB:277.</par>

均 OC *ki̯wĕn, GSR #391c 'even, equal, uniform'; Schuessler 2007:328 *kwin; B & S 2011: *C.qʷin {C.qʷi[n]}; Mand. jūn.
鈞 OC *ki̯wĕn, GSR #391d 'weight of 30 kin'; Schuessler 2007:328 *kwin; B & S 2011: (*C.qʷin {C.qʷi[n]}); Mand. jūn.

<par><hanform>隻</hanform> OC <latinform>*t̑i̯ɑ̌k</latinform>, GSR #1260c; Coblin 86:114 ST <latinform>*gtyik</latinform> > OC <latinform>*tjik</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:614 <latinform>*tek</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tek</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>zhī</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root has so far been best attested in Tani, where it has an adverbial meaning. In a few other languages, its meaning is restricted to the body part.</par>

<par>The final <latinform>*-n</latinform> is reconstructed on the basis of Chinese. There is probably a relationship between this root and <xref ref="5739">#5739</xref>, which has more abstract, grammatical meaning.</par><par>The velar prefix is attested both in Qiangic (including Tangut).</par>

<par><hanform>遠</hanform> OC <latinform>*gi̯wɑ̆n</latinform>, GSR #256f 'far'; Schuessler 2007:594 <latinform>*wanʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*C.ɢʷanʔ {C.ɢʷa[n]ʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yuǎn</latinform>.</par>

<par>The typical word for earwax in TB languages is a compound EAR + SHIT. Cf., e.g. Lotha <latinform>E No chu</latinform> 'earwax', where the last syllable is from <xref ref="572">#572</xref>. It is quite possible that some of the forms grouped under the present etymon may have a similar origin.</par>

<par>This etymon has been identified in Himalayish, Jingpho, and Qiangic, though more support from the latter group would be welcome. The PTB root initial is taken to have been a *lateral, with both a labial nasal and a labial stop prefix attested.</par><par>According to K. P. Malla, the second syllable of Newar <latinform>mi jɔ̃</latinform> 'male' is from Skt. <emph>jana-</emph> 'man', not from the present root. The first syllable is from the widespread TB morpheme <xref ref="1002">#1002</xref>.</par><par>J. Sun 1993:131 reconstructs Proto-Tani <reconstruction>*mrak</reconstruction>, but the forms he cites are better assigned to <xref ref="548">#548</xref>, above.</par><par>This root seems distinct from <xref ref="1624">#1624</xref>.</par><par>Also to be brought into this etymon is WB <latinform>lâŋ</latinform>, which (as suggested in Matisoff 1995:52-3 ["Sino-Tibetan palatal suffixes revisited"]) is to be compared with Chinese <hanform>郎</hanform> (Mand. <latinform>láng</latinform>) [WHB <reconstruction>*C-rāŋ</reconstruction>], now meaning 'young man; bridegroom; clf. for sons'. This word is glossed only as 'place name (Tso); double roof, one roof above the other' in GSR #735r. However, it would be rash to infer that the synchronic meaning did not exist at all in spoken OC.</par><par>See the remarks by Handel, below.</par>

郎 OC *lɑ̂ng, GSR #735r ‘husband, young man’ ; Li 1971: *lang; Baxter 1992: *C-rang; Mand. láng.The meaning 'husband, young man' is not attested until quite late. It appears to be derived from the basic sense 'veranda or corridor (of a palace or mansion)' via 'official (doing duty there)', making it an improbable cognate to PTB roots meaning 'male', cf. Schuessler 2007:344.Schuessler suggests that WB laŋ 'husband' is actually a loan from Chinese, though this seems unlikely in view of the paucity of Chinese loanwords in Burmese. [JAM] Furthermore, nearly all scholars are now in agreement that Middle Chinese l- derives from earlier *r-, and does not normally correspond to PTB *l-.
[ZJH]

<par>STC #426 originally reconstructed this root as <latinform>*r-ta-t</latinform>, but this was revised in note 291 to <latinform>*r-tas</latinform>, where the <latinform>s</latinform> is considered part of the root, not a suffix.</par>

粗 *N-sˁra 'coarse, thick'
鹵 *rˁaʔ {[r]ˁaʔ} 'rustic, coarse'

<par><hanform>癰</hanform> OC <latinform>*ʔi̯ung</latinform>, GSR #1184h 'swelling, ulcer'; Schuessler 2007:577 <latinform>*ʔoŋ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*qoŋ {q(r)oŋ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yōng</latinform>.</par>

Mandarin 黃油 <latinform>huángyóu</latinform> 'butter' (literally, "yellow oil").</par>

<par>This etymon is well-attested in Burmish, with good-looking but scattered cognates in Loloish, the NE Indian Areal Group, and Himalayish. It seems best to reconstruct this root with a palatal (rather than dental) affricate, as demonstrated in Matisoff 1969 (cf. the discussion in STC, n. 178 (p. 53)). Some reflexes of this etymon are cited in Bauer's far-ranging article (Bauer, R. 1991. "Sino-Tibetan *vulva", LTBA 14.1:147-172).</par><par>See TBRS #77, #77a, #77b.</par>

<par>This root is extremely well-attested, in Lolo-Burmese and many other branches of the family. In some languages (Kiranti, Tani, Karbi), it has been specialized to refer to animal births.</par><par>A variant with nasal final is also sporadically found.</par><par>The Old Chinese comparandum is reconstructed with final <latinform>*-t</latinform>, but this could well be a secondary development from an original velar after a front vowel. <plainlatinform>STC</plainlatinform> compares this Chinese etymon with PTB <xref ref="2708">#2708</xref>, a meaning which is not given in <plainlatinform>GSR</plainlatinform>, but which appears in Erh Ya.</par>

<par>Like other roots reconstructed with <reconstruction>*pʷ-</reconstruction>, the reflexes of this etymon vary between labial stops and <latinform>w-</latinform>, <latinform>v-</latinform>, or zero initial.<footnote>See Matisoff 2000, "An Extrusional Approach to <latinform>*p-/w-</latinform> Variation in Sino-Tibetan."</footnote> This root also shows the widespread Tibeto-Burman patterns of variation between medial <latinform>-i-</latinform> and <latinform>-u-</latinform>, and between final <latinform>-l</latinform> and <latinform>-n</latinform>.</par><par>A key doublet justifying this reconstruction are the Lushai (Mizo) forms <latinform>pil</latinform> and <latinform>vun</latinform>, both cited in Marrison 1967:233.</par><par>The Sunwar forms with initial <latinform>s-</latinform> and final <latinform>-l</latinform> (<latinform>'ku-<emph>sul</emph></latinform> 'skin', <latinform><emph>'sul</emph>-cā</latinform> 'feel (by touch)', <latinform>kru<emph>sul</emph></latinform> 'bark (of tree)', <latinform>ku<emph>sul</emph> 'u:-cā</latinform> 'skin') might also be related, if we assume an intermediate form like <reconstruction>**s-wul</reconstruction>. Cf. also French's (1983) Proto-Northern-Naga reconstructions <reconstruction>*wur</reconstruction> SKIN and <reconstruction>*swun</reconstruction> FLESH. </par>
