傍 OC *b’wɑ̂ng, GSR #740m’ ‘at the side of; assist’; Mand. bàng.ZJH: 膀 bǎng is not attested in early Chinese sources, which is the reason it is not found in GSR or the other sources for OC reconstruction (the reconstructions I have provided may therefore be anachronistic); it is either an old colloquial/dialectal term that was not recorded in texts, or a later development within Chinese. Schuessler (2007:170) raises the possibility that it derives from Chinese 膊 *pâk ‘shoulder blade’ (Mand. bó). But in terms of semantics and phonetics, 膀 bǎng is a good match for the TB root.
<par>The <latinform>s-</latinform> prefix is attested in the WB variant <latinform>swai'</latinform>, and perhaps by Lushai <latinform>hui</latinform>.</par>
<par><hanform>逶</hanform> winding, curving / swagger <hanform>委</hanform> > bend, hang down</par>

<par><hanform>丈</hanform> OC <latinform>* d̑’i̯ɑng</latinform>, GSR #722a 'measure of ten feet; older male, husband'; Schuessler 2007:606 <latinform>*draŋʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*draŋʔ {[d]raŋʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>zhàng</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Cf. comparanda for <xref ref="2725">#2725</xref>.</par>

<par>As mentioned above, a number of languages have forms with open syllables and initial <latinform>l-</latinform>, <latinform>y-</latinform>, or <latinform>d-</latinform> with the meaning 'foot'. Although this might seem strange, it cannot be excluded that these etyma for <emph>hand</emph> and <emph>foot</emph> are somehow related. This is rather analogous to the situation with NOSE and EAR, which are reconstructed as <reconstruction>*s-na</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*r-na ⪤ *g-na</reconstruction>, respectively.</par><par>The prefixal <latinform>lə</latinform> in Jingpho forms referring to the feet are assigned to this etymon, while similar referring to the hand are interpreted as deriving from <xref ref="695">#695 </xref>. This ambiguity is the result of the weakening of the rhyme in the prefixization process.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root occurs in only a few TB languages of NE India.</par>
<par>This root is solidly established for Lolo-Burmese. The Pa-O (Karenic) form may be a borrowing from Burmese.</par>
<par>Note the difference between the present reconstruction and that of Bradley 1979. The Lahu reflex is consistent with either.</par>

<par>This etymon is a simplex/causative pair, with the former marked by the nasal prefix and the latter by prefixal <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction>. Written Tibetan shows variation of the final nasal (<latinform>mthoŋ</latinform> 'see' ⪤ <latinform>ston</latinform> 'show').</par>

<par><hanform>黨</hanform> OC <latinform>*tɑ̂ng</latinform>, GSR #725r 'know, understand'; Schuessler 2007:206 <latinform>*tâŋʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tˁaŋʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>dǎng</latinform>.</par>
<par>related to <xref ref="600">#600</xref>?</par>

<par>This rather sparsely attested root has an apparently strange range of meaning, including both GUMS and PALATE, perhaps explicable by their common reddish color.</par><par>The second element of the Lahu compound <latinform>cì-qhɔ</latinform> is a possible phonological descendant of this root, but is better assigned to <xref ref="820">#820</xref>.</par><par>It seems likely that there is a connection between this root and <xref ref="473">#473</xref>.</par>

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>

<par>This Karenic etymon is probably to be identified with <xref ref="2393">#2393</xref>, which is of Mon-Khmer origin. See <plainlatinform>STC</plainlatinform> n. 225, p. 72. The final stop is paralleled in the Chinese comparandum mentioned in <plainlatinform>STC</plainlatinform>, <emph>loc. cit</emph>.</par>
<par>This is apparently a Southeast Asian areal word; see also <xref ref="4159">#4159</xref>.</par>

方 OC *pi̯wɑng, GSR #740a-f 'two boats lashed together; lash boats together; cross (a river by boat)'; Schuessler 2007:231 *paŋ; B & S 2011: *paŋ; Mand. fāng.
舫 OC *pi̯wɑng ~ *pwɑ̂ng, GSR #740g-h 'boat'; Schuessler 2007:231 *paŋh; B & S 2011: *paŋ-s ~ *pˁaŋ-s; Mand. fǎng.
<par>This weakly attested root has so far been found only in Tani and Himalayish.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed as <latinform>*m-glun</latinform> (HPTB 73, 125, 198), largely because of the voiced initial in the OC comparanda <latinform>*d̑i̯ĕn</latinform> and <latinform>*di̯ən</latinform> (GSR #368h 'kidney' and #450h-i 'small of the back'). On the TB level, however, it now seems best to reconstruct it with a voiceless root initial.</par>

腎 OC *d̑i̯ĕn, GSR #368h 'kidney'; B & S 2011: *ginʔ {(g)i(n)ʔ}; Mand. shèn.
夤 OC *di̯ĕn, GSR #450h-i 'small of the back, region of the reins'; B & S 2011: *ɢər {ɢ(r)ə(r)}; Mand. yín.
<par>HPTB (p.73) erroneously reports GSR <latinform>#450h-i</latinform> 'small of the back' as <latinform>#540h-i</latinform>.</par>
<par>[Internal Note:] <emph>GSR <latinform>#450h-i</latinform> now cited correctly in note above and in comparandum below</emph>. [spbaron]</par>

<par>Lepcha <latinform>a-ší</latinform> looks like a possible reflex of this root, but the lack of a final <latinform>-p</latinform> is a problem. This root was formerly reconstructed as <latinform>*sep</latinform> (HPTB:376).</par><par>The final velar in the Limbu form is unexplained.</par>

<par>We set up this root provisionally as being separate from the much better attested <xref ref="805">#805</xref>. The Yimchungrü form is included here because of the velar final, despite its anomalous initial.</par>
<par>This root, although apparently valid, is still no better attested than it was in STC #34.</par>

<par><hanform>物</hanform> OC <latinform>*mi̯wət</latinform>, GSR #503h 'variety, sort, class, thing; to sort'; Schuessler 2007:520 <latinform>*mət</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*C.mut</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>wù</latinform>.</par>

<par>This pronominal element appears sometimes as a prefix and sometimes as a suffix. See the discussions in STC pp.93, 121, 123, 130.</par><par>There may well be a relationship between this etymon and <xref ref="5450">#5450</xref>.</par>
<par>This root appears in Kiranti as well as in the TGTM group, and there may well be cognates in other Himalayan languages.</par>

<par>The initial cluster <latinform>*tr-</latinform> is extremely rare in TB. While the evidence from PCC and TGTM is convincing, the Karenic forms apparently descend from a simple dental stop. However, the Karenic allofam with medial <latinform>-w-</latinform> suggest that some sort of glide should be reconstructed at the PTB level.</par>

<par>Jingpho <latinform>pràt</latinform>, <latinform>šə pràt</latinform> 'bear, give birth' are probably to be assigned to this etymon, the semantic connection being the separation of the child from the mother.</par>

Likely comparanda are:裂 OC *li̯ɑt, GSR #291f; Mand. liè 'tear asunder; divide'
別 OC *b'i̯ɑt, GSR #292a; Mand. bié 'divide, separate, distinguish, different'.
<par>See <reconstruction>*brat ⪤ *prat</reconstruction> 'cut apart, cut open', HPTB:330,334.</par>

<par><hanform>別</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯ɑt</latinform>, GSR #292a 'divide, separate'; Li 1971: <latinform>*pjiat</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>*prjat</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bié</latinform>.</par><par>There is a related Chinese word with voiced initial, GSR <reconstruction>*b'i̯ɑt</reconstruction> (<emph>ibid.</emph>), also written <hanform>別</hanform>, meaning 'to be different'.  This voicing alternation is a common Old Chinese morphological process seen in transitive/intransitive verb pairs.  Although the TB etymon also has a voicing alternation, this is likely to be an independent phenomenon.</par><par>The Chinese and PTB forms correspond perfectly.  This cognate set has been proposed by Gong (1995 #209).  Gong 2001 reconstructs the intransitive Chinese verb as <reconstruction>*N-brjat</reconstruction> > <reconstruction>*brjat</reconstruction> and the transitive counterpart as <reconstruction>*s-brjat</reconstruction> > <reconstruction>*s-prjat</reconstruction> > <reconstruction>*prjat</reconstruction>.</par>

列 OC *li̯ɑt, GSR #291a 'divide, distribute, arrange, rank'; Li 1971: *ljat; Baxter 1992: *C-rjat; Mand. liè.裂 OC *li̯ɑt, GSR #291f 'tear asunder, divide'; Li 1971: *ljat; Baxter 1992: *C-rjat; Mand. liè.These two homophonous Chinese words are clearly related. Gong (2001) relates 裂 liè to the two words written with 別 bié, while Schuessler (2007:167) says that 別 bié is probably cognate to 列 liè.Schuessler gives the Baxter system reconstruction of 別 as *prjet. While this is a possible reconstruction, *prjat is more likely given the word-family connections. Gong reconstructs 裂 liè as *brjat > *rjat, treating it as the root from which the two words written with 別 bié are derived.On Baxter's *C-r-, see the discussion under #3438. The relationship to 別 bié suggests that in Baxter's reconstruction the consonant represented by *C would most likely be reconstructed as *b.
[ZJH]

<par>A number of languages in Eastern Nepal have developed final labials in this root, also showing alternation between final stop and nasal: <reconstruction>*rwam ⪤ *rwap</reconstruction>. We regard these forms as secondary, due most likely to assimilation of the final to the labial glide.</par><par>It is possible that Karbi <latinform>rèng-</latinform> 'die / be dead' and Dumi <latinform>rəŋ</latinform> gəliːm 'soul / the disincarnate spirit of a dead person' are also somehow related, perhaps reflecting an allofam like <reconstruction>*ryaŋ</reconstruction>.</par><par>Also to be compared to this root is <xref ref="1801">#1801</xref> with lateral initial.</par>
<par>Bodo shows frequent variation between intervocalic <latinform>-d-</latinform> and <latinform>-r-</latinform>, as witness the alternate name Boro for the language.</par>
<par>The nasal prefix is attested directly in Tibetan dialects and indirectly by the voiced Lahu initial.</par>

乏 OC *b’i̯wɑ̆p, GSR #641a 'lack; exhaust; neglect'; Schuessler 2007:234 *bap; B & S 2011: *bop {[b](r)[o]p}; Mand. fá.
廢 OC *pi̯wɑ̆d, GSR #275f 'cast aside; remove; suppress; neglect; cease; fall'; Schuessler 2007:234 *pats; B & S 2011: *pap-s; Mand. fèi.

甥 OC *sěng, GSR #812d 'sister's son or daughter; son-in-law'; B & S 2011: *s.reŋ; Schuessler 2007:460 OCM *srêŋ; Mand. 甥 shēng 'sister's son'; 甥女 shēngnyǔ 'sister's daughter'.Schuessler 2007:460 posits a connection between Chinese 甥 shēng 'sister's offspring' and WT sring-mo 'sister (of a male)'.
[SPB]

<par><hanform>姓</hanform> OC <latinform>*sri̯ěŋ</latinform>, GSR #812q 'clan / family / family name'; Mand. <latinform>xìng</latinform>.</par><par>This word is reconstructed as <latinform>*si̯ěng</latinform> in GSR #812q, but this was revised in STC to OC <reconstruction>*sri̯ěŋ</reconstruction> (STC: p.108, n.304 and p.171, n.304).</par>

<par>Although there is a similar Tai etymon represented by Shan <latinform>khɔɔ</latinform>, this now seems to be a genuine PTB root. See Matisoff 2001: #30, 'The interest of Zhangzhung for comparative Tibeto-Burman'.</par>

<par>This root is attested in Himalayish (including Tsangla), Abor-Miri-Dafla, and also perhaps in Chang Naga. The Lepcha and rGyalrong forms are good evidence for prefixal <latinform>t-</latinform>, which seems to have preempted the root-initial in all the other forms.</par>
<par>The mesoroot attested in Kuki-Chin seems clearly to have arisen from a suffixed form like <latinform>*kəw-a</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>九</hanform> OC <latinform>*ki̯ŭg</latinform>, GSR #992a-d 'nine'; Coblin 86:113 ST <latinform>*dkwjəɣw</latinform> > PC <latinform>*kwjəgwx</latinform> > OC <latinform>*kjəgwx</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:320 <latinform>*kuʔ</latinform>  or <latinform>*kwəʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kuʔ {[k]uʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>jiǔ</latinform>.</par>

疾 OC *dźi̯ət, GSR #494a-c; B & S 2011: *dzit {[dz]i[t]}; Mand. jí.
堲 OC *tsiə̯t, GSR #923c 'coaled part of a burning torch'; B & S 2011: (*tsik); Coblin 86:50 ST *tsjik > PC *tsjik > OC *tsjit > *tsjet; Mand. jí.

<par>Tibetan and rGyalrong have an allofam with final <reconstruction>*-n</reconstruction>.</par><par>The general meaning of this root seems to be 'light in color', with the exact color referred to differing from language to language.</par>
<par>This root was reconstructed as <latinform>*ŋow</latinform> in <plainlatinform>STC #296</plainlatinform>.</par>
<par>This root seems to be confined to Burmish. The Jingpho and Sak forms may well be borrowings from Burmese. (This etymology is due to Liberty Lidz.)</par>

<par>The Tamangic forms have medial <latinform>-y-</latinform>, which appears to be secondary. It is possible that this <latinform>-y-</latinform> derives from earlier prefixal <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> (see Benedict 1943, <emph>Secondary Infixation in Lepcha</emph>). Note the similar alternation between <latinform>l-</latinform> and <latinform>y-</latinform> in <xref ref="695">#695</xref> and <xref ref="699">#699</xref>.</par><par>There is apparently no connection <xref ref="2588">#2588</xref>.</par>

<par>The velar prefix is attested in Jingpho.</par><par>The present root seems to overlap somewhat with <xref ref="5595">#5595</xref> and <xref ref="1621">#1621</xref>, and further microlinguistic work will be necessary to separate them out.</par>

築 OC *ti̯ôk, GSR #1019d 'pound earth, build'; B & S 2011: *truk; Mand. zhù.
宗 OC *tsông, GSR #1003a-3 'ancestral temple; ancestor'; Schuessler 2007:634 *tsuŋ; B & S 2011: *tsˁuŋ {[ts]ˁuŋ}; Mand. zōng.
造 S601-2.
造 S601

<par>The Phunoi, Mpi, and Bisu forms are probably loans from Tai (cf. Siamese <latinform>saaj</latinform> < Proto-Tai <latinform>*zaay</latinform>). The Siamese word is written with an initial indicating Proto-Tai <latinform>*dr-</latinform>, but this is held to be spurious by Li Fang Kuei (1977:161-162).</par><par>If the WT form meaning 'earth' is indeed cognate to Chinese <latinform>shā</latinform> 'sand' (OC <latinform>sa</latinform>) [GSR #16a-c] / <latinform>*srāj</latinform> [WHB]), as suggested in STC p. 188, it is possible that this is an old loan from Sino-Tibetan into Tai.</par><par>The palatal suffix in this root, perhaps with a diminutive meaning, is discussed in Matisoff (1995).</par><par>A medial <latinform>-l-</latinform> turns up in the WB variant form <latinform>sa-lâi</latinform>, in Achang, and in Southern Plains Chin.</par>
<par><hanform>沙</hanform> OC <latinform>*sɑ</latinform>, GSR #16a-c 'sand'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*{[s]ˁraj}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shā</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>麝</hanform> OC (<latinform>*d̑’i̯ɑ̆g</latinform>), (not in GSR #807) 'musk deer'; Schuessler 2007:457 <latinform>*m-lah</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*Cə.lak-s</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>shè</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Cf. Karlgren 1923:865, where the reconstruction <latinform>*dź’i̯ɑg</latinform> is given. [JAM]</par>

<par>See Gong 2001:32.</par><par>For the connection between GAME ANIMAL (in general) and DEER, see the <latinform>*syan</latinform> allofam of <xref ref="34">#34</xref>. A similar well-known example in Germanic is the cognate pair German <latinform>Tier</latinform> 'animal' / English <latinform>deer</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root has been identified in the NE Indian Areal Group and Himalayish. The reconstructed rhyme <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction> is directly reflected by the Mru, Tiddim, Mizo, and Chepang reflexes, and indirectly by Written Tibetan and Karbi <latinform>-e</latinform>, which are also the regular reflexes of <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction>. See Matisoff 1985a #140 and HPTB pp. 206-219 for many corroborative cognate sets. The Lakher (Mara) word for 'add; increase' is tentatively included in this set, since the same association between WOMB/PLACENTA and ADD/INCREASE is found in <xref ref="679">#679</xref>.</par>
<par>The reflexes grouped here bear some resemblance to those of <xref ref="3602">#3602</xref>, but we are treating them for now as having descended from a separate etymon.</par>
<par>This tentatively reconstructed root looks fairly solid, although the Angami and Liangmei forms have unexplained dental stops instead of fricates.</par>

<par><plainlatinform>STC p. 174</plainlatinform> derives <plainlatinform>GSR</plainlatinform>'s OC <latinform>*xi̯wăŋ</latinform> from an earlier <latinform>**plwăŋ</latinform> on the basis of a possible connection with <hanform>伯</hanform> OC <latinform>*păk</latinform> (<plainlatinform>GSR 782i</plainlatinform>) 'eldest brother', as well as a putative parallel example of PST <reconstruction>*labial-plus-w</reconstruction> clusters becoming <latinform>χw-</latinform> in OC, i.e., <xref ref="2152">#2152</xref> <hanform>燔</hanform> <latinform>*b‘i̯wăn</latinform> BURN / ROAST (<plainlatinform>GSR 195i</plainlatinform>) ⪤ <hanform>火</hanform> <latinform>*χwâr</latinform> (<plainlatinform>GSR 353a-c</plainlatinform>).</par><par>Also see <xref ref="2440">#2440</xref>.</par>
<par>This root somewhat resembles <xref ref="1213">#1213</xref>, but seems independent from it.</par>
<par><hanform>擔</hanform> OC <latinform>*tɑ̂m</latinform>, GSR 619k 'carry on the shoulder'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*mə-tˁam</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>tān</latinform>.</par>
<par>The stronghold of this root seems to be the Tamangic family, with possible cognates in Kiranti and Qiangic.</par>

夾 OC *kɑ̆p, GSR #630a 'support (v.); press between; tweezers'; Schuessler 2007:300 (*krêp); B & S 2011: *C.kˁ‹r›ep; Mand. jiā.
狹 OC *g'ɑ̆p, GSR #630e 'narrow'; Schuessler 2007:300 *grêp; B & S 2011: *N-kˁ‹r›ep; Mand. xiá.
梜 OC *kɑp ~ *ki̯ɑp, GSR #630f 'chopsticks'; Schuessler 2007:300 *krêp; B & S 2011: *kˁ‹r›ep; Mand. jiā ~ jié.
頰 OC *kiɑp, GSR #630h 'cheek, jowl'; Schuessler 2007:537 *kêp; Mand. jiá.
挾 OC *g’iɑp, GSR #630l 'grasp, hold, clasp under arm'; Schuessler 2007:300 *krêp; B & S 2011: *m-kˁep; Mand. xié.
協 OC *g’iɑp, GSR #639b 'in harmony, together; conform'; Schuessler 2007:537 *gêp; Mand. xié.
脅 OC *χi̯ɑ̆p, GSR #639e 'flank, side of body, ribs'; Schuessler 2007:300 (*khrêp); B & S 2011: *qʰ‹r›ep; Mand. xié.Comment: Regarding the above Chinese etyma, 夾 Mand. jiā and 梜 Mand. jié, jiā could in particular be treated as comparanda for #1279, q.v.

<par>This etymon was formerly divided between two separate etyma, one meaning GRASS and the other meaning WEEDS / DETRITUS. The latter was reconstructed both in STC #363 and in HPTB:360 as <reconstruction>*muːk</reconstruction>. However, despite the WB doublet <latinform>mrak</latinform> 'grass' and <latinform>ăhmuik</latinform> 'refuse, dust', we have now decided to combine them into a single etymon.</par>
<par>This etymon has so far only been found in Kiranti, Tangut, rGyalrongic, and Bai. It is nowhere near as widespread as the major PTB root for DREAM, <xref ref="126">#126 </xref>.</par>
<par>This putative etymon has so far only been identified in two languages.</par>

<par>Lolo-Burmese distinguishes between the open syllable allofam <latinform>*la</latinform> and the suffixed form <latinform>*lay</latinform>, with a semantic difference: signaling yes/no questions vs. substance questions, respectively. See GSTC set #131, Matisoff 1995 ("Palatal suffixes") #21, and HPTB p. 488.</par>

<par>Chinese <hanform>與</hanform> or <hanform>歟</hanform> 'final (yes/no or rhetorical) interrogative particle' is reconstructed as OC <latinform>*zi̯o</latinform> in GSR #89b-d and #89e (Mand. <latinform>yú</latinform>), but as <latinform>*lă</latinform> in WHB's system.</par>

<par>Of the two prefixes set up for this root, <latinform>*m-</latinform> is much better attested. The <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix appears in Dimasa, as well as in Written Tibetan <latinform>sgo</latinform> 'door'. The current etymon comprises three roots set up separately in STC (468, 469, 470), which Benedict judges as "apparently all related" (p. 120).</par><par>There is a likely Chinese comparandum <hanform>戶</hanform>, OC <latinform>*g’o</latinform> [GSR #53a-b] 'door, opening'.</par><par>Kuki-Chin (and perhaps other) languages show variation between a plain and aspirated initial.</par><par>There is some slight evidence for an allofam with medial <latinform>-y-</latinform>, <latinform>*m-k(y)a</latinform>, as witness rGyalrongic forms like <latinform>tə mɟja</latinform>, <latinform>tA mgya</latinform>, and <latinform>tə-mdʒa</latinform> 'chin'.</par>

戶 OC *g’o, GSR #53a-b ‘door, opening, house, family’; Schuessler 2007:282 *gâʔ; B & S 2011: *m-qˁaʔ ; Mand. hù.
開 OC *k’ər, GSR #541a 'open'; B & S 2011: *kʰˁəj {[k]ʰˁəj}; Mand. kāi.
<par>related to <xref ref="1779">#1779</xref>?</par>
<par>This root has so far mostly been found in Himalayan languages, and further confirmation from other branches of the family would be welcome.</par>
<par>This root bears a certain resemblance to <xref ref="5776">#5776</xref>, although the velar in the latter root is part of an initial consonant cluster and not a prefix.</par>

<par><hanform>丘</hanform></par><par><hanform>街</hanform> OC <latinform>*kĕg</latinform>, GSR #879o 'road crossing, street'; Coblin 1986:156 ST <latinform>*rɣwjɨaɣ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*gwiag</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kˁre {[k]ˁre}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>jiē</latinform>.</par><par>The comparison with Chinese <hanform>街</hanform> in the sense 'village/street' is due to <plainlatinform>Coblin 1986:156</plainlatinform>.</par>

<par>Several languages with <latinform>-a-</latinform> vocalism (Chepang, Lepcha, Bantawa) might point to an allofam <latinform>*zyak</latinform>. This variational pattern is well-attested in TB, the best example being <xref ref="33">#33</xref>.</par>

<par>French says, "Cf. Burmese-Lolo <latinform>(*k-)la</latinform> (> WB <latinform>kyà</latinform>), which Benedict identifies as "ultimately a loan from Austro-Asiatic <latinform>*k(u)la</latinform>" (STC 177-178, n.472); the Khasi is <latinform>u-kla</latinform> (Robinson). <par>This etymon has an impressive number of variants, motivating an extreme pan-allofamic formula like <xref ref="6713">#6713</xref>. The sub-roots fall into several categories: some of the reflexes derive from prototypes with final *liquids; others point to earlier final *nasals; while still others derive from *open syllables. For convenience's sake, we will present the allofams in that order.</par><par>The semantic developments of this root are also quite varied, ranging from FIRE / BURN / KINDLE / ROAST to SHINE / LIGHT / BRIGHT to HEAT UP / SWEAT to WHITE / YELLOW. Less this be thought implausible, cf. Proto-Indo-European *bhel- "shine; flash; burn; shining white and various bright colors" which give us English reflexes including 'black', 'blank', 'blanch', 'bleak', 'bald', 'bleach', 'blue', 'blaze', 'blind', 'blend', 'blond', 'blink', etc. See HPTB 428-430.</par>

<par>This etymon was originally set up as <reconstruction>*tsyuːŋ</reconstruction> in STC #390, on the basis of Lushai (Mizo), Bodo, and Dimasa forms. Benedict later revised this to <reconstruction>*tuːŋ</reconstruction> (STC n. 63, p.17), and specifically banished WT <latinform>gźuŋ</latinform> as probably not cognate. This reconstruction was also adopted in HPTB pp. 287, 310. It seems clear that the most ancient version of this root had a simple or palatalized dental stop, as witnessed by the certain Chinese cognate <hanform>中</hanform> GSR #1007a-e <latinform>ti̯ông/t̑i̯ung</latinform> 'middle'  (see below). From an early date, however, many languages developed a sibilant or affricate initial before this medial vowel <latinform>-u-</latinform>, in a manner reminiscent of a phenomenon in modern Japanese, where the phonemic syllable /tu/ has come to be pronounced [tsɯ]. The same variation between stop and (af)fricate initials before <latinform>-u-</latinform> is to be found in 'mortar' PTB <reconstruction>*(t)sum</reconstruction>, with most reflexes pointing unambiguously to PTB <reconstruction>*tsum</reconstruction> (e.g. WB <latinform>chum</latinform>, Lahu <latinform>chɛ</latinform>, Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>sum</latinform>), while Jingpho <latinform>thùm</latinform> reflects <reconstruction>*tum</reconstruction>. See also <xref ref="526">#526</xref>.</par><par>For convenience I am here assigning the reflexes of this etymon into two allofamic roots, according to whether they have retained a stop initial (<xref ref="3447">#3447 </xref>) or have undergone initial frication (<xref ref="1628">#1628 </xref>).</par><par>The semantic range of this word-family includes the notion of CENTER (as does the phonologically unrelated root <xref ref="517">#517</xref> [q.v.]).</par><par>The <reconstruction>*-u- ⪤ *-wa-</reconstruction> variation posited for this root is paralleled in several other etyma, e.g. <xref ref="2009">#2009</xref>, <xref ref="359">#359</xref>, and <xref ref="2379">#2379</xref>. (See HPTB:513-514.) This variation parallels the <reconstruction>*-i- ⪤ *-ya-</reconstruction> alternation found in a number of roots, notably <xref ref="33">#33</xref>.</par>
<par>This root has so far only been unearthed in the relatively obscure Asakian branch of Tibeto-Burman.</par>
<par>A Proto-Karenic form has yet to be reconstructed.</par>

<par>This etymon bears some resemblance to <xref ref="3564">#3564</xref>, but should be kept distinct.</par><par>A couple of forms have apparently acquired a labial prefix: cf. Garo <latinform>ak-brit-a</latinform> and Gurung <latinform>wre◦baq</latinform> 'scratch'.</par>
<par>The distribution of this form makes it look like a probable loan. This etymology is due to Liberty Lidz.</par>

<par><hanform>鷹</hanform> OC <latinform>*ʔi̯əng</latinform>, GSR #890a-c 'eagle, falcon'; Coblin 86:76 <latinform>*lang</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:574 <latinform>*ʔəŋ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*qəŋ {[q](r)əŋ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yīng</latinform>.</par><par>There is an apparent Chinese doublet with stopped final: OC <latinform>*di̯ək</latinform> ~ <latinform>*i̯ək</latinform> 'hawk, kite' (see <plainlatinform>STC</plainlatinform> n. 225, p. 72). Cf. also <xref ref="7343">#7343</xref>.</par>

<par>STC makes an artificial distinction between two groups of forms, one reconstructed <reconstruction>*kor</reconstruction> 'valley; pit; cave' (#349) and the other <reconstruction>*kwar</reconstruction> 'hole' (#350), here combined into a single etymon. This etymon is widely distributed in the NE Indian Areal Group, with scattered cognates elsewhere (Himalayish, Qiangic, Nungish, Bai). It appears as the last syllable in compounds referring to orifices of the body, especially EAR, NOSTRIL, ANUS, and VAGINA; occasionally also in compounds for EYE.</par><par>Sometimes, however, a similar morpheme appears as a monosyllable or as the first syllable in a compound; these cases I refer to a separate (but perhaps allofamically related) etymon <xref ref="3558">#3558</xref>, with the specific meaning EAR. A key form here is Kom Rem <latinform>kōr khur</latinform> 'ear canal', with the structure <xref ref="3558">#3558</xref> + <reconstruction>*kor</reconstruction>.</par><par>While I occasionally reconstruct <latinform>*kwar</latinform> for the second syllables of compounds meaning EYE (e.g. Wancho <latinform>mək-<emph>ər</emph></latinform>), there is a group of Bodo-Garo forms meaning EYE where I set up a separate (but again perhaps allofamic) etymon <xref ref="3557">#3557</xref>, with the specific meaning EYE. Compare Bodo <latinform>ha-khor</latinform> 'hole, valley' vs. <latinform>me-gon</latinform> 'eye'; Garo <latinform>ging-kol</latinform> 'nostril' vs. <latinform>mik-on</latinform> 'eye'. There is also some evidence from Monpa Tsangla for the independence of this etymon and <xref ref="3557">#3557</xref>: <latinform>miŋ-khor</latinform> 'eye' vs. <latinform>miŋ-khoŋ-(taŋ)</latinform> 'eye (< <xref ref="682">#682</xref> + <xref ref="3557">#3557</xref>).</par><par>This etymon may well be related to <xref ref="5748">#5748</xref>, the common core of meaning perhaps residing in the round appearance of many holes (cf. WT <latinform>kor</latinform> 'round, circular hollow in the ground, pit').</par><par>It is often difficult to distinguish reflexes of this etymon from those of <xref ref="820">#820</xref>, especially with respect to Loloish and Qiangic. More than one allofam are evidently involved; cf. e.g. Lahu <latinform>qhɔ</latinform> 'inside; hole' ⪤ <latinform>qō</latinform> 'hollow object'.</par>

<par>On the basis of the Akha doublet <latinform>po HS</latinform> 'roll over, search for' and <latinform>pyaw ̂</latinform> 'turn over, be twisted', two separate allofamic roots were reconstructed in <plainlatinform>LTSR ##19-20</plainlatinform> and in <plainlatinform>HPTB pp. 337, 369</plainlatinform>. However, it now seems better to simply combine them. The Akha doublet is handled by positing a variant with medial <latinform>-y-</latinform>. Further accurately transcribed Loloish forms will clarify this etymology.</par>

<par><hanform>覆</hanform> OC <latinform>*pʼi̯ôk</latinform>, GSR #1034m 'turn over, overthrow'; Schuessler 2007:246 <latinform>*phuk</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pʰuk {pʰ(r)uk}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>fù</latinform>.</par>

<par>This extremely widespread etymon seems distinct from <xref ref="3337">#3337</xref>. A number of languages have developed a dental or palatal nasal through the influence of a following yod or front vowel. A similar development has occurred in roots like <xref ref="33">#33</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>吹</hanform> OC <latinform>*t̂’wiɑ</latinform>, GSR #30a; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tʰoj</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>chuī</latinform> 'blow (wind); play (wind instrument)'.</par>

<par>The takin (/<latinform>ˈtɑːkɪn</latinform>/; <emph>Budorcas taxicolor</emph>; Tibetan: ར་རྒྱ་, Wylie: ra rgya), also called cattle chamois or gnu goat, is a goat-antelope found in the eastern Himalayas. Mitochondrial research shows the takin is related to sheep; its similarity to the muskox is an example of convergent evolution. The takin is the national animal of Bhutan. <footnote> cited from Wikipedia: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Takin</footnote></par>
<par>This root has only been found in a couple of languages of the Deng group, and is in dire need of further confirmation.</par>
<par>This etymon, although nowhere near as widespread as <xref ref="1288">#1288</xref>, seems quite valid, despite its rather scattered distribution. The velar prefix is found in Tshona.</par>

<par>A few languages (Qiang, Bai) have a labial semi-vowel, but this might be secondary, in view of a similar tendency to labialize velar onsets in languages like Lisu: cf. e.g. BITTER <xref ref="229">#229 </xref>, Lisu <latinform>khwa²¹</latinform>.</par><par>The first syllable of Chang <latinform>ku khu</latinform> might also reflect this labialization, a similar development is characteristic of Chinese (cf. Mandarin <latinform>ku³</latinform> 'bitter' from <xref ref="229">#229</xref>). See STC p. 186.</par><par>For a phonosemantically similar root, see <xref ref="3399">#3399</xref>.</par>

<par>Prefixal <latinform>*s-</latinform> is set up because of the WB creaky tone, while the velar prefix is reconstructed on the basis of the Pumi forms.</par><par>This root seems quite distinct from <xref ref="115">#115</xref>.</par>

<par>Written Tibetan has both an <latinform>r-</latinform> and an <latinform>s-</latinform> prefix, with the former apparently metathesized to a glide in other languages (Apatani, Tshona, Tamang).</par><par>The Tangut form may reflect the nasal allofam, although its cognacy is by no means certain.</par><par>The first member of the Apatani doublet <latinform>har-č(ʰ)eʔ</latinform> 'groin' and <latinform>kʰrjɯ-po</latinform> 'waist' has a palatal affricate initial, similar to the Lepcha and Hpun forms, leading one to wonder whether two separate roots are involved.</par>
<par>There is a possible relationship between this root and <xref ref="540">#540</xref>, the latter also now being reconstructed with a medial glide. Cf. also <xref ref="2260">#2260</xref>.</par>
<par>The final <latinform>*-t</latinform> in this meso-root is unexplained, but we are including it here for now. </par>
<par><hanform>咳</hanform> OC <latinform>*k’əg</latinform>, GSR #937g 'cough'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kʰˁək-s</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>ké</latinform>.</par>

姪 OC *d’iet, GSR #413o-p
姪 OC *dʼi̯ĕt, GSR #413o-p; Mand. zhí.Note: nominalizing final *-t in Chinese.

It appears as second element in compounds meaning WOMB / PLACENTA, where the first element means CHILD.</par><par>This root shows vocalic variation between <reconstruction>*-u-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*-i-</reconstruction>. Some languages have reflexes with initial velar stop, but others (e.g. Lushai [Mizo], WB) point to a variant beginning with <latinform>y-</latinform> or <latinform>i-</latinform>; that is, some languages have treated the velar as a prefix.</par>

坅 OC *k’i̯əm, GSR #651i 'pit'; Coblin 86:119 ST *khyim > OC *khjəmx; Schuessler 2007:430 *khəmʔ; B & S 2011: *(kʰəmʔ; Mand. qǐn.

<par><hanform>彡</hanform>/<hanform>髟</hanform> OC <latinform>*sɑm</latinform>, GSR #1154a 'long hair'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*sˁram {s[ˁ]ram}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shān</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>炭</hanform> OC <latinform>*tʼɑ̂n</latinform>, GSR #151a 'coal, charcoal; lime'; Coblin 86:68 ST <latinform>*thal</latinform> > OC <latinform>*thanh</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:491 <latinform>*thâns</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tʰˁan-s {[tʰ]ˁa[n]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>tàn</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>無</hanform> OC <latinform>*mi̯wo</latinform>, GSR #103a 'no, not have'; Coblin 86:113 ST <latinform>*mjaɣ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*mjag</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:518-19 <latinform>*ma</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ma</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>wú</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon, which frequently occurs reduplicated, may have a hypocoristic (baby-talk) flavor. It is sometimes difficult to distinguish reflexes of this etymon from those of <xref ref="253">#253</xref>, which also often appear reduplicated. Particularly problematic are the Qiangic forms, many of which have front vowels, but which after much vacillation I have finally assigned to <xref ref="253">#253</xref> instead of to the present set. Both <xref ref="3585">#3585</xref> and <xref ref="254">#254</xref> occasionally take a nasal suffix, which further complicates the picture (cf. the *Tamang, Chepang, Hayu, and Pa-O forms, below).</par><par>A couple of Himalayish languages (Gurung, Thakali) have a velar initial, which appears to be a secondary development from the palatal nasal <reconstruction>*ny-</reconstruction>. A pair of other forms with voiceless nasals (Lushai [Mizo], E. Chepang) reflect the <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix.</par>
<par><hanform>嬭</hanform> OC <latinform>*niər</latinform>, GSR #359d-f 'mother; breasts, milk'; Schuessler 2007:395 <latinform>*nêʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>nǎi</latinform>.</par>

See the series of entries beginning with <emph>dugdhá-</emph> in Turner 1966 (<emph>A Comparative Dictionary of the Indo-Aryan Languages</emph>), pp. 365-6.</par>

STC reconstructs <hanform>薯</hanform> as PTB <latinform>*źra</latinform>, which he regards as a doublet of his PTB <latinform>*śra</latinform>, reconstructed in STC #434 (but mistakenly listed as <latinform>*s-ra</latinform> in the Index p. 207).</par><par>We are regarding both Chinese forms cited as allofamically related.</par>

<par><hanform>饙</hanform>/<hanform>餴</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯wən</latinform>, GSR #437i-l / #438e 'steam rice'; Schuessler 2007:235 <latinform>*pən</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pur {pu[r]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>fēn</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon is usually found as the last element of compounds, but appears as an independent monosyllable in Kham. The nasal prefix is attested in Simi, while the <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix is reflected in the Nungic words for 'finger'.</par>
<par>The reconstruction of a nasal prefix in this poorly attested root is on the basis of the Muya form.</par>

<par>We set up this root with an initial affricate to accommodate the Tibetan and Northern Naga forms. At the PLB level, the initial had simplified to a sibilant, a development which is replicated in scattered forms elsewhere, e.g. Sangtam <latinform>a ze</latinform> and Kayan Pekon <latinform>sên</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root seems definitely to be allofamic with <xref ref="380">#380</xref>.</par>

<par>This root has scattered but good-looking reflexes in the NE Indian Areal Group (Maram, Meithei), Himalayish (Kanauri, Manchati), Loloish (Ahi, Nesu), Nungish (Dulong), Qiangic (Muya), and Tujia. Most reflexes have a simple sibilant fricative initial, but the Loloish and Tujia forms have affricates. There does not seem to be any connection with <xref ref="641">#641</xref> or <xref ref="1601">#1601</xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon is of very restricted distribution, occurring only in the Jingpho-Asakian group. It appears unrelated to the equally restricted etymon <xref ref="3492">#3492 </xref>. A Lepcha form <latinform>tălam-pót</latinform> 'testicles' is not to be brought in here, since the last element (<latinform>pót</latinform>, <latinform>a-pót</latinform>) means 'fruit; ball', and recurs in other bodypart compounds like <latinform>nyen-pót</latinform> 'woman's breast; cow's udder' and <latinform>anyor-pót</latinform> 'dewlap'.</par>
<par>This etymon was reconstructed as <reconstruction>*pot</reconstruction> TBRS: #120.</par>
<par>This word seems to refer especially to heavy breathing or panting. The <latinform>*r-</latinform> prefix is attested in Tibetan.</par>
<par>This root, confined apparently to Himalayish, requires further confirmation.</par>

<par><hanform>閉</hanform> OC <latinform>*pied</latinform> ~ <latinform>*piet</latinform> 'close, obstruct', GSR #412a; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pˁit-s {*pˁi[t]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bì</latinform>.</par>
<par>There is a similar Tai root: cf. Siamese <latinform>pìt</latinform>.</par>

<par>Several phono-semantically similar variants are to be grouped together in this etymon. Their center of gravity is ROUND OBJECT, as shown in various compounds for rounded body parts and inanimate objects. The variants with final <latinform>-m</latinform> and <latinform>-ŋ</latinform> are equally well-attested, even within a single subgroup (cf. Bruhn's [2014] reconstructions for Proto-Central-Naga: PCN <latinform>*m-luŋ</latinform> HEART / BE ROUND and PCN <latinform>*ləm</latinform> HEAD / HEART / MIND.</par><par>Bahing <latinform>thym</latinform> and Hayu <latinform>thum</latinform> 'heart' look as if they might somehow be related, although the initial dental stop is a problem.</par>
<par>There is a possible allofamic relationship between this root and <xref ref="7401">#7401</xref>.</par>

<par>This sparsely attested root, which in TB languages refers to the neck, seems to be distinct from <xref ref="66">#66</xref>, whose semantic center of gravity is more abstract. </par><par>The putative Chinese comparandum, however, has a more "internal" meaning, referring to the voice or throat. Cf. Mandarin <latinform>sǎngzi</latinform> ‘voice’, which occurs neither in GSR 704 nor in Baxter's system, so that it probably has no early attestation. By analogy with <latinform>sāng</latinform> ‘mulberry’, which is in both sources, it would be reconstructed <reconstruction>*sɑ̂ng</reconstruction> by Karlgren and <reconstruction>*sangʔ</reconstruction> by Baxter.</par>
<par>The cognacy of these Tujia syllables is uncertain.</par>

<par><hanform>妹</hanform> OC <latinform>*mwəd</latinform>, GSR #531k-m 'younger sister'; Schuessler 2007:378 <latinform>*mə̂s</latinform> ~ <latinform>*mîs</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*C.mˁət-s {C.mˁə[t]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>mèi</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>旁</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’wɑ̂ng</latinform>, GSR #740f' 'side'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*bˁaŋ {[b]ˁaŋ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>páng</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>俄</hanform> OC <latinform>*ngɑ̂</latinform>, GSR #2h 'slanting'; Schuessler 2007:222 <latinform>*ŋâi</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*ŋˁaj</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>é</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root has the general meaning of FOREPART. In combination with etyma for TOOTH it means FRONT TOOTH/INCISOR. In combination with SKIN it can mean FORESKIN (cf. WT <latinform>mdun-lpags</latinform>). The Lepcha form requires special comment (see note).</par>

<par><hanform>短</hanform> OC <latinform>*twɑ̂n</latinform>, GSR #169a-c 'short'; Schuessler 2007:219 <latinform>*tônʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tˁorʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>duǎn</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is independent from <xref ref="271">#271</xref>, despite a certain phonological resemblance between the two. This etymon, far and away the most widely attested root in this semantic area in TB as a whole, is characterized by considerable morphophonemic complexity. The basic allofam is #695 (1a), but in many daughter languages the lateral has become a yod, yielding forms like #696 (1b). By a further sound change, this yod has developed into z- or ʑ-, but reflexes with these initials are also assigned to (1b). In other languages we find forms with d- rather than l-. Two explanations are possible here: (a) These languages could have added a prefix d- to the yodated variant, yielding #699 (1c), which sometimes developed into reflexes with voiced affricate initials like dʑ-; in other cases, the prefix "preempted" the initial entirely, giving forms like #700 (1d). (b) Alternatively, we might assume that these latter forms arose directly from #695 via a sporadic "deltacism", perhaps via an intermediate **lyak. A handful of reflexes contain both an l and a d, probably best explained of reprefixation after the original l had become d.For an extended discussion of d/l interchange in a variety of languages, see Matisoff 2013 ("The dinguist's dilemma: regular and sporadic d/l interchange in Sino-Tibetan and elsewhere"). Other reflexes point to a prefix g- added to the yodated root, #702 (1e), with secondary meanings like 'cubit' or 'armpit'; some reflexes with affricated initials are assigned to this variant rather than to #699 (see especially the Written Burmese triplet for 'armpit'). Finally, an honorific Written Tibetan form for 'hand' reflects a labial prefix, #705 (1f).Note that all these prefixes (d-, g-, p-) are deemed to have been added to the variant with y- rather than l-, as reflected in the pan-allofamic formula given above, where "C-" stands for a consonantal prefix:*d
*g -yak
*pBenedict originally reconstructed this root simply as *lak, but in the revised version of STC he included prefixal g- in the protoform (STC #86). This reconstruction was followed by Weidert (1987:#689), and by French (1983:500) for Proto-Northern Naga. This elevation of g- to special proto-status seems unwarranted, however, since the d- prefix is actually much more widespread. There are in fact no unambiguous reflexes of a prototype *g-lak. It is extremely interesting to note that a number of languages have forms for FOOT that reflect the open syllables *la or *ya, both of which could also take a prefix (either d- or g- that sometimes led to affrication of the initial). This is noted with puzzlement in STC (p. 34 and n. 308), but it seems irresistible to posit some sort of connection with this etymon for HAND.

<par>This is only one of a large number of verbs than can be used to mean EJACULATE.<footnote>Another such verb is <reconstruction>*m-tis</reconstruction> 'be wet'. See below, <xref ref="298">#298</xref>.</footnote> This particular root has so far been identified only in a couple of Loloish languages. In Ahi it means specifically 'eject semen', while the Lahu cognate is used to mean 'squirt urine'. Although this etymon had a *stopped final in Proto-Loloish (as indicated by the Ahi constriction and the Lahu high-rising tone), it was not reconstructed in Matisoff 1972a.</par>

In general it is more ‘hearty’, while <xref ref="297">#297 </xref> is more ‘chesty’.</par>

<par>This etymon must rank among the most richly prefixable roots in TB, with at least five prefixes and prefix combinations attested. The gloss of <latinform>go'l</latinform> or <latinform>gol◦dik</latinform> as 'stick' is confirmed in K. W. Momin's <emph>English-Achikku Dictionary</emph>  (n.d.:227). <par>This etymon is particularly well attested in Himalayish. Most of the NE Indian Areal Group forms (especially those with <latinform>-ma</latinform> as final syllable) look like loans from Tibetan. The root also occurs in Baic. There are several possible explanations for the final <latinform>-m</latinform> in certain Himalayish forms (Bantawa, Chamling, Hayu), as well as the nasalization in Bai <latinform>ɯ̃</latinform> 'nurse; suckle': (a) they could be due to rhinoglottophilia<footnote>See Matisoff 1975.</footnote> because of the zero or glottal-stop initial; (b) they could have arisen by assimilatory epenthesis to the initial <latinform>p-</latinform> of the following syllable; or (c) they could have been metanalyzed from the initial of the second syllable of the binome <reconstruction>*o-ma</reconstruction>, which might originally have been borrowed from Tibetan as a unit before being reduced to a single syllable in the new Bantawa/Chamling compounds. The development of <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction> > WT <latinform>o</latinform> is regular, e.g. <reconstruction>*swa</reconstruction> TOOTH > WB <latinform>swâ</latinform>/WT <latinform>so</latinform>; <reconstruction>*g-lwat ⪤ *s-lwat</reconstruction> LOOSEN / FREE > WB <latinform>lwat/hlwat ⪤ kywat/khywat</latinform>/WT <latinform>glod-pa ⪤ hlod-pa</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root appears principally in Himalayish and contiguous languages of the NE Indian Areal Group, but also in Bai, so that it must be set up for PTB.</par>

<par>We reconstruct this interesting root with medial <latinform>*-l-</latinform> on the basis of the PKC form, even though this medial does not appear overtly elsewhere in TB. Written Tibetan lacks an <latinform>skl</latinform> cluster, so that medial <latinform>-y-</latinform> was substituted for it. Written Burmese has a doublet, <latinform>kyôŋ</latinform> 'feed, tend cattle' and <latinform>ə-coŋʼ</latinform> 'watchman, guard', with the latter apparently a later development. This etymon is thus a good example of a proto-contrast better preserved in a particular subgroup than in the family as a whole.</par><par>This root was formerly reconstructed as <latinform>*s-gyoŋ</latinform> (HPTB:294).</par>

<par>This etymon displays the most pervasive variational pattern in TB vowels, between <reconstruction>*-u-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*-i-</reconstruction> (see Wolfenden 1929:114-5, STC pp. 80-4, VSTB pp. 41-2, HPTB pp. 493-505).<footnote>For other etyma with similar variation, see e.g. <xref ref="3587">#3587</xref>, <xref ref="1612">#1612</xref>, <xref ref="127">#127</xref>.</footnote> It is well distributed, occurring widely in the NE Indian Areal Group (but not in Chin), with convincing cognates in Himalayish, Jingpho, and Qiangic. The Burmish cognates point to different final consonants: WB has <latinform>-k</latinform>, while Achang and Zaiwa have <latinform>-t</latinform>. W.T. French (1983:526) suggests an etymology (which appears fanciful), deriving this root from a compound <reconstruction>*sa-yip</reconstruction> ANIMAL + SLEEP (< <xref ref="34">#34</xref> and <xref ref="127">#127</xref>).</par>

<par>This root bears a strong resemblance to <xref ref="5468">#5468</xref>. For now, we are assigning reflexes with open syllables or final <latinform>-t</latinform> to <xref ref="5468">#5468</xref>, and those with final velars to the present etymon.</par>

<par>This root is set up for "Proto-Tani" (part of the traditional "Mirish" or "Abor-Miri-Dafla" group) by Jackson T. Sun (1993). The possible Kanauri cognate suggests that it might actually be more widely distributed in TB.</par>

<par>This etymon is firmly established in Lolo-Burmese (Burmese itself has reflexes of both the stopped and non-stopped allofams) and Jingpho. The first syllable of Kham <latinform>zuh-riː</latinform> 'testicle' is probably to be assigned to <xref ref="3436">#3436</xref> rather than to the present root, in view of the 2nd syllable of the compound <latinform>'ba-zu-riː</latinform> '(non-human) egg', where the first syllable means 'bird'. Palaychi (Karenic) <latinform>shóq</latinform> 'penis' seems unrelated.</par><par>The semantic center of the root seems to be VIRILITY. It occurs especially in compounds for intact (as opposed to castrated) male animals, with extensions into SEMEN, TESTICLES, and PENIS. This root was reconstructed for PLB in Matisoff 1988a:1225.</par>

<par>As noted in DL:1187, the correspondence between Lahu <latinform>-i</latinform> and WB <latinform>-we</latinform> is irregular with respect to PLB <reconstruction>*swəy²</reconstruction> BLOOD (see <xref ref="230">#230 </xref>), where it is Lahu <latinform>-ɨ</latinform> which corresponds to WB <latinform>-we</latinform>.</par>

<par>The reflexes show a certain amount of vocalic variation, only to be expected in such a sound-symbolic root.</par><par>There is a similar root <xref ref="1715">#1715</xref>, which should be kept distinct, since some languages (Nocte, Tangsa, Tamang) have compounds containing both etyma.</par><par>Several forms reflect a diphthongal rhyme <latinform>*-ay</latinform>, either directly (Tangsa, Lalung, Thakali, Lahu) or indirectly via a fronted vowel (Apatani, Chokri, Karbi).</par>

<par>The initial <latinform>ŋ-</latinform> in the Chin forms is probably due to assimilation to the following <latinform>-w-</latinform>, which also had a velar component in its articulation.</par><par>The Chinese comparandum also has a velar initial.</par>

However, the alternation of <latinform>-ya-</latinform> and <latinform>-i-</latinform> is paralleled in a number of other roots, notably <xref ref="33">#33</xref>.</par>

<par>The initial <latinform>*pr-</latinform> cluster in Khaling seems to be a fusion of <xref ref="5737">#5737</xref> with <latinform>wap</latinform>. The Padam-Mising form also looks like a fusion of these two morphemes, with its syllable initial <latinform>r-</latinform> from <latinform>*pwar</latinform>, and the proto-sequence <latinform>*wap</latinform> becoming the rhyme <latinform>-op</latinform>.</par><par>The final dental in the Chinese comparandum might be due to labial dissimilation from the initial, as in "wind (n.)" <hanform>風</hanform>, which is reconstructed as <latinform>*pi̯ŭm</latinform> for Old Chinese, but which became <latinform>pi̯ung</latinform> by the Middle Chinese period. Another explanation is that this Chinese word is under the qusheng (Tone "C"), which arose, according to Haudricourt's famous theory, through the influence of an <latinform>*-s</latinform> suffix, that could have favored the assimilation of a final labial to apical position.</par>

<par>This etymon is obviously an allofam of <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref>. The reflexes in this set arose through assimilation of the lateral root-initial to the nasal prefix. In some cases (e.g. Jingpho, Meithei) the prefix still co-occurs with the secondary nasal root-initial. In other languages (e.g. Lahu) the prefix has disappeared as such, after supplanting the original root-initial ("prefix preemption").<footnote>For the first use of the term "prefix preemption" see Matisoff 1972, "Tangkhul Naga and comparative TB".</footnote> This allofam occurs in Jingpho, and is widely attested in Lolo-Burmese; there are also a few scattered reflexes in languages of the NE Indian Areal Group (Chang, Meithei, Mru).</par>

<par>This root typically occurs as the last syllable in compounds for such bodyparts as CHEST / BREAST, SHOULDER, FIST, FOREHEAD, and BUTTOCK (cf. Lahu <latinform>ni-ma-qa-pɨ</latinform>, <latinform>làʔ-qá-pɨ</latinform>, <latinform>làʔ-chɨ̂ʔ-pɨ</latinform>, <latinform>nā-qā-pɨ</latinform>, and <latinform>qhɛ̂-qhɔ-pɨ</latinform>, respectively).</par>

<par>This etymon resembles, but seems distinct from, <xref ref="1275">#1275</xref>, which also refers to a variety of rounded body parts. It is sometimes difficult to distinguish these two roots in Lolo-Burmese, perhaps due to insufficient phonemicization in the sources.</par>
<par>There is a similar Tai root: cf. Siamese <latinform>sǔan</latinform>.</par>

<par>A few languages have quite similar reflexes, except for a final <latinform>-k</latinform> instead of <latinform>-p</latinform>. For the moment, we have assigned these to <xref ref="1318">#1318</xref>.</par><par>In a couple of languages (Puiron, Kulung), this root has developed the meaning NOSE, although the usual etymon for nose is the allofamically related <xref ref="803">#803</xref>.</par>

In addition it appears in compounds for UMBILICAL CORD, thence by extension to NAVEL itself. For the concept of "OICC's", see VSTB pp. 184-5.</par>

The meaning of this etymon ranges from 'string, cord' to 'OICC' to the specific plants 'cane, rattan'.</par>

The most likely prefix is <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction>, which is found in a number of OC words for human body parts and is cognate to the PTB prefix <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> having a similar function.  In their latest system, Baxter and Sagart reconstruct this prefix in words for 'nose', 'belly', 'head', and 'right hand', as well as 'skin'.  The OC pronunciation of <hanform>皮</hanform> can be reconstructed as <reconstruction>*m-pra-j</reconstruction>, with root <reconstruction>*pra</reconstruction>. (See Sagart & Baxter 2010.)</par><par>Ignoring the medial <reconstruction>*-j-</reconstruction> (whose existence in OC is increasingly discounted, and which does not correspond regularly to anything in PTB), the correspondences between the variant PTB form <reconstruction>*pra</reconstruction> and the OC root are regular.</par><par><plainlatinform>ZJH</plainlatinform></par>

哺 OC *b’wo, GSR #102i' 'have food in mouth'; Schuessler 2007:174 *bâh; B & S 2011: *bˁa-s {[b]ˁa-s}; Mand. bǔ.

<par>Especially in Himalayish and the NE Indian Areal Group, it is often difficult to distinguish <reconstruction>*d(w)əy</reconstruction> from its probable co-allofam <xref ref="1181">#1181</xref>. Thus, Kulung <latinform>wa-di</latinform> ("bird + egg") looks like Tangsa (N.Naga) <latinform>wu-di</latinform>, Karbi <latinform>vo-ti</latinform>, etc., but other Kiranti languages (e.g. Limbu, Athpare) have reflexes with <latinform>-n</latinform>, apparently from <reconstruction>*-l</reconstruction>. Kanauri, Lepcha, and Tibetan retain <latinform>-l</latinform>. For now, we are assigning all Himalayish reflexes of this word-family to <reconstruction>*dil</reconstruction>, though a better Proto-Himalayish reconstruction would be <reconstruction>*di-l</reconstruction>. Similarly, some languages of the NE Indian Areal Group retain overt reflexes of <reconstruction>*-l</reconstruction>, but many have forms with open syllables. Our assignment of some NE Indian forms to <xref ref="300">#300</xref> rather than <xref ref="1181">#1181</xref> remains arbitrary.</par>

<par>Benedict 1939:225 ("Semantic differentiation in Indo-Chinese") analyzed compounds like Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>ar-tui</latinform> 'egg' as "bird + water". STC postulates a connection between <reconstruction>*twəy</reconstruction> 'egg' (STC #168) and a general root <reconstruction>*ti(y)</reconstruction> 'water; moist' (STC #55 and pp. 45, 135, 196). (This latter root should actually be set up as <xref ref="298">#298</xref>.<footnote>See also LTSR #109 <reconstruction>*N/ʔ-tit/k</reconstruction> 'soak; saturate'.</footnote> The proposed connection between EGG and WATER is complicated by the related forms for EGG with final <reconstruction>*-l</reconstruction>. STC (n. 149) admits that Dhimal has different forms for EGG (<latinform>tui</latinform>) and WATER (<latinform>tśi</latinform>), and yet a third form in <latinform>hna-thi</latinform> 'snot'. Elsewhere in STC (p. 135, discussing the Karen cognates; and p. 196, in connection with the putative two-tone contrast for PTB), Benedict suggests that the etyma for EGG and WATER do indeed descend from separate allofams, different in both tone and initial at the PTB stage. </par>

<par>There is a further semantic connection between EGG and HEEL (both being smooth and rounded). Cf. Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>ar-tui</latinform> 'egg' (perhaps "bird-water", <latinform>ke-ar-tui</latinform> 'heel' ("foot-egg", i.e. "foot-bird-water"). The syllable <latinform>-ar-</latinform> must be bleached of all avian meaning by the time it gets incorporated into HEEL. See Matisoff 1994 ("How ‘dull’ can you get?"), which also brings BUTTOCK into the same network of semantic associations as HEEL.</par>

<par>The putative semantic connection between BIRD and WATER is strengthened by compounds of BIRD with other roots for WATER, e.g. < <xref ref="2080">#2080</xref> (Tangkhul <latinform>tara</latinform> 'water', <latinform>har-ra</latinform> 'egg' (<latinform>har</latinform> 'bird'), <latinform>hai-ra</latinform> 'semen').<footnote>See also <xref ref="1614">#1614</xref>.</footnote> Less clear, but possibly a parallel formation is Muya <latinform>vɑ³³ vɑ⁵⁵</latinform> 'egg' (<latinform>vɑ³³</latinform> 'bird'). Cf. also Maring <latinform>wa-yui</latinform> 'egg', with the second element < <xref ref="1013">#1013</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is widely distributed, appearing in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Jingpho-Asakian, Karen, and Qiangic (including Tangut), and perhaps in a few Loloish forms (Xide, Nosu) where the initial is palatalized to an affricate.</par>

<par>Seven forms from four Luish [Asakian] languages (Sak Bawtala, Sak Dodem, Ganan, Kadu) cited in Luce 1985 (Chart L) have the confusing gloss 'Penis/Testicles'. Since there are two phonologically similar etyma in this area, <reconstruction>*ti</reconstruction> EGG/TESTICLE and <xref ref="641">#641</xref>, these forms have presented serious problems of analysis, and have been reglossed to mean either 'penis' or 'testicle', but not both. See Etymon Note under <xref ref="641">#641 </xref>, below.</par>
<par>See HPTB <reconstruction>*twəy ⪤ *dwəy</reconstruction> 'water; egg', p. 195.</par>

蚳 OC *dʼi̯ər GSR #590m, ‘ant egg’; Li 1971: (*drjid); Baxter 1992: (*drjɨj); Mand. chí.This rare character does not appear in Li or Baxter. However, reconstruction in either system is not in doubt based on reconstruction of other characters with the same phonetic and identical Middle Chinese pronunciation (e.g. Baxter 1992:750 坻 < drij < *drjɨj (Mand. chí)). But it is worth noting that at least one character with the same phonetic is placed in the 微 Wēi group by Baxter and reconstructed with *-ij: 鴟 tsyhij < *thjij (Mand. chī), presumably because it is found rhyming with a 微 Wēi group word (Shijing 24.3A). If this character is not simply an exception, then according to Baxter’s rhyme group division, characters with the same phonetic are spread across the two rhyme groups. In the absence of rhyming evidence, *drjij is also a possible reconstruction for 蚳 in Baxter’s system.Assuming the semantics are not problematic,R. S. Cook (1995:63) offers much evidence that the real meaning of GSR 590m is 'scorpion'. [JAM] the correspondences are quite sound. Examples of OC *-id (Li 1971) corresponding to TB *-əy are numerous, and include 死 OC *sjid ‘die’ (Li), TB *səy, Mand. sǐ; 四 OC *sjid 'four' (Li), TB *b-ləy, Mand. sì; 屎 OC *hrjid 'excrement', TB *kləy, Mand. shǐ. The TB final *-i should not be troubling; we would expect this relatively rare PTB final to correspond to OC in the same way as *-əy (in parallel to TB *-əw and *-u; see the discussion of #1168). On the correspondence of OC medial *-r-, see the discussion of 中 under #3584.
[ZJH]

<par>The second syllable of Phunoi <latinform>ʔɨ⁵⁵ ɲiʔ⁵⁵</latinform>looks like a reflex of this root, but it is isolated in Lolo-Burmese, and the resemblance is probably fortuitous. Similarly, Pa-O Karen <latinform>ŋàʔ</latinform> might also show an accidental resemblance to this root.</par>

<par>This root, discussed in detail in JAM 1995 "Sino-Tibetan palatal suffixes revisited", appears in reduced form as <latinform>-i</latinform> or <latinform>-y</latinform> in a large number of etymologies. See also HPTB:482-489.</par><par>The high-rising tone of Lahu <latinform>ɛ́</latinform> 'baby, child' is evidence for a final stop at the Proto-Loloish level, which motivates Bradley's reconstruction of PL <reconstruction>*s-yak<sup>L</sup></reconstruction>.</par><par>For a similar though distinct root, see <xref ref="2806">#2806</xref>.</par>

<par>This root appears in several Tani languages and is perhaps related to a group of Naga forms with the <latinform>m-</latinform> prefix.<footnote>Chokri <latinform>merü zho</latinform>, Mao <latinform>mare</latinform>, Chang <latinform>mili shen</latinform>, perhaps also Meluri <latinform>a re pi</latinform>.</footnote> Several languages show semantic associations between RAINBOW and BOW (for arrows), as well as with SNAKE. The Naga forms just mentioned resemble many reflexes of <xref ref="2623">#2623</xref>.</par>

<par>We include these Southern Loloish forms in this word family, even though the change from <latinform>*w</latinform> to <latinform>m</latinform> has not yet been discovered elsewhere. Perhaps these forms reflect a doubly-prefixed prototype <latinform>*s-m-wap</latinform>, which in turn derives from the full disyllable <latinform>sin-wap</latinform> that we find in Jingpho.</par>
<par>This sparsely attested root has been found only in Nungic and Burmish.</par>

<par>The basic form of this interrogative morpheme is <latinform>*ka</latinform>, with two suffixed variants. For a discussion of palatal suffixes in Tibeto-Burman, see Matisoff (1995) "Sino-Tibetan palatal suffixes revisited". The variant with final <latinform>*-ŋ</latinform> is attested in Written Tibetan as well as in Lahu.</par>

<par>This etymon seems basically to belong in masculine semantic space, since most TB reflexes mean PENIS. The likely Chinese comparandum <hanform>牡</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>mǔ</latinform>, means MALE (of certain birds and animals; see below), and this sense also occurs in TB (see Taraon <latinform>mau-a</latinform>, below), implying that MALE is the more original meaning.</par><par>Benedict 1979<footnote>"A note on Karen genital flipflop", LTBA 5.1:22-23, n. 36.</footnote> suggests a connection with PTB <reconstruction>*maːk</reconstruction> (better <reconstruction>*s-maːk</reconstruction>) 'son-in-law' [STC #324; HPTB p. 325]: cf. WT <latinform>mag-pa</latinform>, Lepcha <latinform>myok</latinform>, Dhimal <latinform>hma-wa</latinform>, Miri <latinform>mak-bo ~ mag-bo</latinform>, Jingpho <latinform>dà-máʔ</latinform>, WB <latinform>səmak</latinform>, Lahu <latinform>ɔ̀-má-pā</latinform>, Lushai [Mizo] <latinform>maːk-pa</latinform>. The long vowel is supported by the Mizo form, and the <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix is evidenced indirectly by Lepcha (where <latinform>-y-</latinform> < <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction>), and directly by Dhimal and WB. This <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix is in turn undoubtedly a reduction of PTB <reconstruction>*za</reconstruction> SON / CHILD.</par><par>Nevertheless, this root has undergone an enantiodromic shift or "genital flipflop" to mean VAGINA in both Karbi and (probably) Newar (neither of these forms was cited in Benedict 1979), so that it was evidently reinterpreted in some areas as 'genitals (of either sex)'. This makes one wonder whether the root <xref ref="1353">#1353</xref>, reconstructed on the basis of forms from Naga languages, might also be somehow related to the present etymon.</par>

A likely Chinese comparandum has been suggested by Benedict 1979, pp. 22-23 and n. 36: 牡 OC *môg/*mə̯u [irreg.], GSR #1063a ‘male (quadruped)'; Li 1971: *məgwx; Baxter 1992: *m(r)juʔ; Mand. mǔ [JAM].The Middle Chinese reflex of this word has irregular vocalism: *mə̯u. This accounts for the discrepancy between Baxter's reconstruction (with medial *-j-) and Li's (without medial *-j-). The presence of *-j- in Baxter's form accounts regularly for vocalic development, but the medial itself must then be assumed to drop irregularly in Middle Chinese.The proposed connection to PTB *maːk presents some difficulties. The Chinese vocalism would normally correspond to a rounded PTB vowel. (See examples in Gong 1995 #61-69.) Although those open syllables in OC reconstructed with *-g by Karlgren and Li sometimes correspond to syllables with coda *-k in PTB, more generally we find OC *-k corresponding to PTB *-k and open syllables corresponding to open syllables.Schuessler 2007:391 argues that the Chinese word is related to Austroasiatic forms meaning 'male animal'.
[ZJH]

<par>This root seems almost exclusively to be attested in Lolo-Burmese, where both the prefixed and unprefixed variants occur: e.g. Lahu <latinform>vi</latinform> < <latinform>*-wi</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>fī</latinform> < <latinform>*s-wi</latinform>. <par>Interesting reflexes of this etymon with labiovelar initial include the labial-initial Lahu form, the dental-initial Karenic forms, and the zero-initial Chin forms, the last two of which indicate that the velar-initial was sometimes treated as a prefix.</par><par>The nasal suffix is reconstructed on the basis of the Chinese comparandum.</par>

Some reflexes have <latinform>-a</latinform>, others have a back vowel (<latinform>-u</latinform> or <latinform>-o</latinform>); the rhyme is reconstructed as <reconstruction>*-aw</reconstruction> on the testimony of Dimasa.</par>

<par>Several reflexes of this root have a velar rather than a palatal nasal, which appear secondary. Notice the WB doublet <latinform>ŋrui ⪤ ńui</latinform>. Perhaps the wide semantic divergence among the reflexes favored the development of allofamy in this root.</par>

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>

<par>This etymon appears to be confined to the NE Indian Areal Group and Monpa (Mama Tshona), with the basic meaning 'ball; round object', appearing in such transparent compounds as Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>mit-mu</latinform> 'eyeball' and <latinform>til-mu</latinform> 'testicle'. Several Loloish forms also seem to be related.</par>

<par>The basic meaning of this root seems to be 'bloom, open up (as a flower), bud', thence 'give birth'. It is reconstructed in STC #260 as 'open, bud', with reflexes offered from WT, Nung, Jingpho, WB, and Karbi. The semantic connection between this root for 'open, bloom' and 'bear a child' was first suggested by W. T. French (1983:455), who reconstructs Proto-Northern Naga <reconstruction>*ʔ-bəw</reconstruction>. STC reconstructs both voiceless- and voiced-initial allofams (<reconstruction>*bu ⪤ *pu</reconstruction>), which we can now interpret as reflecting a simplex/causative opposition ('bloom' vs. 'cause to bud' [i.e. 'give birth']). A PLB reconstruction <reconstruction>*ʔpu²</reconstruction> (equivalent to <reconstruction>*ʔbu²</reconstruction>) is given in Matisoff 1988, <emph>The Dictionary of Lahu</emph> (p. 831), with the glottalization inferred from the very-low tone of the Lahu reflex <latinform>pū</latinform>. This reinforces French's PNN reconstruction with <reconstruction>*ʔ-</reconstruction>, as does the <emph>a-chung</emph> [<latinform>ḥ</latinform>] in the WT reflex (<latinform>ḥbu</latinform>). The Phom form <latinform>bəʔ</latinform> cited by French (<emph>loc. cit.</emph>) has been reanalyzed as belonging under <xref ref="3480">#3480</xref> [q.v.].</par>

<par>This etymon is closely related to one for BIRD, set up as <xref ref="1603">#1603</xref>. The original version of STC sets up <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction> for BIRD (STC #99), but this is revised to <reconstruction>*(b)wa</reconstruction> (largely because of Bahing <latinform>ba</latinform> 'fowl') in the indexes (pp. 209, 211). In some languages (e.g. Jingpho and Kadu) a reflex of <xref ref="301">#301</xref> occurs as a prefix in bird-names (<latinform>ù-</latinform>). Marrison (p. 459) sets up three Proto-Northern Naga allofams for BIRD: <reconstruction>*C-waw</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*ua</reconstruction>, and <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction>. It is sometimes difficult to distinguish the reflexes of <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*wu</reconstruction>. For example, in the following NE Indian Areal Group forms, the italicized syllables are to be derived from <reconstruction>*wa ⪤ *wu</reconstruction> BIRD, with another constituent conveying the meaning EGG: Chang <latinform><emph>ao</emph>kiak</latinform>, Chokri <latinform>thü<emph>vu</emph>zü</latinform>, Konyak <latinform><emph>ao</emph>kiak</latinform>, Konyak (Tamlu) <latinform><emph>ʌu</emph>ji</latinform>, Ntenyi <latinform>a<emph>wüü</emph>-atsü</latinform>, Sema <latinform><emph>au</emph>khu</latinform>, Tangsa <latinform><emph>¹vu</emph>¹t̪ʌi</latinform>, Tangsa (Moshang) <latinform><emph>wu</emph>◦di</latinform>, Wancho <latinform><emph>ao</emph>-ti</latinform>/<latinform><emph>ɔ</emph>-ti</latinform>, Yacham-Tengsa <latinform><emph>u</emph>-tü</latinform>.</par>

<par>These roots have a wide distribution in TB. In Lolo-Burmese (including Jinuo), the root <reconstruction>*wu</reconstruction> is widely attested with the predominant meanings 'egg; lay egg; hatch'. In Bai this root means 'hatch', while in Northern Naga and Qiang (Mawo, Yadu) it generally means 'bird', occurring as the first element in compounds for 'egg' (see below).</par>

<par>As explained at length in VSTB:113-123, the root reconstructed as <latinform>*tsywap</latinform> in STC #239, is actually a fused version of a binome <latinform>**tsi-wap</latinform>, where the first element is the root <xref ref="458">#458</xref>. The original meaning of the second element seems to be 'soft, spongy', which is its meaning in Jingpho. The Jingpho form <latinform>sìn-wóp</latinform>, literally "spongy organ" is opposed to Jingpho <latinform>sin³¹ tʃaʔ³¹</latinform> 'liver', where the second element means 'hard, firm'. These two organs, dominant in the thoracic and abdominal cavity respectively, are evidently conceived of as antonymous, similar to the Turkish pair <latinform>ak-ciğer</latinform> 'lung' (lit. 'white liver') and <latinform>kara-ciğer</latinform> 'liver' (lit. 'black liver').</par>

The primary comparandum is:蹍 OC *ni̯ɑn, GSR #201c 'trample'; B & S 2011: *nranʔ {[n]r[a][n]ʔ}; Mand. niǎn.But consider the following as well:展 OC *ti̯ɑn, GSR #201a 'roll over, unfold, develop'; B & S 2011: *trenʔ; Mand. zhǎn.
輾 OC *ti̯ɑn, GSR #201b 'roll over and back, roll to the side'; B & S 2011: *trenʔ {tre[n]ʔ; Mand. zhǎn.
碾 OC (*ni̯ɑn), not in GSR #201, but attested in MC, 'grind, crush, husk; stone roller', Mand. niǎn.All four of the above etyma have a common semantic focus, i.e. 'roll over'. Moreover, the first three (and likely the last) belong to the same OC phonetic series, and show OC *alveolar initials differing only in manner. This strongly suggests an allofamic relationship between 蹍, 展, 輾, and possibly 碾, to be reconstructed as *ti̯ɑn ⪤ *ni̯ɑn or similar for Old Chinese, if not earlier.

肚 OC *d'o 'stomach'; GSR: not in #62; Li 1971: *dagx; Baxter 1992: *laʔ or *daʔ; Mand. dù. There is also a variant with a voiceless initial, meaning 'animal stomach used as food'. GSR #62 is reconstructed as a lateral-initial series by Baxter, but since the character is not attested until late, it is possible that this word had a dental initial, and that the character used to write it was created after the change *l- > *d- had taken place, making 土 *hlaʔ > *thaʔ, Mand. tǔ a suitable phonetic element.The difficulty with this comparison lies in the vowel, as we would expect to find PTB *a corresponding with OC *a, as in Chinese 吾 'first person pronoun' *ngag (Li)/*nga (Baxter), Mand. wú and PTB *ŋa (STC #406); and Chinese 魚 'fish' *ngjag (Li)/*ng(r)ja (Baxter), Mand. yú and PTB *ŋya (STC #189).
