<par>This root has so far been attested only in Tamangic and rGyalrongic. The meaning always seems to refer to the contents of a container rather than to satiety from eating. The rGyalrongic forms show evidence both of a palatal and labial glide, but no trace of a final <latinform>-t</latinform>. The Tamangic forms, on the other hand, attest to an alternation between final stop and nasal, as well as evidence of a palatal semivowel (but not of a labial one).</par>
<par>The semantic center of gravity of this root seems to be BODY HAIR, with an extension into FEATHER, and thence to WING.</par>

<par><hanform>髮</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*pi̯wɑ̆t</reconstruction> 'head hair 'GSR #276i; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*pjat</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992 #997: <reconstruction>*pjot</reconstruction>; B & S 2011: --; Schuessler 2007:228-9; Mand. <latinform>fǎ, fà</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>胈</hanform> OC <latinform>*pwɑ̂t</latinform> 'small hairs on body' GSR #276f; Li 1971: <latinform>(*bat)</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>(*bat)</latinform>; B & S 2011: --; Schuessler 2007:228-9; Mand. <latinform>bá</latinform>.</par><par>The Chinese and Yakha forms suggest a possible allofam <reconstruction>*pwat</reconstruction>.</par><par>ZJH: The OC vowel might be either <reconstruction>*a</reconstruction> or <reconstruction>*o</reconstruction>; with a labial initial and dental coda, the developments to Middle Chinese are identical, making it difficult to determine the OC vowel with certainty. However, either reconstruction is problematic for a comparison with PTB <reconstruction>*u</reconstruction>.  OC <reconstruction>*o</reconstruction> does sometimes correspond to Tibetan <latinform>u</latinform>, but only in open syllables (where we find Burmese <latinform>uiw</latinform>, as in the well-known words for ‘steal’: <hanform>寇</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*kʰˤo-s</reconstruction>; WT <latinform>rku</latinform>; WB <latinform>khuiw</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>kòu</latinform>), and before velars (where we find Burmese <latinform>o</latinform>, as in ‘torch, light, flame’: <hanform>燭</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*tok</reconstruction>; WT <latinform>dugs</latinform>; WB <latinform>tok</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>zhú</latinform>). There is no evidence for anything other than a coda <reconstruction>*-t</reconstruction> in Chinese, and no evidence for any kind of stop coda in the TB reflexes; the makes both the vowel and the coda problematic for cognacy. An internal Chinese derivation from <hanform>發</hanform> ‘come out; emit’ (Mand. <latinform>fā</latinform>) is also possible for these Chinese words (Schuessler 2007:228-9).</par><par>A comparison of the Chinese to WT <latinform>phud</latinform> ‘hair-knot’ is tempting given the dental coda, but OC <reconstruction>*o</reconstruction> does not correspond to Tibetan <latinform>u</latinform> before dentals.</par>

黃 OC *g’wɑ̂ng, GSR #707a-b 'yellow'; B & S 2011: *N-kʷˁaŋ; Mand. huáng.
光 OC *kwɑ̂ng, GSR #706a-e 'light, bright'; B & S 2011: *kʷˁaŋ; Mand. guāng.
曠 OC *k’wɑ̂ng, GSR #707o 'bright'; B & S 2011: *kʷʰˁaŋ-s {[k]ʷʰˁaŋ-s}; Mand. kuàng.

<par><hanform>塵</hanform> OC <latinform>*d’i̯ĕn</latinform>, GSR #374a 'dust (n.)'; Coblin 86:68 ST <latinform>*rdjiul</latinform> > OC <latinform>*drjin</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:184 <latinform>*drən</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*drən {[d]rə[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>chén</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root occurs mostly in Kuki-Chin (cf. PKC <reconstruction>*phoo</reconstruction> VanBik 2009:#353), but also apparently in Nungic and Loloish. The semantics of this root are interesting, since the skin is here conceived of as a protection or shield from the outside world.</par>

<par>This etymon is extremely well attested, occurring in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese, Nungish, Qiangic, and probably Baic. Its range of meanings extends from WANT/DESIRE to LOVE to COPULATE. This root is notable for the large number of prefixes that it has acquired in various branches of TB: <reconstruction>*r-</reconstruction> (in Qiangic [rGyalrong, Ergong] and Amdo Tibetan); <reconstruction>*N-</reconstruction> (in Loloish and perhaps Meluri); <reconstruction>*d-</reconstruction> (in Written Tibetan), and <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> (in Qiangic [Pumi]).</par><par>This etymon has been grammaticalized in Lahu, where it now functions as a desiderative particle, e.g. <latinform>qay gâ</latinform> 'want to go', <latinform>dɔ̀ gâ</latinform> 'want to drink', etc. See Matisoff 1988a:399-400.</par><par>We have tentatively grouped Loloish forms with initial velar stops with those with initial labial or velar nasals, assuming that the former descend from the unprefixed root while the latter show preemption of the root initial by the nasal prefix.</par>

<par>This root displays considerable vocalic alternation, ranging, e.g. from <latinform>*dar</latinform> (in Bodic) to <latinform>*dur</latinform> (in Tani) to <latinform>*dir</latinform> (in Tangkhulic). The <latinform>-u- ⪤ -i-</latinform> alternation is well established at all stages of TB (HPTB:493-505). The <latinform>-a- ⪤ -u-</latinform> alternation is attested but much rarer (see e.g. <xref ref="1417">#1417</xref> [HPTB:513]).</par><par>The Written Burmese / Chin comparison is due to Kenneth VanBik.</par><par>A nasal prefix is attested in Bodic and Qiangic / rGyalrongic.</par><par>There is a promising Chinese comparandum.</par>

彈 OC *d’ɑ̂n, GSR #147n 'shoot pellets; beat; shake'; Coblin 1986:152 ST *dar > OC *dan; Schuessler 2007:206, 489 *dân; B & S 2011: *dˁar {(Cə.)[d]ˁar}; Mand. tán.
彈 OC *d’ɑ̂n, GSR #147n 'bow for shooting pellets; pellet'; Schuessler 2007:490 *dâns; B & S 2011: *Cə.dˁar-s {Cə.[d]ˁar-s}; Mand. dàn.
憚 OC *d’ɑ̂n, GSR #147o 'fear, dislike'; Coblin 86:152 ST *dar > OC *danh; Schuessler 2007:206 *dâns; Mand. dàn.
戰 OC *t̂i̯ɑn, GSR #147r 'tremble, fear; fight, battle, war'; Schuessler 2007:206, 605 *tans; B & S 2011: *tar-s; Mand. zhàn.The Chinese/Tibetan comparison is due to Coblin 1986:152. The Chinese word has meanings ranging from 'shoot pellets' to 'beat' to 'play on a stringed instrument' to 'shake'. The semantic development to the latter meaning is probably due to the trembling of a string being played.S611 娠 振 晨

<par>This etymon is best attested in Himalayish, with some support also from the NE Indian Areal Group and Asakian, and perhaps Qiangic and/or WB. The reflexes have either simple sibilant or affricate initials. I presume the proto-initial to have been <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction>, in view of the natural tendency to develop secondary affrication before <latinform>-i-</latinform>.</par><par>It is very possible that this etymon belongs together with <xref ref="1349">#1349</xref>, in a single word-family, in view of the similar semantic range of <xref ref="3589">#3589</xref>.</par><par>A particularly interesting form is Newar <latinform>mhu◦tu◦si</latinform>, where <latinform>mhu◦tu</latinform> means 'mouth', and <latinform>si</latinform> means 'corner (of the mouth)'. The basic meaning of <latinform>si</latinform> is 'border, margin, bank', as in <latinform>khu-si</latinform> 'riverbank', <latinform>mi-kha-pu-si</latinform> 'eyebrow' (K. P. Malla, p.c.). Thus Newar <latinform>si</latinform> has nothing to do with the present etymon.</par><par>This etymon seems quite distinct from <xref ref="477">#477</xref>, which so far has been attested only in Himalayish.</par>

<par>Cf. also <xref ref="225">#225</xref>, where the semantic center of gravity is lower in the body (i.e. lower back, buttocks, tail); however, the Sherpa, Lepcha, and Yakha forms are included here because of their stopped finals. </par><par>We recognize three quite similar etyma in this semantic area: the present root <xref ref="3322">#3322 </xref>, <xref ref="225">#225</xref>, and <xref ref="5639">#5639</xref>. In the case of phonologically eroded languages (e.g. Loloish, Qiangic, Baic), it is sometimes difficult to decide among them. Several Loloish languages have distinct reflexes for at least two of them, e.g. Lahu <latinform>tɔ̀</latinform> 'lower back / loins / lumbar region' < <xref ref="225">#225 </xref> vs. <latinform>ɔ̀-mɛ̄-tu</latinform> 'tail' < <xref ref="5639">#5639 </xref>; Written Burmese <latinform>taŋ-pâ</latinform> 'buttock' < <xref ref="225">#225 </xref> vs. <latinform>khâ-tuiŋ</latinform> 'lower back / loins / lumbar region' < <xref ref="3322">#3322 </xref>. Two Southern Loloish binomes are particularly problematic (Akha <latinform>dɔ̀-dàŋ</latinform> 'buttocks' and Sangkong <latinform>toŋ³¹ taŋ⁵⁵</latinform> 'buttocks'), where the first syllables are from <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>, while the second syllables are of uncertain origin despite their superficial resemblance to <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>.</par>

蟺 OC *d̂i̯ɑn, GSR #148p; Mand. shān;
螾 OC *di̯ən, GSR #450j; B & S 2011: *m-qərʔ {[m-q](r)ə[r]ʔ}; Mand. yīn;
蚓 OC *di̯ĕn, GSR #371c; B & S 2011: *m-qərʔ {[m-q](r)ə[r]ʔ}; Mand. yīn.

<par>This etymon appears in only a few NE Indian Areal Group and Himalayish languages. Thulung reflects medial <reconstruction>*-w-</reconstruction> rather than <reconstruction>*-r-</reconstruction>. This root is quite distinct from the coincidentally nearly homophonous etymon <xref ref="1606">#1606</xref> (cf. Bokar <latinform>po-rok</latinform> 'chicken', WB <latinform>krak</latinform> 'id.'), despite the fact that many languages have an association between 'chicken' and 'penis' (cf. Spanish <emph>polla</emph> 1. 'chicken' 2. [<emph>Slang</emph>] 'penis').</par>

脛 OC *g’ieng, GSR #831k 'leg, shank'; B & S 2011: *m-kʰˁeŋ-s; Mand. jìng.
莖 OC *g’ĕng, GSR #831u 'stalk'; B & S 2011: *m-k-lˁ‹r›eŋ; Mand. jìng; cf. STC note 218, p. 70.
<par>STC n. 218, p. 70 already proposed a "possible doublet" PTB <reconstruction>*keŋ</reconstruction> in order to accommodate some Kuki-Chin and Chinese forms.</par>

<par>This is a Nepali form which appears as a loan in a number of Tibeto-Burman languages. Other areal words for DUCK that have been borrowed into TB are Indo-Aryan <xref ref="5716">#5716</xref>, Mandarin <xref ref="5718">#5718</xref>, and Austro-Tai <xref ref="5713">#5713</xref>.</par>
<par>The Kiranti languages reflect a final velar stop, while Tibetan points to a final labial, with this latter development perhaps due to assimilation to the initial labial.</par>
<par>This etymon typically occurs as second element in compounds beginning with <xref ref="803">#803</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed as <reconstruction>*nəw</reconstruction> in STC #419, on the basis of forms from WT, Tsangla, WB, and Lushai (Mizo). (The rhyme <reconstruction>*-əw</reconstruction> in STC is equivalent to Benedict's earlier reconstruction <reconstruction>*-uw</reconstruction>.) The <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> in our reconstruction is reflected in the voiceless nasals in Kuki-Chin and Barish languages (including the Lushai (Mizo) form <latinform>hnu-te</latinform> cited in STC #419), the <latinform>tă-</latinform> prefix in Awa Khumi, and directly in Thanphum <latinform>ʃə̆nũ⁵</latinform> and WT <latinform>snun-pa</latinform> 'suckle'.</par><par>This root has been frequently combined with dental and velar suffixal elements, both nasals and stops. The reflexes are presented in the following sections according to the particular allofam they represent: the unsuffixed root <reconstruction>*s-nəw</reconstruction> in <xref ref="253">#253 </xref>; forms with dental suffixes (<reconstruction>*s-nəwt</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*s-nəwn</reconstruction>) in <xref ref="3586">#3586 </xref>; and forms with velar suffixes (<reconstruction>*s-nəwk</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*s-nəwŋ</reconstruction>) in <xref ref="259">#259 </xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon is very widely distributed in ST, appearing in Chinese, the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese, Karenic, and Qiangic (including Tangut). Many languages have reduplicated forms (e.g. Ergong <latinform>nu-nu</latinform>), as is to be expected in such a hypocoristic concept as BREAST.</par><par>Somewhat similar phonologically is the root <reconstruction>*s-nye-n</reconstruction>, which is here treated as a separate etymon, below <xref ref="254">#254 </xref>.</par>

<par>This richly attested root must be considered distinct from <xref ref="530">#530</xref>, although certain forms (especially those meaning 'muscle') seem to show overlap between the two roots.</par><par>The <latinform>-n</latinform> suffix is directly attested in Jingpho, Geman/Kaman, and Chinese.</par><par>This morpheme appears as a prefix in numerous animal names and words for parts of the body. See HPTB:102 and STC:106-108.</par><par>We are positing an allofamic relationship between the forms with medial <latinform>*-ya-</latinform> and <latinform>*-i-</latinform>. See HPTB: 278, 306, 449.</par>
<par>This root is undoubtedly related to <xref ref="1364">#1364</xref>, but is here treated separately in view of the considerable semantic difference between the two.</par>

<par><hanform>後</hanform> OC <latinform>*g’u</latinform>, GSR #115a 'behind, after'; Schuessler 2007:280 <latinform>*gôʔ</latinform>~<latinform>*ɦôʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ɢˁoʔ {[ɢ]ˁ(r)oʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>hòu</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymon is so far only attested in Kuki-Chin.</par>

<par>The WT doublet <latinform>sod</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>sad</latinform> confirms the reconstruction with <latinform>*-wa-</latinform>.</par><par>For phonologically similar and perhaps overlapping etyma in this area see <xref ref="150">#150</xref>, <xref ref="5768">#5768</xref>, <xref ref="5770">#5770</xref>.</par>
<par>This form is related to <xref ref="7677">#7677</xref>.</par>
<par>A number of languages have developed an initial dental nasal due to the following palatal glide (e.g., Qiang [Mawo], Nasu, Sani).</par>

蝸 OC *kwɑ, GSR #18c 'snail'; Coblin 86:135 ST *kwroy > OC *kwrar; Schuessler 2007:515 *krôj~*C-rôj; B & S 2011: *k.rˁoj (dial. › *kˁroj); Mand. wō, guā.
螺 OC (*lwɑ̂r), GSR (not in #577) 'snail'; Schuessler 2007:515 *krôj~*C-rôj; B & S 2011: *k.rˁoj; Mand. luó.
<par>See Gong 2001:26. The Chinese comparandum is not to be found in GSR #609.</par>
<par><hanform>籃</hanform> OC [not in GSR #609]; B & S 2011: <latinform>*k.rˁam</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>lán</latinform>. 609g </par>

岡 OC *kɑ̂ng; GSR #697a 'ridge, crest of hill'; Mand. gāng.
京 OC *kli̯ɑ̆ng; GSR #755a 'mound, hill, capital city'; B & S 2011: *kraŋ {[k]raŋ}; Mand. jīng.
鯨 OC *g’li̯ɑ̆ng, GSR #755f 'whale'; Schuessler 2007:316 *graŋ; B & S 2011: *ɡraŋ {[ɡ]raŋ}; Mand. jīng.

<par>This root is solidly established. The cognacy of the Lahu form is open to question, although it seems directly cognate to Luquan <latinform>ntʂ'e¹¹</latinform> 'borrow / lend / debt'. (Lahu voiced initials descend from *prenasalized ones.) The Luquan form has, however, been assigned to <xref ref="2314">#2314</xref> for the moment.</par>
<par>The final <latinform>-p</latinform> in Meithei is probably due to assimilation to the preceding rounded vowel.</par>

<par>W.T. French 1983:503 reconstructs Proto Northern Naga <latinform>*baːy</latinform> with the meaning JUMP, clearly a related notion to LAME / LIMP in the sense that special movements of the lower limbs are involved.</par>

The following set of Chinese etyma is offered on the basis of discussion in Schuessler 2007:158-9, 170-71 et pass; (also see Matisoff 1995: ST Palatal Suffixes Revisited). These etyma can be considered allofams within Chinese, as well as primary comparanda for PTB *(p/b)aːy on both phonological and semantic grounds:跛 OC *pwɑ̂, GSR #25m 'walk lame'; Schuessler 2007:171 *pâiʔ; B & S 2011: *pˁajʔ; Mand. bǒ.
頗 OC *p’wɑ̂, GSR #25p 'slanting, oblique, perverse'; Schuessler 2007:158 *phâi; Mand. pō.
坡 OC (*p’wɑ̂), (not in GSR #25) 'slope, slanted'; Schuessler 2007:158 *phâi; Mand. pō.
陂 OC *piɑ, GSR #25i 'river bank, dike'; Schuessler 2007: *pai; B & S 2011: *paj {p(r)a[j]}; Mand. bēi, bī.
詖 OC *piɑ, GSR #25h 'biased, insincere'; Schuessler 2007:158 *paih; Mand. bì.

<par>This etymon is certainly co-allofamic with <xref ref="2154">#2154</xref>, via a set of semantic associations familiar from Western languages, where left-handedness has been regarded as "gauche" or "sinister". </par>

<par>This etymon is derived from the Indo-Aryan root <emph>LIKH</emph> 'scratch, write' (Turner 1966: 640-641). Compare Skt. <emph>likhá-</emph> 'writer' with Jingpho <latinform>làikā</latinform> 'book'.</par>
<par>This etymon is so far sparsely attested, with the best putative cognates occurring in Loloish (Lahu, Jinuo), Chin (Lakher [Mara]), and perhaps West Himalayish (Pattani).</par>
<par>This root has so far only been found in TB languages of Nepal.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed as a Proto-Tani sesquisyllable with alternation between the <reconstruction>*mV-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*pV-</reconstruction> prefixes, the latter occurring only in Padam-Mishing. It seems clear that the nasal pre-syllable derives from <xref ref="2473">#2473</xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon displays the same <reconstruction>*-u- ⪤ *-i-</reconstruction> variation found with <xref ref="5541">#5541</xref>, as well as a similar variation in the position of articulation of the final stop: compare Bengni <latinform>muː-pup</latinform>, Rongmei <latinform>ka-pút</latinform>, and Sunwar <latinform>'pu:k◦pu 'pā-cā</latinform>. Weidert 1987 (#651, #1017) sets up "Kuki-Naga-Chin" <reconstruction>*m-but</reconstruction>, and J. Sun 1993 reconstructs Proto-Tani <reconstruction>*pup ⪤ *puk</reconstruction>, but in TB generally <latinform>-p</latinform> seems to be the most widespread final consonant.</par><par>The two labials (initial and final) in this root have an imitative flavor, apparently mimicking the labial activity involved in sucking and kissing.</par>

<par>This allofam with <latinform>-u-</latinform> vocalism is more common, and so far it is only before this variant that the nasal prefix is attested. This prefix should be set up for PTB as a whole, since it occurs in Himalayish (Bunan) <latinform>a◦mbok də◦ca</latinform> and Qiangic (rGyalrong) <latinform>kəwu◦nəpok</latinform>, as well as widely in Naga languages. This variant frequently occurs in compounds after reflexes of <xref ref="3451">#3451</xref>, below (e.g. Milang <latinform>mum-pup-ma</latinform>), and this may in fact be the source of the prefixal <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> in my reconstruction.</par>

<par>The allofam <latinform>pip</latinform> has so far only been found in Himalayish. </par><par>Some reflexes show final <latinform>-t</latinform> or <latinform>-k</latinform>. For similar variability of the final consonant, see <xref ref="5541">#5541</xref>, <xref ref="5537">#5537</xref>, <xref ref="5540">#5540</xref>. The variation in position of articulation of the final consonant seems clearly to have been caused by the influence of suffixal material. Cf. the consonantal sequences across morpheme boundary in forms like Bantawa <latinform>phïpt-</latinform> and Chamling <latinform>pips-</latinform>, which seem to lie behind forms like Limbu <latinform>piːtt-</latinform>, where the labial final has been assimilated to an earlier dental suffix. Note also the variation between final stop and nasal in Thulung.</par>

苦 OC *k’o, GSR #49u 'bitter'; Coblin 86: *khagh; B & S 2011: *kʰˁaʔ; Mand. kǔ.
肝 OC *kɑ̂n, GSR #139l 'liver'; B & S 2011: *s.kˁar {*s.kˁa[r]}; Mand. gān.

<par>This root acquired all three nasal codas (<latinform>*-n</latinform>, <latinform>*-n</latinform>, <latinform>*-ŋ</latinform>) in various languages, but by far the most common allofam is the open syllable form.</par>

<par>This etymon is set up as <reconstruction>*tsyur</reconstruction> (= <reconstruction>*tśur</reconstruction>) in STC #188 with the proto-gloss SQUEEZE/WRING, on the basis of the Bahing, Bunan, Hakha, and Kanauri forms, the latter glossed as 'to milk'. There does not seem to be any allofamic connection between this root and <xref ref="5539">#5539</xref>.</par>
<par>A few forms (Newar <latinform>wakudhi(k)</latinform>, Pwo [Bassein] <latinform>bòʔ phàʔ thèʔ</latinform> ) point to a possible <latinform>*-k</latinform> suffix with this root. </par>

<par><hanform>獵</hanform> OC <latinform>*li̯ɑp</latinform>, GSR #637e; Mand. <latinform>liè</latinform>.</par><par>Citations of <hanform>獵</hanform> 'tortoise; turtle' are seen in <hanform>爾</hanform><hanform>雅</hanform> Er Ya and <hanform>周禮</hanform> Zhou Li. While it doesn't affect the validity of the comparandum, we should point out that the character <hanform>獵</hanform> most often represents a different etymon in OC, i.e. 'to hunt (prey)' (GSR #637e); this is the only commonly recognized sense of <hanform>獵</hanform> <latinform>liè</latinform> in modern standard Chinese. Karlgren (GSR #637e) notes that <hanform>獵</hanform> <latinform>liè</latinform>, when used to mean 'turtle', is a phonetic loan for another character, now obsolete, perhaps attested as the Zhou dynasty pictograph shown in GSR #637b. [SPB]</par>
<par>This root is perhaps from <latinform>*r-tsik</latinform>, a prefixed and pre-empted version of <xref ref="418">#418</xref>.</par>
<par>Since the PTB prefix <latinform>*r-</latinform> regularly develops into the Jingpho syllabic nasal prefix (see STC:109 and HPTB:129), we treat these Jingpho forms as a unit.</par>

20a9a OC *k’ɑ̂m~*ngi̯əm, GSR #607f-g 'precipitous; bank along fields'; Schuessler 2007:332 *khâmʔ; B & S 2011: *N-qʰˁəmʔ; Mand. kǎn ~ yín ~ ǎn.
嵁/磡 OC *k’əm ~ *k’ɛm ~ *ngəm, GSR #658r 'cliff; bank; step'; Schuessler 2007:332 *khə̂ms; B & S 2011: (*kʰˁəm-s); Mand. kàn.

<par>The WT form is the key to this reconstruction, although no other Himalayish cognates have been found so far. This etymon seems to be widespread in Loloish, with probable reflexes also in Northeast India, Nungish (Dulong), and Qiangic (Muya).</par>
<par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Cf. <hanform>夙</hanform> <latinform>sù</latinform>, comparandum for <xref ref="1837">#1837</xref>.</par>

陰 OC *ʔi̯əm, GSR #651y-z; B & S 2011: *qum {q(r)[u]m}; Mand. yīn 'dark'.
蔭 OC *ʔi̯əm, GSR #651a'-c'; B & S 2011: *qəm-s {q(r)[ə]m-s}; Mand. yìn 'shade'.
WB um

<par>French (1983: 468) connects this etymon with PTB <latinform>*sal</latinform> 'choose, select, elect, set apart', which was personally communicated to him by Benedict. Whether this root was ever published is unclear.</par>

販 OC *pi̯wɑ̆n, GSR #262f 'to trade; buy and sell'; Schuessler 2007:230 *pans; B & S 2011: (*pan-s); Mand. fàn.
反 OC *pi̯wɑ̆n, GSR #262a-d 'turn, return; reverse, contrary'; Coblin 86:153 OC *pjanx; Schuessler 2007:230 *panʔ; B & S 2011: *Cə.panʔ; Mand. fǎn.
拚翻 OC *p’i̯wɑ̆n, GSR #220d 'turn over, overturn, change'; Schuessler 2007:229 *phan; B & S 2011: (*pʰar); Mand. fān.
<par>Chinese shows an allofam with final <latinform>-t</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>血</hanform> OC <latinform>*χiwet</latinform>, GSR #410a-c 'blood'; Coblin 86:45 ST <latinform>*shwiʔ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*hwit</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:547-8 <latinform>*hwît</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*m̥ˁik</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xiě</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon is represented in the Naga, Qiangic, and Himalayish branches. The Amdo (Bla-brang) form <latinform>hwoŋ wa</latinform> seems to represent a distinct root. The velar prefix is attested in both Ntenyi and Lepcha.</par><par>The second syllable of Ersu <latinform>da⁵⁵ vɛ⁵⁵ ȵo³³</latinform> looks like a possible reflex of this etymon, but <latinform>vɛ⁵⁵ȵo⁵⁵</latinform> is glossed as 'intestines'. 卵 OC *lwɑ̂n, GSR #179a 'egg; testicle’; Li 1971: *luanx; Baxter 1992 #557: *g-ronʔ; Mand. luǎn.In Baxter’s system, MC l- is always derived from OC *C-r-, that is, an initial r with a prefixed consonant (to be distinguished from *Cr- in which r functions as a medial). In other systems, MC l- may descend from simple initial *r-. Baxter 1992 gives two separate reconstructions for this word: *g-ronʔ (#557) and *C-ronʔ (#949, with *C unspecified). There is some reason to suppose that if the prefix *C- is to be reconstructed it would be a velar (Baxter 1992:387).The correspondence between PTB *r- and Middle Chinese l- is well-attested. Although both the Chinese and PTB forms have a rounded vowel and a nasal final, the correspondences are problematic. No generally accepted OC/PTB cognates show an *-n/*-m correspondence. OC *ua (Li 1971) / *o (Baxter 1992) generally corresponds to PTB *o, not *u; for example, 脫 OC *hluat 'remove' (Li 1971); PTB *hlot; Mand. tuō. We would expect PTB rhyme *-um to correspond to OC *-əm (Li 1971); see examples in Gong 1995.Gong 1995 #41 compares Chinese 卵, which he reconstructs *ruanx, with Written Tibetan sro-ma ‘nit’ (see #1172, below), following Benedict 1976:190 ("Sino-Tibetan: another look"). Schuessler 2007:369 makes the same comparison, citing PTB *(s-)rwa rather than *s-row. For this comparison to hold, a nominalizing *-n suffix must be posited for the Chinese form (Schuessler 2007:74-75).
[ZJH]

<par>The Nocte forms require no further comment. Of special interest is the Jingpho form <latinform>lətáʔ</latinform>, for which several explanations have been offered (cf. STC nn. 102, 109, 137). In my opinion, what happened was, after the obstruentization of original <latinform>l</latinform> to <latinform>t</latinform> in Jingpho, yielding <latinform>taʔ</latinform>, the form was reprefixed by the general root <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>, here reduced to <latinform>lə</latinform>. Note that in Jingpho, a secondary prefix <latinform>lə-</latinform> occurs in many verbs describing action with either the hands or the feet (see my portion of STC, n. 308 and O. Hanson 358-85). The Tangut and Ergong forms seem to have a similar origin.</par><par>Another explanation might be in terms of metathesis, <reconstruction>**d-lak</reconstruction> > <reconstruction>*l-dak</reconstruction>, in order to avoid the disfavored <latinform>dl-</latinform> cluster.</par>

<par>This root is quite widely distributed in TB, appearing in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese (including Jinuo), and Bai. It was reconstructed as PLB <reconstruction>*b(y)et</reconstruction> in Matisoff 1972b #5 (<emph>The Loloish Tonal Split Revisited</emph>) and HPTB p. 375, but that has been revised here to <reconstruction>*b(y)at</reconstruction> to accommodate those reflexes that have <latinform>-a-</latinform> vocalism (e.g. WB, Kokborok, Bunan). Bradley's (1979) PLB reconstruction without medial glide, <reconstruction>*bat</reconstruction>, does not account for certain Loloish reflexes with front vowels (e.g. Lahu <latinform>cha-pɛ̀ʔ</latinform>, since the regular Lahu reflex of <reconstruction>*-at</reconstruction> is <latinform>-eʔ</latinform>). Bauer 1991 (LTBA 14.1) treats this etymon as part of a binome <reconstruction>*dzu(k)-byet</reconstruction> (see <xref ref="1356">#1356</xref> for the first element), and adduces parallels in Hmong-Mien languages and Chinese. Benedict (1990) hypothesizes a "Proto-Austro-Kadai" binome <reconstruction>*tu-pi</reconstruction> (for the first element see <xref ref="661">#661</xref>). These freewheeling proposals remain to be evaluated by future generations. There does not seem to be any connection between this etymon and <xref ref="668">#668</xref>, which is confined mostly to Himalayish, and whose reflexes have back vowels.</par><par>The Bai forms listed below may well be loans from Chinese.</par><par>K. P. Malla suggests that the first syllable of Newar (Dolakhali) <latinform>pi-ci</latinform> 'vagina' really means 'breast' (see Kathmandu Newar <latinform>pi-si</latinform> 'breast' under <xref ref="276">#276</xref> above), but can be used euphemistically to mean 'vagina'. In the other Himalayish forms cited here, however, this morpheme does definitely seem to mean 'vagina'.</par>

According to H. Stimson 1966,Hugh Stimson, "A tabu word in the Peking dialect" (Language 42.2:285-294). Cited in Bauer 1991:150. the taboo word 屄 (Mand. bī) does not appear in dictionaries until the 17th century. (For the correspondence between OC *e and PTB *ya, cf. 八 OC *pret (Baxter) 'eight', PTB *b-ryat, Mand. bā. However Schuessler believes that this word is derived from 'to open' (PST *pe), with the addition of *-t marking "nouns of naturally occurring objects". See Schuessler 2007:161, 414. 癢 ‘itch’ ⪤ 痒 'disease', OC *zi̯ɑng, GSR #732i,732r; Li 1971: *rang(x); Baxter 1992: *(l)jang(ʔ); Mand. yǎng 'itch', yáng 'disease'.Setting aside the question of semantics, this is a plausible comparison.For other members of this phonetic series, Baxter reconstructs *z(l)ang, *k(l)ang and *kh(l)ang. The presence of medial *-l- ties the pronunciation of these words together; but the medial is given in parentheses because it does not affect subsequent development and Baxter is probably doubtful about its presence. In Matisoff 2010 ("Toward a Eurasian Bestiary"), I surmised that the labial initial arose through a reduction of a Mon-Khmer etymon (see Mon-Khmer <latinform>*bheʔ</latinform>, Shorto 2006:#260).</par><par>Lisu <latinform>pu̱³³</latinform> and Hani (Khatu) <latinform>pjá tɔ̃</latinform> seem clearly to be loans from Burmese.</par><par>After much hesitation, STC chose to treat the initial combination <latinform>*sr-</latinform> as an intrinsic cluster rather than as a sequence of prefix plus root initial. See also Matisoff 1988: 469 ("The Dictionary of Lahu").</par><par>The presumed semantic development of this etymon is fairly complex. According to one scenario, the basic meaning would be TAKE / ACCEPT, whence it acquired the more abstract sense of RELY UPON / DEPEND, and from there, to the notion of HANGING LOOSELY (cf. English <emph>depend</emph>, from Latin <emph>pendere</emph> 'to hang'). On the other hand, the semantic development might have been in exactly the opposite direction!</par>

<par><hanform>略</hanform> OC <latinform>*gli̯ɑk</latinform>, GSR #766v 'sharp'; Schuessler 2007:369 <latinform>*rak</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*rak {[r]ak}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>lüè</latinform>.</par>

<par>The first syllable of this binome apparently means TAIL / BACK (cf. <xref ref="205">#205</xref>), possibly because of the dramatic appearance of a camel's back.</par>

<par>Either reconstruction has something to be said for it. If the root was <reconstruction>*p-kur</reconstruction>, the rGyalrongic forms are straightforward reflexes, while the Kuki-Chin forms would show prefix pre-emption. On the other hand, if the root was <reconstruction>*k-pur</reconstruction>, we must assume simple prefix loss in Kuki-Chin, and metathesis in the rGyalrongic forms.</par>

<par>This root shows the classic pattern of medial <latinform>-u-</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>-i-</latinform> variation, with Written Burmese attesting both variants. (The unaspirated WB initials go back to PTB <latinform>*gr-</latinform>.)</par>

<par>We recognize three quite similar etyma in this semantic area: the present root <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>, <xref ref="3322">#3322</xref>, and <xref ref="5639">#5639</xref>. Two Southern Loloish binomes are particularly problematic (Akha <latinform>dɔ̀-dàŋ</latinform> 'buttocks' and Sangkong <latinform>toŋ³¹ taŋ⁵⁵</latinform> 'buttocks'), where the first syllables are from <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>, while the second syllables are of uncertain origin despite their superficial resemblance to <xref ref="225">#225 </xref>.</par>
<par>Jingpho has a double reflex of this root, one with <latinform>-p</latinform> and one with <latinform>-ʔ</latinform> (from PTB <latinform>*-k</latinform>).</par>

<par>In some TB languages, an apparent reflex of PTB <latinform>*s-ŋar</latinform> may refer not to ‘frost’ per se, but instead refer e.g. to ‘frost’ or ‘dew’ equivocally, or to ‘dew’ alone.  Cases in point are particularly evident within Lolo-Burmese, especially among closely related languages. For instance, WB has an etymon <latinform>hnâŋ</latinform> or <latinform>hnɑŋ³</latinform>, glossed ’dew’, but also as ‘fog, mist’; the notion ‘frost’ is expressed as WB <latinform>hnɑŋ³khɑi³</latinform>, i.e. ‘hard dew’. Similary, Atsi (Zaiwa) has <latinform>ŋa̱n⁵¹</latinform> ‘dew’, while ‘frost’ is <latinform>ŋa̱n⁵¹phju⁵¹</latinform> lit. ‘white dew’, and ‘ice’ is <latinform>ŋa̱n⁵¹thuŋ²¹</latinform>. Single lexemes which can mean either ‘frost’ or ‘dew’, apparently depending on context, are seen in Langsu (Luxi) <latinform>ŋa̱ŋ³¹</latinform> and Maru (Langsu) <latinform>ŋə̱ŋ³¹</latinform>. </par><par>In Loloish, reflexes of PTB <latinform>*s-ŋar</latinform> most often refer to ‘frost’ and/or ‘ice’, cf. Black Lahu <latinform>ᴀ³³ŋə³³</latinform> ‘frost, ice’; the notion ‘dew’ is then expressed through reflexes of a separate etymon PTB <latinform>*(d)zil</latinform> ‘dew’ (PLB <latinform>*ʔ-dzi²</latinform>), either alone or in compounds, cf. Black Lahu <latinform>tsi³¹ɣə³¹</latinform>, lit. ‘dew water’.  The situation in Nusu (Bijiang), however, is noteworthy: PTB <latinform>*s-ŋar</latinform> yields Nusu <latinform>hɑ̃ʴ³⁵</latinform>, which as a single lexeme is glossed ‘frost’, while the compound <latinform>hɑ̃ʴ³⁵tɕhe³⁵</latinform> , literally ‘frost dew’, is the term for ‘dew’ (compare e.g. Nusu (Southern), which simply has <latinform>tɕhe⁵⁵</latinform> ‘dew’). In Leqi (Luxi) (a Burmish language), <latinform>ŋa̱n³³</latinform> by itself is glossed ‘ice’; the term for dew is <latinform>ŋa̱n³³tʃu⁵⁵</latinform>,  lit. ‘ice dew (?)’, 'frost' is <latinform>ŋa̱n³³phiu³³</latinform>, lit. 'white ice’.</par><par>In dealing with the above examples, it should be kept in mind that in the real world, the phenomena we call ‘fog, mist, dew, frost’ refer to points along a continuum. When air temperature cools to near the dew point, then we see ‘fog’ or ‘mist’. When the surface temperature of an exposed, thin object cools to near the dew point, then we see ‘dew’. When the surface temperature of that object cools to near a dew point that is already below freezing, then rather than ‘dew’, we see ice in the form of ‘frost’.</par><par>We can, then, cautiously say that some languages describe this meteorological continuum using relatively more single lexemes (e.g. English and Chinese), while other languages use relatively fewer single lexemes but more lexemic compounds (e.g. Burmese, Atsi, Langsu and Maru). Regarding Nusu (Bijiang) <latinform>hɑ̃ʴ³⁵</latinform> and Leqi <latinform>ŋa̱n³³</latinform>, we surmise that the underlying lexemic meaning is maximally inclusive, i.e. ‘condensed/congealed water in general’; this may account for the seeming disparity between the surface connotation of single lexemes vs. the same lexemes in compounds.</par><par>Admittedly, our suppositions above are based on data likely collected in part via prepared word lists, which can unwittingly impose lexemic categories and distinctions that speakers of a particular language being studied may not find natural. More in-depth and beyond the glossary studies of Lolo-Burmese languages will hopefully become available to help us better shed light on this interesting area of the TB lexicon.</par>

It occurs in Tani, Himalayish, Jingpho, and perhaps Karenic. There is also a good Chinese comparandum, <hanform>蓋</hanform>, meaning 'cover; lid' (below).</par>

蓋 OC *gɑ̂p/*kɑ̂b>*kɑ̂d, GSR #642q, ‘to cover, thatch’ ⪤ 'a cover, lid'; Li 1971: *gap/*kabh>*kadh; Baxter 1992 #1732, 1731: *ɦkap/*kaps>*kats; Mand. hé ⪤ gài.These two related words perhaps derive from an unattested root *kap. The noun has an *-s suffix, which derives a passive noun from a transitive verb (i.e. ‘the thing that is used to cover’ > ‘a lid’). In the verb form, Baxter’s *ɦkap develops as *gap, the notation suggesting morphological derivation from a root with initial *k-. While voicing derivation is a well-known Old Chinese morphological process, it usually derives intransitives from transitive verbs, so the function here is not clear.The only difficulty with the TB comparison is the vowel. While Baxter’s six-vowel system permits the possibility of reconstructing *ɦkop or *gop for the verb, the noun must be reconstructed with *a to account for subsequent sound changes, and this forces a reconstruction of *a in the verb as well.It is possible that OC *a corresponds regularly to PTB *o before bilabial codas. Unfortunately, there are very few proposed cognate sets that would help us establish or refute such a possibility.
[ZJH]

<par>The source of this prefix is a matter of speculation, but cf. the apparently prefixal dental that occurs before other roots referring to the upper limb in scattered TB languages: e.g. [Himalayish] WT <latinform><emph>d</emph>puŋ</latinform> 'shoulder'; [Qiangic] rGyalrong <latinform><emph>te</emph>khlye</latinform> 'upper arm'; [Asakian] Kadu <latinform><emph>ta</emph>paung, <emph>tə</emph>hù</latinform> 'arm', Sak <latinform><emph>ta</emph>kú</latinform> 'arm', Lui <latinform><emph>ta</emph>khu</latinform> 'arm'; [Naga] Ao Chungli <latinform><emph>te</emph>ben</latinform> 'arm', <latinform><emph>ta</emph>shikang</latinform> 'wing', Ao Mongsen <latinform><emph>tü</emph>pen, <emph>tü</emph>cha</latinform> 'arm', <latinform><emph>ta</emph>cha</latinform> 'wing', Yacham Tengsa <latinform><emph>ta</emph>ka</latinform> 'wing'; [Tani] Taraon <latinform><emph>ta:</emph>lõ</latinform> 'feather'.</par><par>We assign to this allofam reflexes with affricate initials (e.g. Tujia, Qiang, Naga).</par><par>The cluster <latinform>dl-</latinform> is not tolerated in most TB languages<footnote>An exception is Sani =Nyi (Loloish), which has developed <reconstruction>*dl-</reconstruction> < <reconstruction>*by</reconstruction> (e.g. 'bee' <reconstruction>*bya</reconstruction> > Nyi <latinform>dla-ma</latinform>).</footnote>, so that the presence of the dental prefix has favored the palatalization of the root-initial lateral.</par><par>This allofam is the immediate source of the Karenic forms assembled below <xref ref="773">#773</xref>.</par><par>Forms from Northern Naga languages show initial variation among the simple palatal semivowel, dental stops, and palatal affricates: e.g. Tamlu <latinform>bɯn-yak</latinform>, Tangsa <latinform>³pul-džak</latinform>, and Nocte <latinform>¹ʌ-dʌk</latinform>, all meaning 'leaf'. To Weidert belongs the credit for identifying the morphemic structure of these compounds as "tree-hand". For convenience's sake, we list all these forms under the present allofam.</par>

<par><hanform>翼</hanform> OC <latinform>*gi̯ək</latinform>, GSR #954d 'wing'; Li 1971: <latinform>(*rək)</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #469: <latinform>*ljɨk</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ɢrəp</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:570; Mand. <latinform>yì</latinform>.</par><par>ZJH: As discussed in Handel 2009:133-134, if reconstructed with initial OC <reconstruction>*l-</reconstruction> (a view shared by Schuessler 2007:570), this is a perfect match to PTB <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>, a comparison that is preferable to <reconstruction>*d-yak</reconstruction>. Chinese <hanform>翼</hanform> ‘wing’ <latinform>yì</latinform> and <hanform>亦</hanform> ‘armpit’ <latinform>yì</latinform> thus differ only in the reconstructed main vowel (<reconstruction>*ə</reconstruction> vs. <reconstruction>*a</reconstruction>), both of which correspond regularly to PTB <reconstruction>*a</reconstruction>. The nature of the word-family relationship of the two words within Chinese is unclear.</par><par>Note that Baxter & Sagart (2011) reconstruct coda <reconstruction>*-p</reconstruction> for <hanform>翼</hanform> <latinform>yì</latinform>, with “dialectal” development of <reconstruction>*-p</reconstruction> to later <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction>, because they believe this is the same word as that written with the homophonous character <hanform>翌</hanform>, also ‘wing’, which has phonetic <hanform>立</hanform> ending in <reconstruction>*-p</reconstruction>. Their reconstruction would separate <hanform>翼</hanform> ‘wing’ <latinform>yì</latinform> and <hanform>亦</hanform> ‘armpit’ <latinform>yì</latinform> into two different Chinese words families, and preclude the comparison of the former to the <reconstruction>*lak/*yak</reconstruction> family of PTB arm/hand/wing roots.</par>

<par><hanform>亦</hanform> OC <latinform>*zi̯ɑ̆k</latinform>, GSR #800a-c; Li 1971: <latinform>*rak</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>(*ljAk)</latinform>; B & S 2011:  <latinform>*m‑q(r)Ak</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:568; Mand. <latinform> yì</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>掖</hanform> OC <latinform>*zi̯ɑ̆k</latinform>, GSR #800l; Li 1971: <latinform>*rak</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>(*ljAk)</latinform>; B & S 2011: --; Schuessler 2007:568; Mand. <latinform> yì</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>掖</hanform> OC <latinform>*zi̯ɑ̆k</latinform>, GSR #800m; Li 1971: <latinform>*rak</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>(*ljAk)</latinform>; B & S 2011: --; Schuessler 2007:568; Mand. <latinform> yì</latinform>.</par><par>These three characters are considered by GSR to represent the same lexical item.</par><par>ZJH: It seems likely, as noted in Handel 2009:133-5, that the Chinese word began with <reconstruction>*l-</reconstruction> rather than Karlgren’s <reconstruction>*z-</reconstruction>, so that this word is a perfect match for the basic PTB root <xref ref="695">#695 </xref> rather than <xref ref="696">#696 </xref>. Schuessler (2007:568), who like Baxter (1992) reconstructs initial <reconstruction>*j-</reconstruction>, compares this Chinese word instead to PTB <reconstruction>*(g-)yak</reconstruction> just as Matisoff does here. Baxter & Sagart’s most recent (2011) reconstruction, <reconstruction>*m-q(r)Ak</reconstruction>, would seem to make the comparison to PTB problematic, but the general correspondence patterns of their uvular initial <reconstruction>*q-</reconstruction> in Tibeto-Burman are unclear, so the possibility of a comparison of the root <reconstruction>*qAk</reconstruction> to PTB <reconstruction>*g-yak</reconstruction> is not out of the question (<reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> is a human body part prefix). Because Baxter and Sagart have not published a full explanation of their individual reconstructions, it is not clear why they have chosen to reconstruct <reconstruction>*q-</reconstruction> for this word, but most likely it is because uvular initials are reconstructed for other words in GSR #800 and #954. </par>

臍 OC *dzʼiər, GSR #593f ‘navel’; Li 1971: *dziəd; Baxter 1992: *dzɨj; Mand. qí.The initial correspondence of OC *dz- to PTB *s-t- could be explained by metathesis, as discussed in Bodman 1969“Tibetan sdud ‘folds of a garment’, the character 卒, and the *st- hypothesis.” Bulletin of the Institute of History and Philology, Academia Sinica 39:327-45.. Baxter 1992:229-30 allows for the general developments *St- > ts- and *Sd- > dz-, where *S is a metathesizing prefix. This prefix is usually reconstructed when phonetic series evidence suggests an original stop initial. Within GSR #593, however, there is no evidence for original dental stops. Nevertheless, Schuessler 2007:421 admits the possibility, suggesting possible pre-Old Chinese forms *dz(l)əi < *s-d(l)əi.On the apparent mismatch between the voiceless PTB initial and the voiced OC initial, see the discussion under #1654.For a discussion of the correspondence between OC *-ɨj and TB *-ay, see #1160.
[ZJH]

There also seems to be a phonosemantic connection between the stop-initialled allofam *s-tay and a PTB root *taːy meaning BELT/ZONE/WAIST, first reconstructed in Matisoff 1985a (GSTC #95) on the basis of WT sde 'part, portion (e.g. of a country), province, district, territory, zone', Lahu de 'belt of land between the high rain-forest and the plains; large expanse of terrain', Luquan Lolo ntʰe¹¹ 'plain, flat expanse', Lushai (Mizo) tai 'waist', Karbi daykha 'middle, intermediate'. This implies that WT lte-ba 'navel' is a co-allofam of WT sde 'zone'.A very likely Chinese comparandum is 帶 OC *tɑ̂d 'girdle, sash', Mand. dài 'belt, zone'. For the semantics, cf. Eng. zone < Gk. zōnē 'girdle' < PIE *yōs-nā (*yōs 'to gird').

<par>On the basis of the data so far available, this etymon has a fairly wide though scattered distribution, with reflexes in a single Himalayish language (Hayu), a few closely related Loloish languages (Noesu, Nosu, Nusu), and a couple of Qiangic languages (rGyalrong and Xixia). It is noteworthy that the nasal prefix is overtly attested in Hayu, rGyalrong, and Xixia, so that it may confidently be set up for PTB. This etymon is quite distinct from the most widely attested root for PENIS with nasal prefix, <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref> above, as proven e.g. by the Hayu reflexes: <reconstruction>*m-ley</reconstruction> > Hayu <latinform>mli</latinform>, <reconstruction>*m-be</reconstruction> > Hayu <latinform>-(m)be</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon looks rather similar to <latinform>*s-bwam</latinform> PLUMP / SWOLLEN [STC #172] as well as to <xref ref="1317">#1317</xref> [STC #30], since the bicep of the arm is protuberant like the calf of the leg. Cf. Mizo <latinform>puam</latinform> 'swell', <latinform>pan-puam</latinform> 'muscle of upper arm'.</par><par>Both Meithei and Lui show assimilation of the final consonants of the first syllable (< <xref ref="745">#745 </xref>) to the initial of the second. The Liangmei form seems to show radical reduction of the first syllable to syllabic <latinform>m</latinform>, implying a two-stage development: assimilation to the initial of the second syllable, followed by reduction and prefixization of the first syllable.</par>

<par>The Lahu rhyme <latinform>-ɛ</latinform> is often from PL <reconstruction>*-um</reconstruction>.</par><par>The putative Bisu and Phunoi reflexes of this weakly attested root show initial <latinform>h-</latinform> rather than a labial stop. If these are indeed valid reflexes, they would point to a development reminiscent of Old Japanese <reconstruction>*p-</reconstruction> going to Modern Japanese <latinform>h-</latinform>.</par>

<par>The first syllable of this Tani binome is from <xref ref="695">#695</xref>. The Proto-Tani rhyme <latinform>*-ər</latinform> is rare, so that J. Sun reconstructs it only for two roots.</par>

<par><hanform>鐵</hanform> OC <latinform>*t’iet</latinform>, GSR #1256b 'iron'; Coblin 1986:98-99 ST <latinform>hliek*</latinform> > PC <latinform>*hlik</latinform> > OC <latinform>*thit</latinform>; Schuessler 2003:497 <latinform>*lhêt ~ lhît</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*l̥ˁik</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>tiě</latinform>.</par><par>For <hanform>鐵</hanform>, Karlgren posits OC <latinform>*t’-</latinform>; later reconstructions have ST, PC and/or OC voiceless lateral <latinform>*lh-</latinform>, <latinform>*hl-</latinform>, or <latinform>*l̥-</latinform> (at the MC stage, all reconstructions for <hanform>鐵</hanform> show initial <reconstruction>*t’-</reconstruction>, cf. B & S 2011 MC <latinform>*thet</latinform>).</par><par>Significantly, the recent ST, PC and OC reconstructions for <hanform>鐵</hanform> cited above, with initial <latinform>*lh-</latinform>, <latinform>*hl-</latinform>, or <latinform>*l̥-</latinform>, bear close comparison with PTB <latinform>*l-tsyak</latinform> 'iron'. In fact, some scholars would prefer to derive WT <latinform>lčags</latinform> 'iron' via palatalization of earlier PT <latinform>*hlyak</latinform> (Coblin, ibid.) or earlier <latinform>*lhyaks</latinform> (Schuessler, ibid.). This looks phonetically plausible; however, it begs the question of subgroup relationships within TB. Is the same shift <latinform>*hly/lhy-</latinform> > <latinform>*lč</latinform> intended to account not only for WT <latinform>lčags</latinform>, but presumably, and ultimately also, WB <latinform>jak</latinform> 'bit of a bridle (of iron)' and Lotha Naga <latinform> jon¹ tʃak²</latinform> 'iron' (Bruhn 14 PCN:III.174)? For now, this intriguing issue will be left open for further inquiry. [SPB]</par>
<par>The stronghold of this root seems to be Tani, with a solid Thulung cognate and possible reflexes in Naish and Karenic.</par>
<par>This poorly attested root requires further confirmation.</par>
<par><hanform>發</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯wɑ̆t</latinform>, GSR #275c; B & S 2011: <latinform>*Cə.pat</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>fā</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is not reconstructed in STC, but has been reconstructed as <latinform>m-pat</latinform> in several sources (LTSR #124, GSTC #26, HPTB:330). </par><par>A medial <latinform>*-r-</latinform> is reconstructed for Proto-Tani and Proto-Karenic, and appears overtly in several other scattered languages (Khezha, Mao, Pattani, Tsangla, Motuo Menba). Final <latinform>*-s</latinform> appears instead of <latinform>*-t</latinform> in Kanauri and Kiranti languages; in at least two rGyalrongic dialects (Heishui Shashiduo <latinform>ka'pʰəts</latinform>) and (Maerkang Baiwan Muerji <latinform>kamə'pʰatz</latinform>) a final sequence <latinform>-ts</latinform> or <latinform>-tz</latinform> appears, motivating our reconstruction where the stop + sibilant co-occur syntagmatically. Chepang <latinform>has</latinform> also has final <latinform>-s</latinform> but the development of a *labial stop to Chepang <latinform>h</latinform> is not otherwise attested.</par><par>The nasal prefix is abundantly attested in Qiangic, rGyalrongic, and Jingpho. </par><par>The Chinese comparandum with medial <latinform>*-j-</latinform> was originally proposed by Coblin (1986:130).</par>

24226 OC *χmi̯wər, GSR #583e 'burn'; B & S 2011: *m̥əjʔ; Mand. huǐ.
毀 OC *χi̯wɑ̆r, GSR #356a 'demolish (by fire)'; B & S 2011: *m̥ajʔ {[m̥](r)ajʔ}; Mand. huǐ.
燬 OC *χi̯wɑ̆r, GSR #356b 'fire (blazing)'; Coblin 86:79: ST **myəy ~ sməy/smyəy > OC *hmjərx; Mand. huǐ.
火 OC *χwɑ̂r, GSR #353a-c 'fire'; Coblin 86:79: ST **myəy ~ sməy/smyəy > OC *hmərx; Mand. huǒ.

<par>PKC has two homophonous roots, <latinform>*may</latinform> TAIL and FIRE. These are quite separate etymologically (<xref ref="1288">#1288</xref> vs. <xref ref="2136">#2136</xref>), although KVB (#794, #767) glosses certain forms with both English words.</par>

聿 OC *bi̯wət, GSR #502a 'writing pencil'; B & S 2011: *m-rut {*[m-]rut}; Mand. yù.
筆 OC *pli̯ət, GSR #502d 'writing pencil'; B & S 2011: *p.rut {p.[r]ut}; Mand. bǐ.Cf. #110. In Chinese, 筆 bǐ has the additional sense 'write (v.)'.Also cf. 理 lǐ, comparandum for #2616.

<par>This "funny" root is quite well attested in several branches of the family, usually as a sesqui- or di-syllable. Several languages (Ao, Bisu, Magar) show a variant with final velar stop, while others (WB, Gurung) show some sort of laryngeal feature after the vowel.</par><par>Yang 1985 cites several extra-TB lookalikes: Khmer <latinform>kliək</latinform>, Indonesian <latinform>ketiak</latinform> 'armpit', Cham <latinform>kəlĕk</latinform> 'tickle'. See also Benedict 1975a:410 ("Austro-Thai Language and Culture, with a glossary of roots").</par>
<par>This root is part of a complex word family with perhaps as many as ten allofams, with meanings ranging from 'white, yellow' to 'bright, shine' to 'fire, kindle, roast'. See HPTB:428-430.</par>

<par>There is no single widespread root for SEMEN to be reconstructed for PTB, though several promising local candidates are attested here and there. Many forms for this concept are euphemistic (e.g. words that otherwise simply mean 'water'); many others are compounds of the structure PENIS + WATER (e.g. Tangkhul <latinform>shaŋ-ra</latinform>). Other "sementic" associations include SEMEN <----> SAP (Akha <latinform>aˇ dzi˯</latinform> means both [Lewis p.35]); SEMEN <----> SHIT (Lahu <latinform>nī-qhɛ̂</latinform>); SEMEN <----> FAT (Lahu <latinform>nī-chu</latinform>); SEMEN <----> CONTEMPLATION (WT <latinform>t'ig-le</latinform> [Jäschke p. 231]). The present root appears in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, and Qiangic.</par>
<par>This root is so far only been discovered in TB languages of Nepal. It was reconstructed with a short vowel in PSLTB.</par>
<par>This tentatively offered root is so far quite weakly attested.</par>

<par><hanform>燭</hanform> OC <latinform>*t̂i̯uk</latinform>, GSR #1224e 'candle, taper; shine, illuminate'; Schuessler 2007:625 <latinform>*tok</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*tok</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>zhú</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Also see <hanform>灼</hanform> <latinform>zhuó</latinform> and <hanform>的</hanform> <latinform>dì</latinform>, comparanda for <xref ref="5387">#5387</xref>.</par>

<par>There are several Tujia forms with initial velar (e.g. <latinform>si³lu¹<emph>ga¹</emph></latinform> 'pig's bone', <latinform>lu¹<emph>ga¹</emph></latinform> 'bone'), which might belong to this root, if we assume preemption by the prefix. On the other hand, we are considering the Bai forms to descend from this etymon on the strength of the <latinform>-w-</latinform> medial.</par><par>Jingpho has developed a nasal prefix with this root.</par>

呂 / 吕 OC *gli̯o, GSR #76a-c 'spine; pitchpipe'; Schuessler 2007:367 *raʔ; B & S 2011: *raʔ {[r]aʔ}; Mand. lǚ.
梠 OC *gli̯o, GSR #76e-f 'beam supporting rafters of a roof'; Schuessler 2007:367 *raʔ; B & S 2011: (*raʔ {[r]aʔ}); Mand. lǚ.
旅 OC *gli̯o, GSR #77a 'arrange in a series; one after another; troops, multitude'; Schuessler 2007:367 *raʔ; B & S 2011: *raʔ {[r]aʔ}; Mand. lǚ.Comment: The etymon above glossed 'arrange in a series...', etc. is apparently the earliest to be associated with the graph 旅 lǚ in Chinese. The same graph, 旅, was later borrowed to represent another, unrelated etymon glossed 'guest, stranger, ... lodging', which is also a comparandum for #5531.Note that the graph 膂 (GSR #77e), Mand. lǚ, is essentially the same etymon as 旅, with the same OC reconstruction.
<par><hanform>鼻</hanform> OC <latinform>*bʼi̯əd</latinform>, GSR #521c 'nose'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*m-bit-s {m-[b]i[t]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bí</latinform>.</par>
<par>For phonosemantically similar roots, see <xref ref="1421">#1421</xref> and <xref ref="5756">#5756</xref>.</par><par>Cf. also <xref ref="2130">#2130</xref>.</par>
<par>Further cognate identifications are to be expected, as witness the non-Kiranti Magar form.</par>

<par><hanform>般</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’wɑ̂n</latinform>, GSR #182a 'turn round'; Coblin 86:153 ST <latinform>*pran</latinform> > OC <latinform>*pran</latinform> ~ <latinform>*ban</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:409 <latinform>*bân</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>pán</latinform>.</par>

能 OC *nəg / nəng, GSR #885a 'be able'; B & S 2011: *nˁə {nˁə(ʔ)}~ nˁəŋ; Mand. néng.
態 OC *t’nəg, GSR #885f 'bearing, manner'; Schuessler 2007:398 *nhə̂h; B & S 2011: *n̥ˁə-s; Mand. tài.
<par>Further confirmation of this root would be welcome.</par>
<par>This root might be allofamically related to <xref ref="1723">#1723</xref>.</par>
<par>This sparsely attested root requires further confirmation.</par>

<par>The Bodic and Magar forms resemble the second syllable of WT <latinform>mig-h̥bras</latinform> 'eyeball' (lit. "eye-fruit"). For a semantically similar compound, cf. Sema <latinform>anhe thi</latinform> 'eyeball'.</par>

<par>The vocalism of the Tujia reflexes varies from <latinform>-o</latinform> to <latinform>-a</latinform>, leading to the hypothesis that the Proto-Tujia form was something like <latinform>*lwa</latinform>. Several Northern Tujia forms actually have the vocalism <latinform>-uo</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root (originally proposed by Weidert 1987:477) is distinct from <xref ref="1783">#1783</xref>. Several languages have reflexes from both roots, e.g. Mizo <latinform>luak</latinform> (<xref ref="1783">#1783</xref>) and <latinform>lùi</latinform> from the present root. Some Tamangic languages have apparently developed a <latinform>-t</latinform> suffix. </par>
<par>This etymon bears some resemblance to <xref ref="2615">#2615</xref>, but should be kept distinct.</par>

剌 OC *lɑ̂t, GSR #272a 'wicked; cut, hurt'; Schuessler 2007:342 *rât; B & S 2011: *rˁat (~*C.rˁat ?); Mand. là.
割 OC *kɑ̂t, GSR #314d-e 'cut, harm'; Schuessler 2007:252 *kât; B & S 2011: *Cə-kˁat; Mand. gē.
害 OC *g’ɑ̂d, GSR #314a-b 'injure; harm'; Schuessler 2007:252 *gâts; B & S 2011: *m-kˁat-s; Mand. hài.
犗 OC *kɑd, GSR #314c 'castrate'; Schuessler 2007:252 *krâts; B & S 2011: *kˁrat-s; Mand. jiè.
<par>The Burmish languages show hesitation in the position of articulation of the final nasal. This root requires further confirmation.</par>

<par>Jingpho illustrates both the <latinform>*s-</latinform> and the <latinform>*ʔ-</latinform> prefix, which motivates our unusual PTB reconstruction with either prefix.</par><par>There is a possible allofamic relationship between this root and <xref ref="2804">#2804</xref>. If this is true, it would be another example of the unusual alternation between medial <latinform>*-u-</latinform> and <latinform>*-a-</latinform> to be found in <xref ref="1417">#1417</xref>.</par>

<par>Several Bodo-Garo forms begin with a syllable <latinform>bi(ŋ)</latinform>, which superficially resembles this root. However, R. Burling points out that this is merely a prefix which occurs with many body part terms, e.g. Garo <latinform>bi-bik</latinform> 'intestines', <latinform>bi-bil</latinform> 'afterbirth', <latinform>bi-gil</latinform> 'skin; bark; peel; pod; leather', <latinform>bi-gron</latinform> 'testicle', <latinform>bi-ka</latinform> 'liver', <latinform>bi-kit</latinform> 'gall bladder', <latinform>bi-mang</latinform> 'body of person or thing; shape, form; middle portion of an object, such as a glass', <latinform>bi-rot</latinform> 'pimple, boil, pox'; also Meche <latinform>biŋgur</latinform>, <latinform>bigur</latinform> 'skin'. See Burling 2003.</par><par>The first syllables of Tshona (Mama) <latinform><emph>phe⁵⁵</emph> khu⁵³</latinform> 'skin' and WT <latinform><emph>pʻyi</emph> srin</latinform> 'vermin living on skin' also superficially resemble this etymon, but are really a different morpheme meaning 'outside'. See Jäschke (1881:348-50). Thus, 'vermin living on skin' means lit. "outside vermin".</par><par>A few Karenic forms (Pa-O <latinform> ʼbeŋ¹</latinform>,  Pho (Delta) <latinform>ɓẽ⁴</latinform>, Sgaw <latinform>ɓẽ⁴</latinform> 'skin, bark') point to a final nasal, but these could well reflect a different etymon. The morphemic identity of the second syllables of Karen (Sgaw/Hinthada) <latinform>a³¹ <emph>be̱³¹</emph></latinform> and <latinform>pʰi⁵⁵ <emph>be̱³¹</emph></latinform> 'skin' remains obscure.</par>

<par>This root so far seems to be confined to Qiangic (including Tangut). The <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix is reconstructed on the basis of the voiceless liquids in Ergong, NW rGyalrong, and (Sofronov's) Tangut. The aspirated dental stops in Ergong and NW rGyalrong appear to be secondary "extrusions" of the previous liquids. There is no apparent connection with <xref ref="3458">#3458</xref>, which always means BREAST, not MILK.</par>
<par>The allofam with prefixal <latinform>*s-</latinform> is posited on the basis of the Eastern Kiranti forms with initial <latinform>h-</latinform>.</par>
<par>A number of languages (Tani, Tibetan, Lepcha, Lahu) show preemption of the initial by the dental prefix.</par><par>Jingpho has acquired the homorganic stop in this root.</par>

<par><hanform>熊</hanform> OC <latinform>*d-wɑm</latinform>, GSR #674a-b ‘bear’; Coblin 86:40 ST <latinform>*dɣwjəm</latinform> > OC <latinform>*gwjəm</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:542 <latinform>*wəm</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*C.ɢʷəm {C.[ɢ]ʷ(r)əm}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xióng</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is confined to a few languages of the Deng group. All three nasals are attested in final position, motivating the reconstruction with a neutral capital <latinform>*-N</latinform>. The variation between medial <latinform>-i-</latinform> and <latinform>-u-</latinform> is a well-established TB pattern.</par>

<par>The Meluri, Konyak, and Mao forms end in <latinform>-i</latinform>. It is still unclear whether they belong with the other forms which have a high back vowel.  The final <latinform>n</latinform> in the Ntenyi forms is also unexplained.</par>
<par>Burmish forms have back vowels, while Loloish reflects <latinform>*-a-</latinform>.</par>

<par>There is a well-established allofam with a homorganic final nasal <latinform>m-glaŋ</latinform>, see HPTB:325. This nasal-final allofam has also acquired a nasal prefix in Lolo-Burmese: cf. WT <latinform>graŋ-ba</latinform>, Trung <latinform>glaŋ</latinform>, Lahu <latinform>gɔ̀</latinform>.</par>

涼 OC *gli̯ɑng, GSR #755l 'chilly'; Schuessler 2007:355; B & S 2011: *C.raŋ; Mand. liáng 'cool, chilly', liàng 'to cool, chill'.
霜 OC *ṣi̯ɑng, GSR #731g 'frost'; Schuessler 2007:355; B & S 2011: *sraŋ {[s]raŋ}; Mand. shuāng.
<par>STC cites several Chinese comparanda, including GSR 766q, 766g, 813a-f, 813g, and 789a.</par>

赫 OC *χɑ̆k, GSR #779a 'red, fiery; brilliant; majestic, awe-inspiring; frighten'; Schuessler 2007:276 *hrâk; B & S 2011: *qʰˁrak; Mand. hè.
嚇 OC *χɑ̆k, GSR #779b 'frighten'; Schuessler 2007:276 *hrâk; B & S 2011: *qʰˁ‹r›ak qʰˁ‹r›ak-s; Mand. hè.
嚇 OC *χɑ̆g, GSR #779b 'frighten'; Schuessler 2007:276 *hrâkh; B & S 2011: *qʰˁ‹r›ak-s; Mand. xià.
虩 OC *χi̯ɑ̆k, GSR #787d-f 'frightening'; Schuessler 2007:276 *hak; Mand. xì.
覤 OC *χi̯ɑ̆k, GSR #789a 'fear'; Schuessler 2007:276 *hak; Mand. xì.
恪 OC *k’lɑ̂k, GSR #766g 'respect; reverent'; Coblin 86:78 ST *khlak > OC *khlak; Schuessler 2007:276 (*khrâk); B & S 2011: (*kʰˁrak); Mand. kè.
<par>The ordinary WB word for TEN is <latinform>chay</latinform>; <latinform>kyip</latinform> is substituted "in the numbering of rational beings" (Judson 1893:215).</par>

<par><hanform>十</hanform> OC <latinform>*d̂i̯əp</latinform>, GSR #686a-d 'ten'; Coblin 86:147 ST <latinform>*gr̵ip</latinform> > PC <latinform>*grjəp</latinform> > OC <latinform>*djəp</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:462 <latinform>*gip</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*t.ɡəp {t.[ɡ]əp}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shí</latinform>.</par>
<par>The initial element is evidently the "animal prefix", ult. < PTB <latinform>*sya</latinform>.</par>

兄 OC *χi̯wɑ̆ng, GSR #765a-e 'eldest (of brothers)'; B & S 2011: *m̥raŋ; Mand. xiōng.
孟 OC *mɑ̆ng, GSR #761e 'eldest, great-'; B & S 2011: *mˤraŋ-s; Mand. mèng.
S375 mang huang
<par>A borrowing from Mandarin <hanform>騾子</hanform> <latinform>luózi</latinform> 'mule'.</par>

往 OC *gi̯wɑng, GSR #739k 'go to; gone, past'; Coblin 86:86 ST *ɣwjang > OC *gwjangx; Schuessler 2007:508 *waŋʔ; B & S 2011: *ɢʷaŋʔ; Mand. wǎng.
迋 OC *gi̯wɑng, GSR #739l 'go'; Schuessler 2007:508 *waŋh; B & S 2011: *ɢʷaŋʔ-s {[ɢ]ʷaŋʔ-s}; Mand. wàng.

<par>Several languages (Sani, Nocte, some Tibetan dialects) have a dental stop instead of the lateral. Written Tibetan has a doublet <latinform>ldag</latinform> 'lick' ⪤ <latinform>ldz̀ags</latinform> 'tongue, lick'.</par>

舐 OC *gʼi̯ɑk ~ g’i̯ok, GSR #803h 'tongue'; Coblin 86:102 *; Schuessler 2007: *; B & S 2011: *Cə.leʔ; Mand. shì.
食 OC *d̑i̯ək, GSR #921a-c 'eat'; Schuessler 2007:463 *m-lək; B & S 2011: *mə-lək; Mand. shí.
食/飼 OC *dzi̯əg, GSR #921a-c 'food; give food to; raise, nourish'; Schuessler 2007:463 *s-ləkh; B & S 2011: *s-m-lək-s; Mand. sì.

<par>This etymon is not reconstructed in STC, but appears in VSTB pp. 225-6 and Matisoff 1983<footnote>"Translucent insights: a look at Proto-Sino-Tibetan through Gordon H. Luce's <emph>Comparative Wordlist</emph>." BSOAS 46.3:462-76.</footnote> #52.</par>

<par>This root occurs in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, and perhaps Lolo-Burmese (Ugong).  There are possible Chinese comparanda (below). WT shows variation between <latinform>-r-</latinform> and <latinform>-l-</latinform>. The <latinform>b-</latinform> has been treated as a prefix and dropped everywhere but in WT and Chinese. For a similar doubly prefixed form set up for PTB, cf. <reconstruction>*s-b-rul</reconstruction> SNAKE. WT <latinform>hlums</latinform> reflects an alternant <reconstruction>*s-lum</reconstruction>. This <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix seems to have preempted the initial consonant in Tsangla <latinform>shum</latinform>, <latinform>wak◦tsa ɕum</latinform> 'placenta'.</par><par>We now consider this etymon to be independent of <xref ref="680">#680</xref>, even though they were treated as allofams in VSTB and Matisoff 1983.</par>

<par>Luce 1981 ("A Comparative Word-List of Old Burmese, Chinese, and Tibetan") compares this root with <hanform>範</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*b'i̯wɑ̆m</reconstruction>, Mand. <latinform>fàn</latinform>, glossed by him as 'mould, matrix'. See Matisoff 1983: #52 ("Transclucent insights"). For the semantic connection, cf. Latin and English <emph>matrix</emph> 'womb; a situation or surrounding substance in which something originates', ult. < <emph>mater</emph> 'mother'.</par>

範 OC *bʼi̯wɑ̆m, GSR #626d ‘mould; rule, law’; Li 1971: *bjamx; Baxter 1992: (*bjamʔ / *b(r)jomʔ); Mand. fàn.Baxter does not reconstruct this word. There are several possible reconstructions in his system, as indicated above. The Chinese word 範 may be related to 凡 'general rule, pattern', Mand. fán and 法 'law, model', Mand. fǎ, reconstructed *b(r)jom and *pjap respectively by Baxter.
[ZJH]

妊 OC *ńi̯əm, GSR #667i ‘pregnant’; Li 1971: *njəmh; Baxter 1992: *njɨms; Mand. rèn.Gong (1995 #368) proposed that this Chinese word was cognate to WT sbrum < *smrum. However, the vowel and initial correspondences are not regular, and the comparison is not found in Gong's later publications. Schuessler 2007:441 treats 妊 rèn ‘pregnant’ as an internal Chinese derivation from 任 rèn ‘carry on the shoulder, to load’.
[ZJH]

<par>This root, which has allofams both with and without a final velar stop, is found in several different subgroups of TB: NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Asakian, Qiangic (including Xixia), and Baic. It has so far not been discovered in Lolo-Burmese or Karenic.</par><par>This root resembles <xref ref="3423">#3423</xref>. The possibility that these two etyma might be one and the same is reinforced by the natural tendency of /<latinform>t</latinform>/ to become affricated before /<latinform>u</latinform>/, as witnessed by Japanese, where /<latinform>tu</latinform>/ is realized as [tsɯ].</par><par>This etymon also bears a perhaps accidental resemblance to <xref ref="661">#661</xref>.</par><par>An apparently prefixal <latinform>k-</latinform> is attested in Kham and Spiti. Cf. also the Tangut binome <latinform>khǐu̱ thu</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root occurs in several Loloish languages, but has yet to be unambiguously attested elsewhere.</par>
<par>This root bears some resemblance to <xref ref="2614">#2614</xref> but appears to be independent of the latter.</par>
<par>VanBik feels that this root is independent of <xref ref="4285">#4285</xref>. See also the complex PAF <xref ref="273">#273</xref>.</par>
<par>This root has a strong phonosemantic similarity to <xref ref="5620">#5620</xref>, but separate roots are undoubtedly involved.</par>

<par><hanform>撮</hanform> OC <latinform>*ts’wɑ̂t</latinform>, GSR #325e 'pinch/pick up with fingers; collect, gather'; Schuessler 2007:200 <latinform>*tshôt</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tsʰˁot {[tsʰ]ˁot}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>cuō</latinform>.</par>
<par>This poorly attested root awaits further confirmation.</par>
<par><hanform>禿</hanform> OC <latinform>*tʼuk</latinform>, GSR #1205a 'bald'; Mand. <latinform>tū</latinform>.</par>

<par>The Nusu voiceless nasals point to an <latinform>ʔ-</latinform> prefix at the PLB stage. The Lahu mid tone is also compatible with the glottal prefix.</par><par>It has been suggested that there is a connection between this root and <xref ref="2499">#2499</xref>, since lean meat has a red appearance due to the lack of fat marbling, but this remains to be seen.</par>
<par>This etymon seems to partially overlap with <xref ref="411">#411</xref>, and should perhaps be merged with it in the future.</par>
<par>This etymon seems valid, but further confirmation would be welcome.</par>

<par>This root probably stands in an allofamic relationship with <xref ref="264">#264</xref>. The latter etymon is reconstructed with <latinform>*-y-</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*-r-</latinform>, so that three different glides would be represented in a single word family.</par>
<par>This root is very well established in Qiangic and Loloish, with several languages having transitive/intransitive or simplex/causative doublets, e.g. Lalo, Nusu (Bijiang).</par>
<par>This root seems quite separate from <xref ref="2449">#2449</xref>. It is attested mostly in Central Naga, with scattered support elsewhere.</par>
<par>The first syllable is <xref ref="7621">#7621</xref>.</par>

<par>This root exemplifies several typical TB allofamic patterns: variation in initial voicing, affricate/spirant alternation, and alternate suffixations.</par><par>The notion of URINATE is often conveyed by an OV cognate object construction, lit. "to urinate urine": e.g. Jingpho <latinform>jìt jí</latinform>, Nung <latinform>tsi tsi</latinform>.</par>

䞓 OC *t’i̯ĕng; GSR #831x 'red'; Schuessler 2007:184 *hreŋ; B & S 2011: *t-kʰreŋ; Mand. chēng.
赬 OC *t’i̯ĕng, GSR #834m 'red'; Coblin 86:123 ST *skhryeng > OC *skhrjing; Schuessler 2007:184 *hreŋ; Mand. chēng.Comment: The separate graphs 䞓 chēng and 赬 chēng represent essentially the same etymon in Chinese.

瀼 OC *ńi̯ɑng, GSR #730f 'heavy with dew'; Coblin 86:62 ST *(C)njang > OC *njang; Mand. ráng.
穰 OC *ńi̯ɑng, GSR #730h 'rich growth (of grain), abundant'; Mand. ráng.
濃 OC *ni̯ung ~ nung, GSR #1005i 'thick, rich (e.g. of dew)'; Schuessler 2007:404 *nroŋ ~ nôŋ; B & S 2011: (*nˁuŋ); Mand. nóng.
醲 OC *ni̯ung ~ nung, GSR #1005j 'thick, strong (e.g. of drink)'; Schuessler 2007:404 *nroŋ ~ nôŋ; B & S 2011: (*nˁuŋ); Mand. nóng.

<par>Several Loloish forms (e.g. Lahu Lancang <latinform>nɛ⁵⁴</latinform> 'thick (porridge)', Black Lahu <latinform>nê ~ né</latinform> 'chewy, elastic, viscous', Lisu <latinform>e⁴⁴nɯ³⁵</latinform> 'turbid / muddy', Lisu <latinform>e⁵⁵na̱³³kha³⁵</latinform> 'thick (porridge)') might be reflexes of this root, although the vowel correspondences seem quite irregular.</par>

<par>This root was reconstructed as the binome <reconstruction>*u-p(h)rok</reconstruction> for "Proto-Kuki-Naga" in STC p. 41. The putative Thakali cognate makes it possible to reconstruct the second syllable for PTB. For the first syllable, see the PKC root <xref ref="4487">#4487</xref>.</par>

<par>Some languages point to a voiceless initial with this root, although most forms descend from a *voiced prototype. Both an <latinform>-n</latinform> and a <latinform>-t</latinform> suffix are attested with this root: cf. #4165, and form II of this verb in several Chin languages, e.g. Tiddim <latinform>paːt</latinform> BE THIN. See HPTB: 440-441.</par><par>The basic allofam of this etymon appears to be <xref ref="5551">#5551</xref>, with an open rhyme.</par>

<par>There is evidently an allofam with final velar, reflected by WT <latinform>nyag-mo</latinform> 'woman' and Chinese <hanform>孃</hanform> / <hanform>娘</hanform> 'lady, woman, mother', OC <latinform>ni̯ang</latinform> (not in GSR #730); AD 541 reconstructs <latinform>ni̯ang</latinform> for MC.</par><par>HPTB:173 errs in relating Chinese <hanform>女</hanform> both to the present root and to <xref ref="1623">#1623</xref>. In addition, a wrong GSR citation is given under <reconstruction>*nya</reconstruction>.</par><par>In any case, there seems to be considerable overlap among this root, <xref ref="1623">#1623</xref>, and <xref ref="1621">#1621</xref>, and further work will be needed to clarify their interrelationship.</par>

<par><hanform>女</hanform> OC <latinform>*ni̯o</latinform>, GSR #94a-e 'woman, lady, girl'; Coblin 86:161 ST <latinform>*nr̵yak</latinform> > OC <latinform>*nrjakx</latinform>; B & S 2011 <latinform>*nraʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>nǚ</latinform>.</par>
<par>More confirmation is required.</par>

<par>This reconstruction replaces <latinform>*s-not ⪤ *s-nut</latinform> (HPTB:381) and <latinform>*s-not</latinform> (STC:144-145, 150).</par><par>The variational pattern adduced here between medial <latinform>*-wa-</latinform> and <latinform>*-u-</latinform> parallels the better known alternation between <latinform>*-ya-</latinform> and <latinform>*-i-</latinform> to be found in etyma like <xref ref="33">#33</xref>. We cannot assume a single prototype <latinform>*s-nwat</latinform>, since that would have yielded WB <latinform>hnwat</latinform>.</par><par>This etymon is widely distributed with the meaning MOUTH in Burmish and Karenic, with good-looking cognates in Qiangic (Pumi) and Bai. The Tibetan cognate gives a clue to its more general meaning of VESSEL; the expression <latinform>snod drug</latinform> 'the six vessels' refers in traditional Tibetan anatomy to the gall-bladder, stomach, small and large intestines, urinary bladder, and uterus (in females) or spermatic vessels (in males) (see Jäschke 1881/1958, p. 319). The Tibetan compound <latinform>bu-snod</latinform>, literally "child-vessel", refers specifically to the womb. See STC (pp. 144, 145, 150) for references to the Burmese, Karen, and Tibetan cognates.</par><par>The Burmish (see especially Lashi) forms bear a strong surface resemblance to Siamese <latinform>nùat</latinform> (< Proto-Tai <latinform>*hn‑</latinform>) ‘beard’, suggesting that this is a loan from Burmish into SW Tai. This word is not widely distributed in Tai. “Except Tay (a Central Tai dialect) nuôt D1L this word seems restricted to the SW dialects” (Li Fang Kuei 1977:116). The Northern and Central Tai dialects generally have forms descending from Proto-Tai <latinform>*mum</latinform> (HCT pp. 72-73), which bears an apparently coincidental resemblance to <xref ref="2058">#2058</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>須</hanform> / <hanform>鬚</hanform> OC <latinform>*si̯u</latinform>, GSR #133a-c, d  'beard'; Schuessler 2007:544 <latinform>*sno</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s.no </latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xū</latinform>.</par>
<par>See the discussion in WTF p. 545 under SHIN, which is not very convincing. For a discussion of the Karen forms, see the note under Chapter 4.3.</par>
<par>See also <xref ref="3393">#3393</xref> and <xref ref="3409">#3409</xref>.</par>

夕 OC *dzi̯ɑ̆k, GSR #796a-d 'night'; Coblin 86:112-3 ST *rjiak > OC *rjiak; Schuessler 2007:522 *s-jak; B & S 2011: *s-N-rak {s-N-rAk}; Mand. xī.
昔 OC *si̯ɑ̆k, GSR #798a-e 'long ago'; Schuessler 2007:523 *sak or *sjak; B & S 2011: *sak {[s]Ak}; Mand. xī.

<par>For detailed discussion of the numeral FOUR, see Matisoff 1995 ("Numerals"), pp. 183-186.</par><par>The Chinese cognate has been disguised by a prefixal substitution of <latinform>*b-</latinform> to <latinform>*s-</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>四</hanform> OC <latinform>*si̯əd</latinform>, GSR #518a-d 'four'; Coblin 86:83 ST <latinform>*bƚyid</latinform> > OC <latinform>*ljidh</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:478 <latinform>*sis</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s.lij-s {s.li[j]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>sì</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root bears a strong phonosemantic resemblance to <xref ref="5685">#5685</xref>, and perhaps should be considered part of the same etymon.</par>
<par>This root is well-distributed, occurring in Bodo-Garo, Kuki-Chin, Kiranti, and Chinese.</par>

白 OC *bɑ̆k, GSR #782a ‘white’; Schuessler 2007:153-4 *brâk; B & S 2011: *bˁrak; Mand. bái.
魄 OC *p’ɑ̆k, GSR #782o 'animal soul of man; phase of the moon'; Schuessler 2007:417 *phrâk; B & S 2011: *pʰˁrak; Mand. pò.
<par>This root is well attested in Karenic and Baic, but apparently not elsewhere.</par>
<par>This root displays the same <latinform>-a- ⪤ -u-</latinform> alternation that is found in a few other roots; see <xref ref="1443">#1443</xref> and <xref ref="1417">#1417</xref>.</par>
<par>This etymon was formerly reconstructed as <latinform>*m-kret</latinform> (HPTB:375).</par>

契 OC *k’iɑd, GSR #279b 'notch, engrave'; Coblin 86:129 ST *kret > OC *kriat; Schuessler 2007: *khêts; B & S 2011: *kʰˁet-s {[kʰ]ˁet-s}; Mand. qì.
鍥 OC *k’iɑt, GSR #279f 'cut, cut through'; Schuessler 2007:129 *khêt; Mand. qiè.
刮 OC *kwɑt, GSR #302n 'scrape'; Schuessler 2007:264 *krôt; B & S 2011: *kʷˁrat {[kʷˁ]r[a]t}; Mand. guā.
<par>Sanskrit <latinform>khura</latinform>, Nepali <latinform>khur</latinform> 'hoof'</par>
<par>This etymon bears a close resemblance to <xref ref="632">#632</xref>, as does <xref ref="688">#688</xref>.</par>
<par>re-transcribe as <latinform>*m-gywa</latinform> or <latinform>*m-gʷya</latinform> so as not to violate HPTB: 82 [fn.121] ?</par>

<par>The PKC etymon <latinform>*kal</latinform> (a meso-root of <xref ref="201">#201</xref>) definitely seems related to this root, perhaps pointing to a PKC variant <latinform>*k(y)al</latinform>.</par>
<par>This Lai - Burmese comparison is due to Kenneth VanBik.</par>

<par><hanform>兕</hanform> OC <latinform>*dzi̯ər</latinform>, GSR #556a 'water buffalo; rhinoceros'; Schuessler 2007:479 <latinform>*s-jəiʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s.ɢijʔ {[s.ɢ]ijʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>sì</latinform>.</par>
<par>The two prefixes in this reconstruction are attested in Qiangic/rGyalrongic.</par><par>For a discussion of the "extrusional" <latinform>-ʷ-</latinform> in this root, see Matisoff 2000:22.</par>

<par><hanform>補</hanform> OC <latinform>*pwo</latinform>, GSR #102c' 'supplement, mend, repair'; Schuessler 2007:172 <latinform>*pâʔ</latinform> or <latinform>*mpâʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*Cə-pˁaʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bǔ</latinform>.</par>

<par>This sparsely attested root has a possible Chinese comparandum <hanform>肩</hanform> (GSR #240a <reconstruction>*kiɑn</reconstruction> 'shoulder, carry').</par><par>See also <xref ref="3409">#3409</xref> and <xref ref="3346">#3346</xref>.</par>
<par><hanform>肩</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*kiɑn</reconstruction>, GSR #240a 'shoulder, carry'; B & S 2011: <latinform>kˤen {[k]ˤe[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>jiān</latinform>.</par>

<par>Jingpho has a doublet <latinform>khái</latinform> and <latinform>gat</latinform>, with the latter showing a suffix <latinform>-t</latinform>. The same suffixal element occurs in Nocte <latinform>khet</latinform> and <latinform>a khiet</latinform>.</par>

<par>We posit a PTB etymon <latinform>*phya(k)</latinform> 'open; release' based on evidence mostly from Himalayish languages. An allofamic relationship with <xref ref="5616">#5616</xref> is likely; at the same time, <latinform>*phya(k)</latinform> and <latinform>*pryak</latinform> differ in their semantic focus as well as their areal distribution within Tibeto-Burman. Perhaps the two etyma might be treated as mesoroots of an PTB super-etymon, as yet unproposed.</par><par>Good Chinese comparanda exist for both <latinform>*phyak</latinform> and <latinform>*pryak</latinform>; these comparanda can themselves be considered allofamic within Chinese.</par>

<par><hanform>闢</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’i̯ĕk</latinform>, GSR #853k 'open; open up'; Coblin 86:114 ST <latinform>*byeʔ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*bjik</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:414 <latinform>*bek</latinform> < <latinform>*bai-k</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*bˁek</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>pì</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon, with final dental nasal, seems so far to be relatively rare; yet its occurrence in widely separated languages like Jingpho, Meithei, and Chepang require us to set it up for PTB. Like <xref ref="3589">#3589</xref>, <xref ref="686">#686</xref>, <xref ref="1349">#1349</xref>, the semantic range of the present etymon includes both VAGINA and MOUTH/LIP; but all of these etyma, despite their phonosemantic similarity, may well be independent.</par>
