<par>This etymon is usually used when the exact borrowed item is to be returned. Contrast <xref ref="2314">#2314</xref>, where only an equivalent (like money) is to be returned.</par>

垂 OC *d̑wiɑ, GSR #31a 'hang down'; Schuessler 2007:196-7 *doi < *djoi; B & S 2011: *doj {[d]oj}; Mand. chuí.
睡 OC *d̂wiɑ, GSR #31d 'lie down, sleep'; Schuessler 2007:475 *doih~*djoih; Mand. shuì.
朵 OC *twɑ̂, GSR #10a 'hang from a tree; CL for blossoms'; Schuessler 2007:221 *tôi; Mand. duǒ.橢 OC *t’wɑ̂, GSR #11c 'long and narrow; oval, ellipse'; Schuessler 2007:505 *lhôiʔ; B & S 2011: *l̥ˁojʔ; Mand. tuǒ.
惰 OC *d’wɑ̂, GSR #11l 'lazy, negligent, careless, useless'; Schuessler 2007: (*lôiʔ); B & S 2011: *lˁojʔ; Mand. duò.緌 OC *ńi̯wər, GSR #354e 'tassel'; Schuessler 2007:447 *nui; B & S 2011: *nuj; Mand. ruí.
惢/蕊 OC *ńźwie̯:~*ńźwi:, GSR #1238k 'ovary of flower; unopened flower; fruit'; Schuessler 2007:447 *noiʔ~*nuiʔ; B & S 2011: (*nujʔ); Mand. ruǐ.
繠 OC *ńźwie̯:~*ńźwi:, GSR #1238j 'hang down'; Schuessler 2007:447 *noiʔ~*nuiʔ; B & S 2011: (*nujʔ); Mand. ruǐ.
<par>This root displays an unusual type of variation in initial consonant.</par>
<par>The final labial in Bunan seems to be due to assimilation to the initial.</par><par>Cf. also <xref ref="810">#810</xref>.</par>
<par><xref ref="810">#810</xref></par>
<par><hanform>噬</hanform> OC <latinform>*d̑i̯ɑd</latinform>, GSR #336c; B & S 2011: <latinform>*det-s {[d]e[t]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shì</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root is so far sparsely attested, with support only from Lahu and Chinese. The voiced Lahu initial points to a PLB prenasalized consonant <reconstruction>*mb-</reconstruction>.</par>

<par><hanform>剥</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*pŭk</reconstruction>, GSR #1228a 'cut, flay, peel'; WHB-OC 544; Schuessler 157; Li: <reconstruction>*pruk</reconstruction>; Baxter: <reconstruction>*prok</reconstruction> > Mand. <latinform>bō ~ bāo</latinform>.</par><par>The poor vowel correspondence, and the lack of a directly attested <reconstruction>*-r-</reconstruction> on the TB side,<footnote>Although -<latinform>r</latinform>- would leave no trace in Lahu here.</footnote> cast doubt on the proposed cognate, but more TB support for the root would be necessary to reconstruct a reliable PTB root and fully evaluate the connection.</par><par>Schuessler cites Manomaivibool 1975:120 and relates the Chinese word to Thai <latinform>pɔɔk<sup>D1</sup></latinform> 'to peel' and Thai <latinform>lɔɔk<sup>D2</sup></latinform> 'to skin, peel' <latinform>⪤ plɔɔk<sup>D1</sup></latinform> 'encasement, slipcover, sheath' < PTai <reconstruction>*dlɔɔk</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>This etymon is so far attested only in Tangkhulic and Burmish (Bola, Maru).</par>
<par>This etymon is now considered be a separate etymon from the phonologically similar root <xref ref="670">#670</xref>. See VSTB pp. 224-227; TBRS #97; HPTB p. 250.</par>
<par>This etymon is evidently valid, but more confirmation would be welcome.</par>

<par><hanform>身</hanform> OC <latinform>*śi̯ĕn</latinform>, GSR #386a-c; Mand. <latinform>shēn</latinform>.</par><par>Unclear whether Chinese <hanform>身</hanform> is to be considered a comparandum for <latinform>both</latinform> the current etymon <xref ref="318">#318</xref> <latinform>and</latinform> <xref ref="34">#34</xref>. If so, this would support the allofamic relationship between the two etyma argued for in <xref ref="5711">#5711</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>五</hanform> OC <latinform>*ngo</latinform>, GSR #58a-d 'five'; Coblin 86:80 ST <latinform>*lngaɣ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*ngagx</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:519 <latinform>*ŋâʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011 <latinform>*C.ŋˁaʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>wǔ</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon seems solid enough, though it has a rather strange distribution, occurring in scattered languages of the Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese (including Jinuo), and Asakian groups, as well as in the unclassified Tujia. There is not yet enough evidence to decide whether to reconstruct either a *voiced or *voiceless initial, or both *voiced and *voiceless allofams.</par><par>This root looks as if it might stand in an allofamic relationship with <xref ref="641">#641</xref>. Note, however, that the reflexes of <xref ref="641">#641 </xref> and the present root are quite different in Nesu and Sani.</par>
<par><hanform>產</hanform> OC <latinform>*sɑ̆n</latinform>, GSR #194a; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s-ŋrarʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>chǎn</latinform>.</par>

<par>This reconstruction is due to Coblin (1986:40) and Gong (2001:29). Li Fang Kuei's OC reconstruction <latinform>*srianx</latinform> (cited in Coblin) looks closer to the PTB reconstruction than Karlgren's. See HPTB: 422, 425.</par>

<par>This widespread etymon is attested all over TB (NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese, Qiangic). It comprises both a simplex and a causative variant, i.e. DRINK (with underlying nasal prefix) vs. GIVE TO DRINK (with underlying <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix), and by extension SMOKE (tobacco) vs. GIVE TO SMOKE. (Cf. Japanese <emph>tabako wo nomu</emph> 'smoke ("drink") tobacco'.)</par><par>The nasal prefix is clearly reflected in Himalayish (Baima, Amdo Tibetan), Qiangic (Namuyi, Tangut), and Loloish (e.g. Yi Dafang, Luquan, Nasu, Xide), sometimes with an actual nasal segment, sometimes more indirectly (as in Lahu <latinform>dɔ̀</latinform>, where the voiced initial is a reliable reflex of PLB *prenasalization).</par>
<par>There is a phonologically excellent Chinese comparandum with the meaning MILK: <hanform>湩</hanform> [GSR 1188c]. See below.</par>

湩 OC *ti̯ung/*tung, GSR #1188c ‘milk’; Li 1971: *tjungh/*tungh; Baxter 1992: *tjongs/*tongs; Mand. dòng ~ zhòng.The correspondences are a perfect match. OC *u (Li)/*o (Baxter) regularly corresponds to PTB *u before velar codas.However, there is a competing etymology suggesting that this word is an early borrowing from a Central Asian language (Pulleyblank 1962:250ff). This etymology is supported by the fact that this is not the ordinary Chinese word for 'milk'; early glosses define it as 'milk (of cows and mares)'. The Chinese themselves did not drink such milk, so it would not be surprising if this word were borrowed from nomadic peoples.
[ZJH]
<par>Loloish has a simplex/causative alternation in this root: cf. Lahu <latinform>dɔ̀</latinform> 'drink' ⪤ <plainlatinform>tɔ</plainlatinform> 'give to drink'.</par>
<par>Care must be taken to distinguish this root from <xref ref="227">#227</xref>.</par>

<par>Jingpho <latinform>yak</latinform> 'difficult' is evidently a borrowing from Tai (cf. Proto-Tai <latinform>*jaak</latinform>), since PTB <latinform>*-k</latinform> regularly becomes Jingpho <latinform>-ʔ</latinform>.</par>

阸/阨 OC *ʔěk ~ *ʔěg, GSR #844g-h 'defile, narrow pass; straits, difficulties'; Schuessler 2007:223 *rêkh ~ *ʔêkh; Mand. è ~ ài.
隘 OC *ʔěk ~ *ʔěg, GSR #849h ‘defile, narrow pass; in straits, distress’; B & S 2011: *qˁ‹r›ik {qˁ‹r›[i]k}; Mand. è ~ ài.
<par>See Matisoff 1983 ("Translucent insights") #70.</par>
<par>Cf. also <xref ref="1470">#1470</xref>.</par>
<par>This etymon is the eponym for Burling's "Sal" grouping, a putative TB subgroup comprising Jingpho, Barish, and Northern Naga. See Burling 1983.</par>
<par>This root seems to be a variant of <xref ref="7709">#7709</xref>. See also the resemblant root <xref ref="1643">#1643</xref>.</par>

<par>Note the superficial similarity between this genuine TB etymon<reconstruction>*g-ryaŋ</reconstruction> and the Nepali word <latinform>karang</latinform> 'rib'. While Dimasa <latinform>sang-khreng</latinform> 'rib' and Lalung <latinform>krengʔ</latinform> 'bone' are descended from <reconstruction>*g-ryaŋ</reconstruction>, Chantyal <latinform>kərəŋ</latinform> and Yakha <latinform>karaŋ</latinform> are borrowings from Nepali.</par><par>The Karbi forms show preemption by the velar prefix.</par><par>The Miji and Darang forms reflect an <latinform>s-</latinform> prefix.</par>

<par>The position of articulation of the final nasal in this etymon is difficult to reconstruct based on the Rongmei, Tangkhul, and Khoirao reflexes; but perhaps the Sangkong form can settle the matter in favor of <reconstruction>*-ŋ</reconstruction>. For now, we symbolize the uncertainty by using an <latinform>N</latinform>.</par>
<par>Several of these Tani forms refer specifically to cattle rather than to animals in general. It is thus possible that they descend from a distinct root.</par>

<par><hanform>身</hanform> OC <latinform>*śi̯ĕn</latinform>, GSR #386a-c; Mand. <latinform>shēn</latinform>.</par><par>Unclear whether Chinese <hanform>身</hanform> is to be considered a comparandum for <latinform>both</latinform> the current etymon <xref ref="34">#34</xref> <latinform>and</latinform> <xref ref="318">#318</xref>. If so, this would support the allofamic relationship between the two etyma argued for in <xref ref="5711">#5711</xref>.</par>

<par>A simplex-causative pair is involved here. Reflexes with voiceless laterals are to be assigned to the causative allofam with the <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix, as are undoubtedly a number of other forms (e.g. in Loloish) recognizable by their special tones.</par><par>The allofam with medial <latinform>-i-</latinform> is confined to Bodo-Garo, Nungish, and Northern Naga.</par><par>Various scattered languages (Darang, Yidu, Sak, Mpi) have final <latinform>-ŋ</latinform> instead of <latinform>-m</latinform>.</par>

尋 OC *dzi̯əm, GSR #662a 'warm up (food)'; Coblin 86:158 ST *ljum > OC *rjəm; Schuessler 2007:539 *s-ləm; B & S 2011: *sə-ləm {sə-l[ə]m} (< -um?); Mand. xún.
融 OC *dʼi̯ông, GSR #1009d 'hot air, steam, heat'; Schuessler 2007:443 *luŋ; B & S 2011: *luŋ (? < *lum); Mand. róng.

<par><hanform>貶</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯ɑm</latinform> ~ <latinform>*pi̯ɑ̆m</latinform>, GSR #641d 'diminish; demote; reduce; wanting, of inferior quality'; Coblin 86:63-4 ST <latinform>*pjam</latinform> > OC <latinform>*pjamx</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:166 <latinform>*pamʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pomʔ {p(r)[o]mʔ}</latinform> ~ <latinform>*promʔ {pr[o]mʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>biǎn</latinform>.</par>

<par>Lahu has developed two allofams of this root: the verb <latinform>tâʔ</latinform> from PLB <latinform>*ʔtakᴴ</latinform>, meaning both ASCEND and HOIST / CARRY ON SHOULDERS, and the spatial noun <latinform>thàʔ</latinform> UPPER SURFACE from PLB <latinform>*C-takᴸ</latinform>, the latter ultimately grammaticalizing to <latinform>thàʔ</latinform> ACCUSATIVE PARTICLE. See LTSR #42, Matisoff 1973/1982: p. 158, DL:676. </par>

<par>This root looks as if it is related to <xref ref="391">#391</xref>.</par><par>Several forms point to a variant with back vowel, perhaps <latinform>*twak</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*twaŋ</latinform>, e.g. WT <latinform>thog-ma</latinform> 'upper end, uppermost place', Limbu <latinform>thuŋ-daŋ</latinform>, <latinform>thuŋ-tho</latinform>.</par><par>STC (p. 52) entertains the possibility that the general Kiranti root <latinform>*taŋ</latinform> HORN is related to this etymon, although it seems better to assign it to <xref ref="371">#371</xref>.</par><par>This root has developed the metaphorical extension of meaning from CARRY UPWARD into BEAR / ENDURE / CARRY A MENTAL BURDEN, in a manner quite similar to English <latinform>bear</latinform> (v.).</par><par>In Lahu, this root has been grammaticalized into an accusative particle, used mostly with animate objects. See DL:676-677 and Matisoff 1973/1982:3.641 (<emph>The Grammar of Lahu</emph>).</par><par>A number of Chinese comparanda have been proposed, for what is evidently a rather complicated word family.</par><par>For an allofamically related root in this semantic area, see <xref ref="2725">#2725</xref>.</par>

陟 OC *ti̯ĕk, GSR #916a-c 'ascend, promote'; Schuessler 2007:619 *trək; B & S 2011: *trək; Mand. zhì.
拓 tuò
<par><hanform>承</hanform> OC <latinform>d̂i̯əŋ</latinform> 'lift; to present; support, assist', GSR 896c; Mand. <latinform>chéng</latinform></par>
<par>The cognacy of the Jingpho morpheme is open to question, since final <latinform>*-l</latinform> usually becomes Jingpho <latinform>-n</latinform>.</par>
<par>It seems that this is a separate root from <xref ref="3550">#3550</xref>.</par>

<par>WB <latinform>khre-thok</latinform> 'leg; shank' and <latinform>khji⁵¹tok⁵⁵</latinform> 'leg' show a final stop, but might well be related to this etymon.</par><par>Note the Lepcha doublet <latinform>a-tʻon̊</latinform> and <latinform>dyan̊</latinform> 'foot'. The Lepcha medial <latinform>-y-</latinform> might reflect the <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix (see Benedict 1943), but the Bodo forms also might point to a medial <latinform>-y-</latinform>.</par>
<par>This Tani root is perhaps related to <xref ref="5771">#5771</xref>.</par>
<par>This etymon illustrates the regular correspondence between WB <latinform>-am</latinform> and Jingpho <latinform>-en</latinform>.</par>

<par>Chantyal <latinform>nak/nakh</latinform> is a borrowing from Nepali <latinform>nāk</latinform>, but the superficially similar Tiddim form <latinform>naːk³</latinform> is a genuine descendant of PKC <latinform>*hnaar</latinform>. (The development of final <latinform>-r</latinform> to a velar is regular in Tiddim.)</par><par>A few Tani languages (Idu, Kaman) have developed secondary final consonants (<latinform>-m, -ŋ</latinform>)). There are separate but perhaps related roots: <xref ref="1415">#1415</xref>, <xref ref="805">#805</xref>. Cf. all the English words relating to the nose that begin with the clusters <emph>sm-</emph> or <emph>sn-</emph>.</par>

<par>Noteworthy is the Qiang (Mawo) reflex with initial <latinform>st-</latinform> rather than <latinform>sn-</latinform>. This obstruentization of <latinform>n-</latinform> after the <latinform>s-</latinform> prefix had hitherto been noted only in Kanauri and Chinese. See HPTB:103.</par>
<par>The semantics of this etymon are rather complicated, but all supporting forms seem valid.</par>

觜 OC *tsi̯ɑ̆r, GSR #358t 'beak, snout, mouth'; Schuessler 2007:636 *tse or *tsai; Mand. zī.
觜/嘴 OC *tsi̯wɑ̆r, GSR #358t 'beak, snout, mouth'; Schuessler 2007:636 *tsoiʔ; Mand. zǔi.
脣/唇 OC *d̑’i̯wən, GSR #455u 'lips'; Schuessler 2007:197 *m-dun; B & S 2011: *sə.dur {sə.[d]ur}; Mand. chun.

<par><hanform>鴨</hanform> OC (<latinform>*ʔɑp</latinform>), GSR (not in #629) 'duck'; Schuessler 2007: (<latinform>*ʔrâp</latinform>); B & S 2011: (<latinform>*qˁrap</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>yā</latinform>.</par>

<par>This tentatively reconstructed root is largely restricted to the final syllables of Loloish compounds, with a possible cognate from Kulung.</par><par>Baima <latinform>ʃhɑ⁵³ pɑ⁵³</latinform> 'skin' (Sun 1991, "Body Parts Questionnaire") and Tamang (Sahu) <latinform>grahm-pa-sa</latinform> 'lip' and <latinform>sya</latinform> 'skin' (Taylor et al. 1972: 1.28, 2.24) look suspiciously like Baima <latinform>shɑ¹³ kɛ³⁵</latinform> 'flesh, meat' and Tamang (Sahu) <latinform>ra sya</latinform> 'meat', suggesting that these syllables derive from <xref ref="34">#34</xref>, rather than from the present etymon.</par><par>Several forms with initial palatal sibilants are assigned by W. T. French to PNN <reconstruction>*C̬-kʰuar</reconstruction>, which we in turn assign to <xref ref="593">#593</xref>.</par>

<par>Clark (1911:898): "TSUNG in <emph>Zungi</emph> and <emph>Mungsen</emph> seems to have an idea of divine, heavenly, or supernatural as indicated in such words as <emph>tsungi</emph>, <emph>tsungpret</emph>, <emph>tsunglu</emph>, <emph>tsungmuk</emph>, <emph>tsungru</emph>, etc."</par>

吐 OC *t’wɑ̂, GSR #31m 'spit'; Coblin 86: OC *thuarh; B & S 2011: *tʰˁaʔ; Mand. tǔ.
吐 OC *t’wɑ̂, GSR #31m 'vomit'; B & S 2011: *tʰˁaʔ-s; Mand. tù.
<par>For parallel developments in WB, cf. NEEDLE/INTESTINES.</par>

<par>This curious etymon has so far been found only in a couple of Kiranti languages, Limbu and Hayu. The meaning seems to be specifically PUBIC HAIR (of either sex). It seems to bear no relationship to any etymon reconstructed with the general meaning BODY HAIR. It is included in this section for convenience, since Limbu <latinform>hi-rā-mu-rik</latinform> contains VAGINA as its first element.</par>

<par>Many reflexes of this etymon are good examples of the process of "prefix pre-emption", whereby the prefix drives out a "weak" root-initial consonant.</par><par>This root seems quite distinct from <xref ref="2417">#2417</xref>.</par>
<par>The Chinese/Tibetan comparison has previously been proposed by Coblin 1986:78 and Gong 2001:#28.</par>

<par><hanform>羽</hanform> OC <latinform>*gi̯wo</latinform>, GSR #98a-b ‘feather, wing’; Coblin 86:78 PC <latinform>*gwljagx</latinform> > OC <latinform>*gwjagx</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:587 <latinform>*waʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ɢʷaʔ {[ɢ]ʷ(r)aʔ}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yǔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root appears mostly in the NE Indian areal group, but also in Kiranti.</par>

<par><hanform>蝙蝠</hanform> OC (<latinform>*pi̯ŭk</latinform>), GSR (not in #933) 'bat (animal)'; Schuessler 2007:165 ST <latinform>*p/bək</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*pək</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>fú</latinform>.</par><par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> In Chinese, <hanform>蝙</hanform> <latinform>fú</latinform> is usually found in the compound <hanform>蝙</hanform><hanform>蝙</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>biānfú</latinform>, which is the common word for 'bat (animal)'. The compound itself is of quite ancient origin (cf. <emph>Shuowen</emph>).</par>

<par>This root, most abundantly attested in Himalayish, appears with all three dental suffixes, <latinform>-n</latinform>, <latinform>-t</latinform>, and <latinform>-s</latinform>. Some Bodo-Garo forms seem to show metathesis of the prefix and the root initial.</par>

古 OC *ko, GSR #49a-e 'old'; Schuessler 2007:259 *kâʔ; B & S 2011: *kˁa; Mand. gǔ.
故 OC *ko, GSR #49i 'old (not new)'; Schuessler 2007:259,263 *kâh; B & S 2011: *kˁaʔ-s; Mand. gù.

<par><hanform>殺</hanform> OC <latinform>*sɑ̆t</latinform>, GSR #319d-e 'kill'; Schuessler 2007:452 <latinform>*srât</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s‹r›at</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shā</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root is well established in Kuki-Chin and Jingpho-Asakian.</par>
<par>This poorly attested root requires further confirmation.</par>
<par>This root has so far only been discovered in Tibetan dialects.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed in Weidert 1987:19. There is a perhaps fortuitous resemblance with Nepali <latinform>khur-kənu</latinform> 'scrape off' (cf. the Indo-Aryan etymon <reconstruction>*KUR</reconstruction> 'scrape' in Turner 1966:171), but in any case this could only have affected the Chepang and Dolakha Newar forms.</par>
<par>From Bengali <latinform>kharagōśa</latinform>, borrowed from Persian <latinform>xar-guš</latinform> ‘hare’ (lit. ‘donkey-ear’).</par>

<par><unicode>21c42</unicode> OC <reconstruction>*d’wən</reconstruction>, GSR #429a 'buttocks'; Mand. <latinform>tún</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>臀</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*d’wən</reconstruction>, GSR #429b-c ‘buttocks’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*dən</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*dun</reconstruction>; Mand. <latinform>tún</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>殿</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*tiən</reconstruction>, GSR #429d ‘rear of army’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*tiənh</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*tɨns</reconstruction>; Mand. <latinform>diàn</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>沌</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*d’wən</reconstruction>, GSR #427h ‘confused, stupid’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*dənx</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*dunʔ</reconstruction>; Mand.  <latinform>dùn</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>鈍</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*d’wən</reconstruction>, GSR #427i ‘dull’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*dənh</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*duns</reconstruction>; Mand. <latinform>dùn</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>頓</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*twən</reconstruction>, GSR #427j ‘worn, dull, spoiled’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*tənh</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*tuns</reconstruction>; Mand. <latinform>dùn</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon is particularly well-attested in Lolo-Burmese, where there is strong evidence for the final <reconstruction>*-p</reconstruction>.</par><par>Other languages seem to point to an open-syllable variant.</par><par>The Qiang (Mawo) binome <latinform>ɕi pi</latinform> might possibly be a dimidiation of the monosyllable with final <latinform>-p</latinform>. The term 'dimidiation' is due to Peter Boodberg, who used it to describe the practice of using two Chinese characters to transcribe a single syllable.</par>

<par>Many languages (e.g. in the Chin group) reflect the simple form <latinform>*lu</latinform>. Other languages (e.g. Chang, Lotha, Naxi) show disyllabic forms, which are plausibly analyzed as sequences of <xref ref="386">#386</xref> and <xref ref="1221">#1221</xref>. In still other languages (e.g. Camling, Dumi, Milang), these two syllables have been compressed into one. For our present purposes, we are treating the latter two cases as reflecting the monosyllabized root, whether or not they are presently disyllabic.</par><par>The forms assigned to this root are distinct from Qiangic and Burmish compounds for HEAD where the last element descends from <xref ref="690">#690</xref>.</par>
<par>This etymon is perhaps related to <xref ref="11">#11</xref> and/or to WB <latinform>lu</latinform> ‘person’ .</par>

<par><hanform>首</hanform> OC <latinform>*śi̯ôg</latinform>, GSR #1102a-c 'head'; Schuessler 2007:470 <latinform>*lhuʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*l̥uʔ-s</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shǒu</latinform>.</par>

<par>This phonologically distinctive root has so far been uncovered only in a few Himalayan languages.</par><par>There is a closely similar root meaning PALM in several Kiranti languages, which seems rather to be related to <reconstruction>*pleŋ</reconstruction> 'flat surface; plank; slab' (STC #138).</par>
<par><hanform>平</hanform> OC <latinform>*bʼi̯ĕng</latinform>, GSR #825a 'flat, even'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*breŋ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>píng</latinform>.</par>

<par>As discussed in Matisoff 1988 ("Straight, flat, full"), this root is allofamically related both to <xref ref="111">#111</xref> and <xref ref="2160">#2160</xref>, the common semantic core being "a complete or ideal realization of a quality appropriate to a particular dimensionality in space".</par>
<par>This root is abundantly attested in Tani, with scattered apparent cognates elsewhere.</par>
<par>This etymon evidently has an onomatopoetic component.</par>
<par>This root occurs as the second element in compounds that begin with <xref ref="385">#385</xref> in two languages, although much more confirmation is needed to set it up for TB as a whole.</par>

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>
<par>There are three similar roots in this semantic area, all possibly related. Cf. <xref ref="4819">#4819</xref> and <xref ref="4903">#4903</xref>.</par>
<par><latinform>Comment:</latinform>: Cf. <hanform>揉</hanform> <latinform>róu</latinform>, comparandum for <xref ref="2510">#2510</xref>.</par>
<par>This root bears a strong resemblance to <xref ref="1816">#1816</xref>, and might well be combined with it in the future.</par>

<par>As indicated in HPTB p. 380, it is sometimes quite difficult to distinguish the Loloish reflexes of the present root from those of <xref ref="2313">#2313</xref>. For now, we are assigning most forms with front vowels to <xref ref="2313">#2313</xref> and most of the others to <xref ref="2739">#2739</xref>.</par><par>The sambar (or sambhur) deer is a large species, <emph>Cervus unicolor</emph>.</par>

<par>A couple of languages (Jingpho, Phunoi) have acquired an <latinform>l-</latinform>prefix with this root, while several others have a nasal prefix which is perhaps a reduction of <xref ref="811">#811</xref>.</par>

<par>The reflexes of this richly attested root range from velar stops to labial stops to <latinform>w-</latinform>, pointing to a complex allofamic reconstruction. The TB languages show much variation in the names of insects and other small creatures, which often run to four or more syllables. The large degree of variation to be found in such words can plausibly be ascribed to the tendency of successive generations of children to create new forms.</par><par>The nasal prefix in the PLB reconstruction accounts for the voicing of the Lahu initial. </par><par>Clearly related are <xref ref="5408">#5408</xref> and <xref ref="5681">#5681</xref>, also with voiced initials in Lahu. Polysyllabic Lahu forms like <latinform>na-gù-na-gá-pɛ̄</latinform> demonstrate that these two roots are separate.</par><par>Also possibly related is <xref ref="5682">#5682</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>紡</hanform> OC <latinform>*pʼi̯wɑng</latinform>, GSR #740r 'twist, spin'; Schuessler 2007:232 <latinform>*phaŋʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*pʰaŋʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>fǎng</latinform>.</par>

<par>This allofam is represented principally by a single WT form with honorific meaning. The source of the labial prefix remains to be discovered. Jäschke (1881:347-8) gives 'bow, compliment, reverence' as an additional meaning. Whether this or 'hand' is the basic meaning is reminiscent of the phenomenon in the history of English whereby a verb meaning 'to pray' had its reference shifted by metonomy to 'bead used in prayer'. (OE <latinform>gebed</latinform> 'prayer' > ME <latinform>bede</latinform>, <latinform>bead</latinform> 'prayer, prayer bead, bead')</par><par>There are also good-looking Lepcha, Limbu, and Karbi cognates, as well as a convincing Chinese comparandum.</par><par>The Karbi variant with <latinform>pak</latinform> seems basically to mean 'leaf', or more generally 'flat object'. The Karbi morpheme <latinform>-pak</latinform> is glossed as 'num. part., flat things' (Walker 1925:119). See also <xref ref="821">#821</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>臂</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*pi̯ĕg</reconstruction>, GSR #853s 'arm'; Li 1971: <latinform>(*pjigh)</latinform>; Baxter 1992: <latinform>(*pjeks)</latinform>; B & S 2011: <reconstruction>*pek-s</reconstruction>; Schuessler 2007:164; Mand. <latinform>bì</latinform>.</par><par>ZJH: The front vowel in Old Chinese would normally preclude a correspondence to PTB <reconstruction>*a</reconstruction>. However, it can be noted that the bulk of the TB evidence points to <reconstruction>*e</reconstruction>; Tibetan occasionally has (dialectally-influenced?) <latinform>ya</latinform> < <reconstruction>*e</reconstruction> where we would normally expect <latinform>e</latinform>. (Tibetan also has <latinform>(y)a</latinform> < PTB <reconstruction>*e</reconstruction> before dental codas, but that is not relevant here.) The comparison can stand, but only if the PTB form is reconstructed as <reconstruction>*pek</reconstruction>, which would have a number of ramifications for the TB etymology, viz.: (1) Unless reconstructed <reconstruction>*pek ⪤ *pyak</reconstruction>, the word-family connection to <reconstruction>*yak</reconstruction> ‘arm’ would be harder to maintain; (2) the Karbi form <latinform>ri-pak</latinform> would have to be attributed entirely to a separate root <reconstruction>*r-pak</reconstruction> ‘leaf’, with subsequent conflation within Karbi of <reconstruction>*pak</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*pek</reconstruction> because of semantic and phonetic overlap. In short, the validity of this Chinese comparison is subject to the uncertainty of the PTB reconstruction stemming from the small number of identified reflexes.  </par>
<par>The Qiangic languages attest both of the principal roots for BAMBOO, the present root and <xref ref="5664">#5664</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>笆</hanform> OC (<latinform>*pɑ̊</latinform>), (not in GSR #39) 'thorny bamboo; bamboo basketry; bamboo fence'; Coblin 86:38 ST <latinform>*pr̵waɣ</latinform> > OC <latinform>*prag</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:152 (<latinform>*prâ</latinform>); B & S 2011: (<latinform>*pˁra</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>bā</latinform>.</par>
<par>The nasal prefix is well-attested in Loloish and Qiangic. It appears overtly in many Northern Loloish languages, and indirectly in e.g. the Lahu voiced initial.</par>

<par>This root could take either the <latinform>*-n</latinform> suffix (as in Sunwar, Newar, and Chinese) or the <latinform>*-t</latinform> suffix (as in Jingpho).</par><par>Many languages have binomes meaning SMOKE, where the first element is <xref ref="2136">#2136</xref>. This first element is sometimes "prefixized" to simple <latinform>m-</latinform>, as in Ergong/Daofu.</par><par>Many Loloish languages (e.g., Lahu <latinform>mû-qhɔ̂</latinform>) have a similar but different formation where the first element is from <xref ref="2473">#2473</xref>.</par><par>Lahu has a doublet derived from this etymon: <latinform>mû-qhɔ̂</latinform> 'smoke' < PLB <latinform>*kəw²</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>qhɔ̀ʔ-lá</latinform> 'ashes' < PLB <reconstruction>*kutᴸ</reconstruction></par>
<par><hanform>熏</hanform> OC <latinform>*xi̯wən~χi̯wən</latinform>, GSR #461a-c 'smoke, steam, ablaze'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*qʰun {qʰu[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xūn</latinform>.</par>

<par>Further support for the notion that this and the preceding etymon are related to the basic root <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction> is provided by the parallelism of allofamy between <reconstruction>*la</reconstruction>/<reconstruction>*ya</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>/<reconstruction>*yak</reconstruction>. Furthermore, there are a group of forms with initial affricates in the Bodo-Garo family that are parallel to <reconstruction>*d-yak</reconstruction> or <reconstruction>*g-yak</reconstruction>. See <xref ref="888">#888 </xref> below.</par>

扁 OC *piɑn, GSR #246a 'flat and thin'; Schuessler 2007:165-6 *pênʔ; B & S 2011: *pˁenʔ {pˁe[n]ʔ}; Mand. biǎn.
板 OC *pw̯ɑn, GSR #262j 'plank, board'; Schuessler 2007:155 *prânʔ; B & S 2011: *C.pˁranʔ; Mand. bǎn.
版 OC *pw̯ɑn, GSR #262k 'register, printing block, edition'; Schuessler 2007:155 *prânʔ; B & S 2011: (*(pˁranʔ); Mand. bǎn.
<par>This root is abundantly attested in Loloish, but has not yet been found elsewhere.</par>
<par><hanform>貧</hanform> OC <latinform>*b’i̯ən</latinform>, GSR #471v; B & S 2011: <latinform>*brən {(Cə.)[b]rə[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>pín</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root has simplex/causative allofams in at least three subgroups: cf. WT <latinform>ḥphro-ba</latinform> and <latinform>spro-ba</latinform>, Jingpho <latinform>próʔ</latinform> and <latinform>śəpróʔ</latinform>, and Sak <latinform>phroʔ</latinform> and <latinform>pru</latinform>.</par><par>The reflexes vary between <latinform>-ro-</latinform> and <latinform>-ra-</latinform>, motivating the reconstruction <latinform>-rwa-</latinform>.</par><par>Kokborok and Tsangla show medial <latinform>-y-</latinform>, while Karenic has medial <latinform>-l-</latinform>, both plausible reflexes of the complex proto-medial.</par><par>The rGyalrong form looks like a borrowing from Tibetan.</par>

<par>The following comparandum assumes a path whereby ST <latinform>*mlyəw</latinform> > + <latinform>-n</latinform> > PCH <latinform>*mlwən</latinform>, yielding Schuessler's OC reconstruction below:</par><par><hanform>吮</hanform> OC <latinform>*d̂’i̯wən</latinform>, GSR #468m 'suck, lick'; Schuessler 2007:476 <latinform>*m-lunʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shǔn</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root has been reconstructed for Proto-Tangkhulic, and has apparent cognates scattered elsewhere.</par>

<par><hanform>母</hanform> OC <latinform>*mə̯u</latinform>, GSR #947a-e ‘mother’; Schuessler 2007:392 <latinform>*mə̂ʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*məʔ</latinform> (? or <latinform>*mˁoʔ</latinform> ); Mand. <latinform>mǔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>The final nasal in the Qiangic forms are probably due to rhinoglottophilia.</par>
<par>WT has both <latinform>ser-mo</latinform> and <latinform>sor-mo</latinform>, but the latter seems to be merely a colloquial variant (Jäschke p. 577 and 580).</par>

<par>This root appears mostly in Lolo-Burmese, Qiangic, and Baic. It frequently occurs reduplicated, and its simple phonological shape makes it likely that it is hypocoristic in origin. (The same may be said for <xref ref="3457">#3457</xref>, below.) Some Lolo-Burmese and Bai forms have been recorded with vowel constriction, but it is not yet clear whether these reflect an allofam with final stop.</par>

<par>The basic meaning of this root in Indo-Aryan was 'donkey' (<latinform>khara-</latinform>), with the word for the formerly exotic lagomorph 'rabbit' expressed by the Old Indo-Aryan compound <latinform>kharabhaka-</latinform> (lit. 'long-eared like a donkey') (Turner 1966). Tibeto-Burman languages have borrowed this IA etymon in both senses. Cf. Nepali <latinform>kharāyō</latinform> 'rabbit' (Schmidt 1993), the source of Newar <latinform>kharāyo</latinform>, as opposed to the meaning 'donkey' in many other TB languages.</par><par>The Guiqiong forms look like IA + Chinese hybrids. Cf. <xref ref="5732">#5732</xref>.</par>
<par>The fit between the Sak and Loloish forms is very good.</par><par>This root seems quite distinct from <xref ref="2684">#2684</xref>.</par>
<par>This root seems to occur mostly in Kuki-Chin, with a possible Naga cognate.</par>
<par>This promising root has so far been uncovered in only two languages.</par>

<par>The final <latinform>-r</latinform> in this root is so far only attested in Nungic and Tangkhulic. The reconstruction of the rhyme needs further evaluation. In particular, some of the forms with open syllables might be better assigned to #163.</par><par>Note the striking similarity of the Dulong form to Karlgren's OC reconstruction <latinform>bʼi̯wər</latinform>.</par>
<par><hanform>肥</hanform> OC <latinform>*bʼi̯wər</latinform>, GSR #582a 'fat, fertile (soil)'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*bər {[b][ə]r}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>féi</latinform>.</par>
<par>Note the triple Tangkhul allofams in <latinform>y- ⪤ w- ⪤ ph-</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon is certainly co-allofamic with <xref ref="6133">#6133</xref>, via a set of semantic associations familiar from Western languages, where left-handedness has been regarded as "gauche" or "sinister". </par>
<par>≠ <hanform>左</hanform>, but add note to refer to comparanda in #6133.</par>

<par>HPTB pp. 219, 510 ill-advisedly reconstructs an allofam <latinform>*r-bi(y)</latinform> to account for Karbi <latinform>ar-vi</latinform>, though STC correctly assigns it to the general <latinform>*b(w)ay</latinform> etymon.</par>

<par>W.T. French 1983:445 sets up a Proto Northern Naga root <reconstruction>*phaːy</reconstruction> AFTER (> e.g. Chang <latinform>pai</latinform> 'backwards, behind'), which he also suggests to be related to the notions of LEFT and LAME / LIMP. (See <xref ref="7190">#7190</xref>.)</par><par>A number of Nungish and Qiangic forms with velar + labial clusters must also reflect this root. If we were to set up an allofam <latinform>*gway</latinform>, this would be tantamount to admitting this root into the select company of etyma with *labiovelar initials like DOG, NEST, COMB, TRUMPET, etc.</par>

樹 OC *d̂’i̯u, GSR #127j-l 'tree'; Schuessler 2007:472 *doʔ; B & S 2011: *m-toʔ-s; Mand. shù.
樹 OC *d̂’i̯u, GSR #127j-l 'plant (v.); place upright'; Schuessler 2007:472 *doh; B & S 2011: *m-toʔ; Mand. shǔ.
<par><hanform>揚</hanform> OC <latinform>*di̯ɑng</latinform>, GSR #720j; B & S 2011: <latinform>*laŋ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yáng</latinform>.</par>
<par>See Gong 2001:21.</par>

<par>This etymon has been found in a few Himalayish languages (Bantawa, Lepcha, Tamang, and perhaps Newar), as well as in the NE Indian Areal Group. The basic meaning seems to be 'come together'. There is a perfect fit between the Karbi and Lepcha forms. The analysis of the Karbi form is as follows: <latinform>i</latinform> 'sleep' + <latinform>rap</latinform> 'befriend; be together' (see <xref ref="1647">#1647</xref>) + <latinform>chom</latinform> 'suffix indicating action performed together' (Walker 1925:37).</par>
<par>Semantically, this root usually refers to monetary repayment, rather than return of the borrowed article. The latter meaning is conveyed by <xref ref="2541">#2541</xref>.</par>

<par>Forms supporting the current etymon are widely attested across TB languages. Early on, Burling 1959:444 reconstructs <latinform>*i</latinform> 'this' for Proto-Bodo; Bradley 1979:454  <latinform>*i</latinform> ~ <latinform>*hi</latinform> for Proto-Loloish; evidence from other TB subgroups justifies treating <latinform>*i</latinform> ~ <latinform>*</latinform> as a root at the PTB level. Schuessler 2007:563 offers a convincing Chinese comparandum: <hanform>伊</hanform> 'this', OC <latinform>*ʔi</latinform>, Mand. <latinform>yī</latinform>.</par><par>Note that we are positing <xref ref="5807">#5807</xref> as an etymon distinct from <xref ref="6595">#6595</xref> and <xref ref="2195">#2195</xref>. Additional comparative and historical research may reveal whether these three PTB etyma should continue to be treated as isolates vs. ultimately as allofams of one another. [SPB]</par>

<par><hanform>伊</hanform> OC <latinform>*ʔi̯ɛr</latinform>, GSR #604a 'this'; Schuessler 2007:563 <latinform>*ʔi</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ʔij</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yī</latinform>.</par>

<par>This is actually a Southeast Asian <emph>Wanderwort</emph>, probably of Austronesian origin. Cf. Proto-Indonesian <latinform>*táʔaŋ</latinform> from PAN <latinform>*saqaŋ</latinform>; Proto-Tai <latinform>xiŋ</latinform> (Li Fang Kuei 1977:208, 210-11); Proto-Kam-Sui <latinform>*siŋ</latinform> (Li Fang Kuei 1965); Hlai <latinform>khüöŋ</latinform>; Proto-Hmong (Mortensen) <latinform>*qhiŋ</latinform>; Khasi <latinform>s’ing/sying</latinform> ‘ginger’ (Singh 1906: 203). See Benedict (ATLC):303.</par>

<par><hanform>薑</hanform> OC <latinform>*ki̯ɑng</latinform>, GSR #710d 'ginger'; Schuessler 2007:307 <latinform>*kaŋ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kaŋ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>jiāng</latinform>.</par>

<par>The Bodic forms listed here seem to exemplify this etymon, but there is a complication. WT <latinform>goŋ</latinform> basically means 'upper part' (Jäschke 1881:72), as in <latinform>pʻrag-goŋ</latinform> 'upper arm' (<latinform>pʻrag</latinform> 'shoulder'), <latinform>bol-goŋ</latinform> 'upper part of foot', <latinform>k'a-lpags goŋ-ma</latinform> 'upper lip'. Less obvious compounds with this morpheme are <latinform>lha-goŋ</latinform> 'larynx' and <latinform>mur-goŋ</latinform> 'temple'.</par><par>Cf. also <xref ref="720">#720</xref>.</par><par>Note the Jingpho doublet with final velar nasal <latinform>sìŋ-kōŋ</latinform>.</par><par>A number of other forms with velar initial and high back vowel are also candidates for assignment to this etymon, including (Asakian) Sak <latinform>ta-kú, tăhù</latinform> 'arm', Lui <latinform>takhu, tahu</latinform> 'arm'; (Kiranti) Limbu <latinform>ku-lāp</latinform> 'wing', <latinform>ku-co</latinform> 'feather'; also Chepang <latinform>waʔ-ko</latinform> 'small feather'.</par><par>There seems no reason to assign Proto Tani <xref ref="1435">#1435</xref> to this root since there seems to be no trace of a liquid medial.</par>

<par><hanform>肱</hanform> OC <latinform>*kwəng</latinform>, GSR #887f 'arm, upper arm'; Li 1971: <latinform>*kwəng</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #522: <latinform>*kʷɨng</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*[k]ʷˤəŋ</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:256; Mand. <latinform>gōng</latinform>.</par><par>ZJH: Schuessler (2007:256) says this Chinese word “has apparently no outside cognate”, and derives it from Chinese <hanform>弓</hanform> 'bow (the weapon)', Mand. <latinform>gōng</latinform>. The proposed correspondence to PTB <latinform>*k(w)aŋ</latinform> is reasonable on semantic and phonetic grounds, but the competing internal Chinese etymology must also be considered.</par>

<par>This root is well-attested, both with a dental stop initial and initial <latinform>r-</latinform>, leading one to hypothesize an ultimate origin in a sequence of consonants like <latinform>*trak</latinform> or <latinform>drak</latinform>, although this cluster is not reconstructed for PTB or PST. Certain branches of the family, like Lolo-Burmese, show evidence for both the stop- and rhotic-initialed variants. </par><par>See <xref ref="2686">#2686</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>織</hanform> OC <latinform>*t̑i̯ək</latinform>, GSR #920f 'weave'; Coblin 86: ST <latinform>*tjək</latinform> > TB *<latinform>tak</latinform> > OC <latinform>*tjək</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tək</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>zhī</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root is attested in Dengic and Tamangic, but further support would be welcome. This etymon was suggested by Liberty Lidz.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root awaits confirmation.</par>

<par><hanform>蝨</hanform> / <hanform>虱</hanform> OC <latinform>*ṣiɛt</latinform>, GSR #506a 'louse'; Coblin 86:106 TB <latinform>*śrik</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:461 <latinform>*srit</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*srit {sri[t]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shī</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is distinct from <xref ref="6705">#6705</xref>, and frequently occurs in compounds with it.</par><par>rGyalrong shows an <latinform>r-</latinform> prefix so far not found elsewhere.</par><par>Lolo-Burmese shows tonal variation in this root, sometimes accompanied by the semantic difference between SING / PLAY and DANCE.</par>
<par>This reconstruction is actually a bit different from that offered in DL:228 (PLB <latinform>*ʔga1¹</latinform> or <latinform>*ga³</latinform>).</par>
<par><hanform>歌</hanform> OC <latinform>*kɑ̂</latinform>, GSR #1q; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kˁaj {[k]ˁaj}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>gē</latinform>.</par>
<par><hanform>鳶</hanform> OC <latinform>*di̯wɑn</latinform>, GSR #230a; Mand. <latinform>yuān</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root occurs primarily in Lolo-Burmese, with a good Chinese comparandum.</par>

<par>This root requires further confirmation, but looks fairly solid. The Kayan form shows a velar prefix.</par><par>This root looks rather similar to <xref ref="122">#122</xref>, but we are keeping them separate for now. The present root seems always to mean 'hungry' while <xref ref="122">#122</xref> apparently always means 'thirsty'.</par>
<par>Written Tibetan attests to both prefixal <latinform>m-</latinform> and <latinform>l-</latinform>. The labial glide remains as such in Nocte.</par>

<par>According to Clark 1911, Ao (Chungli) <latinform>san</latinform> is frequently pronounced <latinform>sain</latinform>. Temsunungsang 2009 posits an underlying form <latinform>sajin</latinform>.</par>
<par>The rime in <latinform>san</latinform> is unusual for Sangtam, indicating a possible borrowing.</par>
<par>This etymology is due to Kenneth VanBik.</par>
<par>This etymon usually occurs as the second element in compounds.</par>

<par>This etymon comprises two allofams, formerly considered to be separate roots: one with a rhotic and one with a palatal element. Both variants reflect a velar pre-initial. Many languages, e.g. WT, Chepang, and Ao (Chungli), have doublets, preserving reflexes of both allofams.</par>
<par>The first element in this binome is <xref ref="1603">#1603</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is well attested in the NE Indian Areal Group and Burmish, with an excellent cognate in Written Tibetan. The correspondence of WT <latinform>-ig</latinform> to WB <latinform>-ac</latinform> is perfectly regular. (Cf. HPTB pp. 343-348). The Maring form points to an allofam with final nasal.</par>
<par>The Qiangic languages attest both of the principal roots for BAMBOO, the present root and <xref ref="2549">#2549</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>尋</hanform> OC <latinform>*dzi̯əm</latinform>, GSR #662a 'measure of 8 chǐ (<hanform>尺</hanform>)'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*sə-ləm {sə-l[ə]m}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xún</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymology is due to VanBik (2009).</par>

編 OC *b’i̯ɑn, GSR #246e 'braid, plait'; B & S 2011: *pˁen {pˁe[n]}; Mand. biān.
辮 OC (not in GSR #219, prob. *b’i̯ɑn) 'braid, plait'; B & S 2011: (*brenʔ); Mand. biàn.

<par>VanBik #366 (p. 126) suggests a connection between the Kuki-Chin root and Written Burmese <latinform>phan</latinform> 'make, create', although the comparison with the present root seems preferable.</par>

<par>VanBik #366 (p. 126) suggests a connection between the Kuki-Chin root and Written Burmese <latinform>phan</latinform> 'make, create', although the comparison with the present root seems preferable.</par>
<par>This root is easily confused with another etymon #2178 <latinform>*bəw</latinform> that has a range covering both INSECT/VERMIN and SNAKE.</par>

<par><hanform>虺</hanform> OC <latinform>*χi̯wər</latinform>, GSR #572a 'snake brood, snake'; Schuessler 2007:287 <latinform>*hmuiʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*r̥ujʔ {[r̥]u[j]ʔ}</latinform> (dial. <latinform>r̥-</latinform> > <latinform>x-</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>huǐ</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymon bears a fortuitous resemblance to <xref ref="174">#174</xref>, a root with the opposite meaning.</par>

<par><hanform>粲</hanform> OC <latinform>*ts’ɑn</latinform>, GSR #154b 'bright, splendid'; Schuessler 2007:176 <latinform>*tshâns</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tsʰˁar-s {[tsʰ]ˁar-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>càn</latinform>.</par>

<par>This is one of over 30 TB etyma showing interchange between <reconstruction>*p-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*w-</reconstruction>, including such important roots as AXE, BAMBOO, BELLY, MAN/HUSBAND, PIG, etc. In this volume, see also <xref ref="674">#674</xref>. These are reconstructed with <reconstruction>*pw-</reconstruction> clusters in the revised version of STC (e.g. <reconstruction>*pak = *pwak</reconstruction>; see n. 78, pp. 23-4). I originally preferred to treat the stop component as prefixal (e.g. <reconstruction>*p-wak</reconstruction>), but later abandoned this approach in favor of an “extrusional” interpretation, where the [-w-] is considered to be a mere phonetic perseveration of the preceding stop, e.g. <reconstruction>*pʷak</reconstruction> (see Matisoff 2000a). This extrusion is especially common before the vowel [-a-], but also, as in EGG, occasionally occurs before [-u-]. See also HPTB:61-62.</par><par>In the following sections, the reflexes of this etymon are presented separately, according to whether they derive from the variant with semivowel (<reconstruction>*wu</reconstruction>) or stop (<reconstruction>*pu</reconstruction>) initial.</par>
<par><hanform>纏</hanform> OC <latinform>*d’i̯ɑn</latinform>, GSR #204c 'bind, wind'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*dran {[d]ra[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>chán</latinform>.</par>
<par>A PST root of this shape was proposed in Gong (2000 #29).</par>
<par>This etymon seems to be confined to the Naga group.</par>
<par>This root bears some resemblance to <xref ref="3327">#3327</xref>, but is probably quite distinct.</par>

<par>This root was reconstructed as PL <reconstruction>*ʔ-ban²</reconstruction> 'ring' in Bradley 1979:235, and as PTB <reconstruction>*bran</reconstruction> in HPTB (p.69, n.101). In favor of this reconstruction is Pumi Dayang <latinform>ʐə̆ bʐéɴ</latinform> (HPTB:69); however, Lahu <latinform>-e</latinform> (as in <latinform>làʔ-pē</latinform> 'finger-ring') can descend from either <reconstruction>*-an</reconstruction> or <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction>, and on balance (see e.g. Tankghul, Mikir, and Jingpho) we now prefer the present reconstruction. </par>

<par>This root has only been discovered in two NE Indian Areal Group languages of Manipur, Meithei and Puiron, but the semantic and phonological correspondence is perfect. However, since Meithei (also known as Manipuri) is the dominant TB language of Manipur, the Puiron form may well be borrowed from it.</par>

<par>STC #85 originally reconstructed this root as <reconstruction>*ruŋ</reconstruction>, but this was later changed to <reconstruction>*rwaŋ</reconstruction> (STC p.32, n.101), treated as an allofam of <reconstruction>*rwa</reconstruction> (see STC p.75, n.231).</par>

角 OC *kǔk, GSR #1225a-c 'horn, corner'; Schuessler 2007:309 *krôk; B & S 2011: *C.kˤrok; Mand. jiǎo.
觡 OC *klɑ̆k, GSR #766b' 'antlers, horns'; Schuessler 2007:253 *krâk; Mand. gé.

<par>Initial <latinform>-r-</latinform> in this root was preemptible after either <latinform>n-</latinform> (e.g. PK <reconstruction>*noŋ</reconstruction>, Dolakha Newar <latinform>ŋwɔ̃</latinform>) or <latinform>-g- </latinform> (e.g. Chinese <emph>jiǎo</emph> <hanform>角</hanform>).</par>

<par>Forms like Limbu <latinform>ùl taŋ</latinform>, Yakha <latinform>ùl taŋ</latinform><emph></emph> ‘horn’ are probably not from this root, but could rather be from <xref ref="371">#371</xref>.</par>
<par>The second syllables of the Milang, Tagin, Idu, and Galo forms resemble <xref ref="388">#388</xref>, but we are keeping them separate for now.</par>
<par>This etymology is due to Gong (2001:25).</par>
<par>cognate to GSR 457ab <latinform>mi̯ěn</latinform> “people” .</par>

<par>The <latinform>*-n</latinform> suffix (a morpheme signifying collectivity, according to Benedict, <plainlatinform>STC n. 428, pp. 157-8</plainlatinform>) is attested in Written Burmese and Chinese.</par><par>This morpheme frequently occurs as first element in compounds signifying body part terms (e.g., in Mzieme, Zeme, Magar).</par><par>It also occurs as final element in names of Tibeto-Burman ethnic groups (e.g., Prinmi, Thangmi, Khumi, Xumi), and as first element in such names as well (e.g., Mizo, Mikir, Mishing, Miji). In this respect, it is similar to <xref ref="5632">#5632</xref>.</par>

<par><hanform>民</hanform> OC <latinform>*mi̯ən ~ mi̯ĕn</latinform>, GSR #457a 'people'; Schuessler 2007:386 <latinform>*min</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*miŋ {mi[ŋ]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>mín</latinform></par>

<par><hanform>力</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*li̯ək</reconstruction>, GSR #928a-b 'sinew, strength, force, power'; Li 1971: <latinform>(*ljək)</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #1314: <reconstruction>*C‑rjɨk</reconstruction>; B & S 2011: <reconstruction>*k.rək</reconstruction>; Schuessler 2007:351; Mand. <latinform>lì</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>仂</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*lək, *li̯ək</reconstruction>, GSR #928c 'a tenth'; Li 1971: <latinform>(*lək, ljək)</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #1315: <reconstruction>*C‑rɨk, C‑rjɨk</reconstruction>; B & S 2011: <latinform>(*[r]ˤək, [r]ək)</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:349-350; Mand. <latinform>lè</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>扐</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*lək</reconstruction>, GSR #928d 'space between fingers'; Li 1971: <latinform>(*lək)</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #1315: <reconstruction>*C‑rɨk</reconstruction>; B & S 2011: <latinform>(*[r]ˤək)</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:349-350; Mand. <latinform>lè</latinform>.</par><par>The graph seems to depict an arm with a hand. Also, in the same phonetic series, <hanform>仂</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*lək ⪤ li̯ək</reconstruction>, GSR #928c ‘a tenth’ (from the 10 fingers); Mand. <latinform>lè</latinform>, and <hanform>扐</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*lək</reconstruction>, GSR #928d ‘space between the fingers’ (where divination sticks were inserted); Mand. <latinform>lè</latinform>.</par><par>For a similar semantic association, cf. Japanese <latinform>ude</latinform> ‘arm’ and te ‘hand’, which may often be used in the sense of ‘ability, competence, skill’.</par><par>ZJH: Handel 2009:133-4 considers Chinese <hanform>亦</hanform> ‘armpit’ <latinform>yì</latinform> and <hanform>翼</hanform> ‘wing’ <latinform>yì</latinform>, both with OC *lateral initial, to be the most direct cognates to the basic PTB root <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>, while <hanform>力</hanform> <latinform>lì</latinform> with initial <reconstruction>*r‑</reconstruction> is probably a more distantly related member of the Chinese word family. <par>This root appears mainly in Southern Loloish. It is reconstructed as PLB <reconstruction>*loŋ²</reconstruction> in Bradley 1979, #119A. This etymon bears some resemblance to <reconstruction>*b-raŋ</reconstruction> CHEST, but we are keeping them separate for now, despite the similarity between, e.g. WB <latinform>raŋ-pat</latinform> 'chest' and Bisu <latinform>lɔŋ-pɛ́t</latinform> 'breast', where the second syllables are both assigned to <xref ref="3463">#3463</xref>.</par>

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>

<par>Reflexes of this root are divided with respect to voicing of the initial, as well as in terms of vowel height. Since PTB <latinform>*-i</latinform> is much better attested than PTB <latinform>*-e</latinform>, we have opted to reconstruct this etymon with the former.</par>

<par>This etymon is well-represented in Himalayish (with the <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> prefix in WT) and in Qiangic (with a lateral prefix in Ergong and rGyalrong). The root-initial <reconstruction>*ŋ-</reconstruction> has become <latinform>n-</latinform> in several languages.</par><par>The first syllables of Meithei <latinform>naw phəm</latinform> 'placenta' and Tangkhul <latinform>nao-pam</latinform> 'womb' are apparently not cognate, since <reconstruction>*-al</reconstruction> > Meithei <latinform>-al</latinform> or <latinform>-an</latinform> (cf. <reconstruction>*m-kal ⪤ *s-gal</reconstruction> SMALL OF BACK > Meithei <latinform>nam-gal ~ nam-gan</latinform>) and > Tangkhul <latinform>-ay</latinform> (cf. <reconstruction>*baːl</reconstruction> FILTH / EXCREMENT > Tangkhul <latinform>páy</latinform>). S. Imoba 2004 (<emph>Manipuri to English Dictionary</emph>, p. 202) records <latinform>-naw</latinform> 'small, little', <latinform>naw-wà</latinform> 'baby', <latinform>nawsum</latinform> 'cradle', and <latinform>nawpu-bə</latinform> 'gestation, pregnancy'.</par>

<par>If the Chepang and Magar forms belong here, they must be explained as due to preemption by the sibilant prefix. It is also possible that these forms would be better assigned to <xref ref="635">#635</xref>.</par>

<par>The rGyalrong form is likely to be a borrowing from Tibetan.</par><par>The putative semantic connection between Kuki-Chin GENERATION / ERA and the Tibetan form rests on the idea that a particular generation is marked by a certain characteristic.</par>

<par>The Qiangic and rGyalrongic forms point to <reconstruction>*s/m-dzi-s/k</reconstruction>, i.e. a prototype with affricated root-initial, either a sibilant or nasal prefix, and either a sibilant or velar suffix.</par>

Mikir <latinform>a-lang</latinform> 'juice'. Cf. PTB <reconstruction>*laŋ</reconstruction> 'water, river, valley' (Prof. Matisoff, p.c.)" Sun 1993a: 120.</par>

The primary comparandum here is:浪 OC *lɑ̂ng, GSR #735k 'name of a river'; B & S 2011: *rˁaŋ {*[r]ˁaŋ}; Mand. (làng).The entry for 浪 làng in 說文 Shuō Wén says simply "滄浪河南入江", where 滄浪河 Cánglàng Hé is the name of a river, purportedly in Central China. Early on, 浪 appears in other toponyms as well, e.g. 莊浪 Zhuānglàng, a county in Gansu prov., 康浪 Kānglàng, a river in Shandong prov. At the very least, we are justified in treating 浪 as a hydronym in the above cases; however, we would further argue that 浪 was originally a more general, Sinitic term for 'river, stream, flow'. A useful analogy can be found in European toponyms such as Aberdour (Scotland), Durance (France), Dora (Italy), which may not immediately appear to be related to each other, but all of which are posited as deriving from a Celtic element *dur, a generic term for 'water'.Also attested early on for 浪 is the meaning 'excessive, dissolute', cited by Karlgren in GSR #735k, but as a separate etymon. Likewise, the meaning 'wave, breaker' the usual hydrologic sense of 浪 in Modern Chinese, may be a separate development; it is not cited in GSR #735, and is apparently not attested earlier than MC.
[SPB]

<par>This PTB root was borrowed into Indo-Aryan at an early date; cf. Sanskrit <latinform>śr̥gāla-</latinform>. Later on, such an Indic form was borrowed into many TB languages, especially in Nepal. This is an excellent example of a "backloan": TB > IA > TB. See "Toward a Eurasian Bestiary", part II: JACKAL in Sino-Tibetan and Indo-European (Matisoff 2010).</par>

<par><hanform>翔</hanform> OC <latinform>*dzi̯ɑng</latinform>, GSR #732p 'roam back and forth; fly to and fro' ; Li 1971: <latinform>(*rjang)</latinform>; Baxter 1992:797 <latinform>*z(l)jang</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*s-[ɢ]aŋ</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:533; Mand. <latinform>xiáng</latinform>.</par><par>JAM: <hanform>翔</hanform> OC <latinform>*dzi̯ang</latinform> 'go to and fro; fly backwards and forwards; soar; walk with elbows kept as outstretched wings' GSR #732p.</par><par>Yang 1980 reconstructs PST <reconstruction>*s-g/ki̯ang</reconstruction>, since the same phonetic (<hanform>羊</hanform>) occurs in <hanform>姜</hanform> '<emph>surname</emph>' (Mand. <latinform>jiāng</latinform>), a word reconstructed with a velar initial as <latinform>*ki̯ɑng</latinform> in GSR #711a.</par><par>ZJH: This Chinese word belongs to a phonetic series that presents unique challenges of reconstruction, as evidenced by the wide variation seen in the proposed reconstructions. Most words in the series suggest a lateral initial, but a few have velar initial; this is an atypical pattern. Li’s reconstruction of <reconstruction>*rj-</reconstruction> is the norm for words with Middle Chinese <latinform>zj-</latinform>, and makes a good match with the PTB syllable <reconstruction>*raŋ</reconstruction>. But there is enough uncertainty about the Old Chinese to throw the comparison as a whole in doubt, especially since the Chinese meaning seems to have a semantic center closer to ‘wander’ than to ‘wing’ (contra the presence of the ‘wing’ element (<hanform>羽</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>yǔ</latinform>) in the written character).</par><par>Schuessler (2007:533) reconstructs “<reconstruction>*s-jaŋ</reconstruction> or <latinform>s-laŋ</latinform> (?)” and places it in the same word family as <hanform>徉</hanform> ‘to walk irresolutely, wander’, Mand. <latinform>yáng</latinform>.</par>

<par>Garo analysis is different from STC #3. There is a claim that <latinform>riŋ < *rni</latinform>. However, in Tsangla, <latinform>sha-ni-ring</latinform>, the second syll is already RED/GUMS 504. Meithei has both <latinform>ya-ri</latinform> and <latinform>so-ni</latinform> (which may be spurious, # 116164)</par>

<par>The Garo form <latinform>wa-riŋ</latinform> is derived from an earlier <reconstruction>**rni</reconstruction> in STC #3, where it is assigned to <xref ref="504">#504</xref>, but the present analysis seems preferable.</par>
<par>This root bears some resemblance to <xref ref="2773">#2773</xref>, although the velar element in the latter is prefixal, not part of an initial cluster.</par>

噆 OC *tsʼəm/tsəp, GSR #660f, 660o ‘have in mouth; bite; sting’; Li 1971: (*tshəm/p); Baxter 1992: (*tshɨm/p); B & S 2011: *tsˁəp {tsˁ[ə]p}; Mand. cǎn, zǎn.Neither Li nor Baxter (1992) reconstructs this word. As Baxter notes (1992:555), this phonetic series presents unusual difficulties for OC reconstruction. Baxter reconstructs some words with *o and others with *i/ɨ.It is not clear why Karlgren lists the character twice in GSR #660. At GSR #660f he reconstructs *tsʼəm for the meaning ‘have in mouth’ and *tsəp for the meaning ‘bite’, while at GSR #660o he reconstructs *tsəp meaning ‘sting and suck’. Karlgren’s usual practice in GSR is to group multiple readings of a single character together, listing that character only once.On the apparent mismatch between the voiceless PTB initial and the voiced OC initial, see the discussion under #1654.Vowel correspondences between OC and PTB before labial codas are difficult to pin down. In Li's system, OC *-ə- regularly corresponds to TB *-u- and/or *-i-. Examples include 三 OC *səm ‘three’, PTB *gsum, Mand. sān; 入 OC *njəp 'enter/sink', PTB *nup ⪤ *nip, Mand. rù; and 寢 OC *tshjəm ‘sleep’, WT gzim, Mand. qǐn (Coblin 1986:134). Using Baxter’s system, however, where six vowels (as opposed to just *a, *ə in Li’s system) occur before bilabial consonants, correspondences are less regular. This is in part because in many cases there is ambiguity in the reconstruction of vowels before bilabial codas in his system. At the present state of our knowledge, the proposed cognate set is viable.
[ZJH]

<par>Many forms descending from this etymon have voiced initials, a fact which we might capture by setting up a separate allofam <latinform>*m-dz(y)up ⪤ *m-dz(y)ip</latinform>. Some of these cases can be ascribed to the influence of the nasal prefix. However, since there is so much variation in voicing even within a single subgroup, we have chosen to group all the reflexes under the prototype with voiceless root initial.</par>

<par>This widespread etymon is set up as <reconstruction>*dzoːp</reconstruction> in STC #69, on the basis of forms from four Barish and Kuki-Chin languages: (Barish) Dimasa <latinform>dźop</latinform> and (Kuki-Chin) Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>foːp</latinform>, Thado <latinform>tsop</latinform>, and Siyin <latinform>tuop</latinform> 'suck; kiss'. (Cf. also Kom Rem <latinform>məǰop</latinform>, Tiddim <latinform>toːp</latinform>, and Lai Chin <latinform>doop</latinform>.) However, the rhymes <reconstruction>*-op</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*-oːp</reconstruction> are extremely rare in TB, with virtually no other examples (see HPTB, pp. 381-2), and it seems preferable to consider these forms with <latinform>-o(ː)-</latinform> vocalism to reflect a localized secondary development. (On the other hand, there is a parallel form in NW rGyalrong <latinform>kantsʰrop</latinform>, alongside Ma'erkang rGyalrong <latinform>kɑ mə scçup</latinform>.) The vast majority of the reflexes point to a high proto-vowel, either <reconstruction>*-u-</reconstruction> or <reconstruction>*-i-</reconstruction>. The alternation between these two vowels is especially frequent in the environment of labial consonants, and indeed this is one of the best attested patterns of variation in TB, as well as in Chinese. (See VSTB pp. 41-2, HPTB pp. 493-505. See also <xref ref="3450">#3450</xref> and <xref ref="1613">#1613</xref>, below.) Several languages show internal variation between these vowels (e.g. Dimasa <latinform>dźop</latinform> 'suck' <plainlatinform>⪤</plainlatinform> <latinform>sep</latinform> 'milk'; Tiddim <latinform>teːp¹</latinform> 'suck' <plainlatinform>⪤</plainlatinform> <latinform>toːp¹</latinform> 'kiss').</par><par>The nasal prefix <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> has been added to the reconstruction since it occurs in many languages of the NE Indian Areal Group (e.g. Sema <latinform>mtsü</latinform>, Ao Mongsen <latinform>mechep</latinform>, Kom Rem <latinform>məǰop</latinform>, Moyon <latinform>njúp</latinform>, etc.), as well as in Qiangic (e.g. Ergong <latinform>ndʑip⁵³</latinform>, Namuyi <latinform>ntshu⁵³</latinform>), and WT <latinform>ḥjib(s)-pa</latinform>, where <emph>a-chung</emph> "ḥ" is interpreted as a nasal prefix (see HPTB pp. 115-6).</par><par>Reflexes of the allofam with <reconstruction>*-u-</reconstruction> vocalism are presented in <xref ref="5541">#5541</xref>, and those from the <reconstruction>*-i-</reconstruction> variant in <xref ref="5541">#5541</xref>.</par><par>I also recognize an opened-syllable allofam <reconstruction>*dz(y)əw</reconstruction> <latinform>MILK/BREAST</latinform>, treated separately below <xref ref="5539">#5539</xref>.</par><par>A few languages show secondary variants with final stops other than <latinform>-p</latinform>. Jingpho has a doublet, one with the expected <latinform>-p</latinform> (<latinform>tšùp</latinform> 'suck, kiss, absorb'), and one that reflects final <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction> (<latinform>tšúʔ</latinform> 'breast'; <latinform>-ʔ</latinform> is the regular Jg. reflex of <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction>). Similar forms appear in several other languages: Sherpa (Helambu) <latinform>chuk pekin</latinform> 'kiss', Tamang (Sahu) <latinform>cyok-pa</latinform> 'kiss', Lashi <latinform>tʃu̱:k⁵⁵</latinform> 'nurse; suckle', Bisu <latinform>tʃhɔ̀k</latinform> 'suck'. All these languages seem to have doublets with both <latinform>-p</latinform> and <latinform>-k</latinform>. WB has an aberrant form with a final dental (<latinform>cut</latinform> 'suck'), paralleled by Dulong <latinform>tsut⁵⁵</latinform> 'suckle' and Tamang (Sahu) <latinform>nyeh <emph>cʰuT</emph> Tai'◦ti-pa</latinform>.<footnote>Cf. LTSR #73, which contains several typos corrected in VSTB, p. 32 and p. 239, n. 41.</footnote></par><par>For other etyma with similar variation in final position, cf. <xref ref="3450">#3450</xref> and <xref ref="1613">#1613</xref>.</par><par>The semantic connection between SUCK and KISS is paralleled by the vulgar English expression <emph>suck face</emph> for 'kiss'.</par><par>There is a possible Chinese comparison, <hanform>噆</hanform> (Mand. <latinform>cǎn, zǎn</latinform>); this character has two OC readings, <latinform>*ts'əm</latinform> 'have in the mouth' and <latinform>*tsəp</latinform> 'bite; sting and suck (as a mosquito)' [GSR #660f, #660o]; see also Coblin 1986:144. TB also shows some variation between final stop and nasal in this etymon: cf. Sunwar <latinform>cim-cā</latinform> 'milk a cow' <plainlatinform>⪤</plainlatinform> <latinform>yup-</latinform> 'suck'; Tsangla (Motuo) <latinform>jum</latinform> 'suck (milk)' <plainlatinform>⪤</plainlatinform> <latinform>tɕup⁵⁵ a⁵⁵</latinform> 'kiss'. The final nasal in the Milang form <latinform>ɟim-ma</latinform> seems clearly to be due to assimilation of the initial to the suffix. See the discussion by ZJH, below. </par>

<par>An undoubtedly related root (not in STC or HPTB) is <xref ref="86">#86</xref>, the semantic connection being the squeezing action required to express milk from a breast or udder. See DL:557-8.</par>

<par>This etymon is set up as PLB <latinform>ʔpök</latinform> in LTSR #55, on the basis of forms like Lahu <latinform>pɔ̂ʔ</latinform> and Bisu <latinform>pɤ̄k</latinform>. The evidence for medial <latinform>*-r-</latinform> is from Tamangic and Kiranti.</par><par>The unusual PLB rhyme (<latinform>*-ök</latinform>) might well be an artifact of the labial glide that is now reconstructed.</par><par>This root now seems quite separate from <xref ref="2563">#2563</xref>, contra HPTB:378-379.</par>

However, we posit a similar relationship between the very well-established <xref ref="2686">#2686</xref> and <xref ref="5669">#5669</xref>.</par>

A number of other Himalayish forms point to a variant with final velar stop (Tamang Sahu <latinform>'prok◦tong 'prok◦tong chyon</latinform>, Thulung <latinform>prok-</latinform>, and Sunwar <latinform>pre:k-cā</latinform>), while Bahing <latinform>prøt-</latinform> has a final dental.</par>

Mand. <latinform>quān</latinform> 'a circle; to draw a circle, delineate'.</par><par><hanform>圈</hanform> GSR #226k includes two separate, yet semantically related etyma, distinguished by the voicing of the initial consonant: 1) 'corral/to confine', and 2) 'circle/to draw a circle' (Karlgren glosses 2) as 'turn around on foot'; in modern Chinese, however, the predominant sense is 'circle').</par><par>No reconstruction for <hanform>圈</hanform> is offered by B & S 2011 or Schuessler 2007. <par>The <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix is reconstructed for this etymon on the basis of forms like Written Tibetan <latinform>snag</latinform> and Written Burmese <latinform>hmaŋ</latinform> 'ink', as well as on the basis of the high stopped tone in Loloish. The Mpi form <latinform>naŋʔ³</latinform> indirectly reflects this prefix by a sort of "rhinoglottal transfer" that occurs when a complex nasal (either with the <latinform>*s-</latinform> or the <latinform>*ʔ-</latinform> prefix) is the onset of a syllable with the <latinform>*-ak</latinform> rhyme: the original final stop was replaced by a vowel quality including both a nasal and a laryngeal component. There are no fewer than nine examples, see JAM (1978:22-23).</par><par>A similar root, PTB <latinform>*s-maŋ</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>*s-mak</latinform> BLACK / DARK / INK, is reconstructed with an initial labial nasal. This may well be the original form of the etymon (there are excellent Chinese comparanda with a labial nasal), with the dental nasal a plausible outcome from the combination of the <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix and a labial nasal.</par>
<par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Cf. comparanda and discussion under <xref ref="6130">#6130</xref>.</par>

<par>This root seems confined to Himalayish and Qiangic. There is also a possible Tani cognate: Tagin <latinform>a din <emph>li</emph> ne</latinform> 'flesh'. </par>

<par>This root is solidly attested in the NE Indian Areal Group. There is also an excellent match between the Kaman reflex and the reconstructed Tangut form, on the basis of which we set up a nasal prefix for the etymon. W.T. French (1983:490-1) suggests that this root shows an association in Northern Naga between BREAST and FLOWER ("flower" = tree + breast).</par>

<par>Coblin (1986: 47) suggests a relationship between this PTB root and Chinese <hanform>弓</hanform> (Mand. <emph>gōng</emph>) 'bow', the semantic connection residing in the shape of a bent tree branch. Cf. Written Burmese <latinform>ə-kuing</latinform> 'stalk, branch', <latinform>kuing</latinform> 'hang over in a curve', <latinform>ə-kùing</latinform> 'large branch, bough of tree'.</par><par>Jingpho has forms with medial <latinform>-ji-</latinform> (<latinform>lă³¹<emph>kji̱ŋ³¹</emph></latinform> 'branch / twig' [TBL], <latinform>phun⁵⁵lă³¹<emph>kji̱n³¹</emph></latinform> 'branch' [ZMYYC]), which are perhaps related to the present root.</par>

<par><hanform>弓</hanform> OC <latinform>*ki̯ŭng</latinform>, GSR #901a-d 'bow (n.)'; Coblin 86:47 ST <latinform>*kjəngw</latinform> > OC <latinform>*kjəngw</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:255 <latinform>*kwəŋ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*kʷəŋ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>gōng</latinform>.</par>
<par>This root has been reconstructed for Proto-Tani (J. Sun 1993a), and receives support from the Chepang form.</par>

<par>This etymon is apparently distinct from <xref ref="5694">#5694</xref>. The <latinform>*-ya-</latinform> medial is reconstructed to account for forms such as Apatani <latinform>prjɯʔ-pu</latinform>, Gurung <latinform>cʰiɖ praːqba</latinform>, and Yi Nanhua <latinform>ʑi³³ phiɑ³³</latinform>, while the <latinform>*-wa-</latinform> medial accounts for the Central Loloish, Naxi, and Bai forms.</par>
<par>The allofamic relationship between this root and <xref ref="6407">#6407</xref> is not suggested in Sun 1993:133, 135, although it seems extremely likely.</par>
<par>Note the "brightening" of the vowel from <latinform>*-a</latinform> to <latinform>-i</latinform> in Tangut.</par>

<par>This morpheme is actually a general noun suffix in Tibetan and other languages. For ease of reference, however, nouns with this suffix relating to the body are here assembled in one place. See Jäschke:324-325.</par><par>Written Tibetan has the three suffixes <latinform>-pa</latinform>, <latinform>-ba</latinform>, and <latinform>-po</latinform>, which sometimes have nominalizing force, but often merely serve to provide an additional syllable to a noun root. After a verbal root, <latinform>-pa</latinform> behaves rather like English 'to', used in the citation form of verbs, creating a kind of infinitive or verbal noun.</par>
<par>This root, which occurs as the final element in compounds, is very well established, occurring in several branches of the family.</par>

<par>This etymon illustrates the widespread variational pattern between <reconstruction>*-u-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*-i-</reconstruction>, with some languages (Jingpho, Dimasa) having reflexes of both variants.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root requires further confirmation, although the Damu-Sumi comparison looks particularly good.</par>

中 OC *ti̯ôngHPTB pp. 287, 310 incorrectly cites GSR's reconstruction as *ti̯əŋ. This was corrected in L. Sagart's review of HPTB (2006:217) to *t̑i̯uŋ, which is also incorrect: this is Karlgren's MC form. [JAM], GSR #1007a-e ‘middle, center’; Li 1971: *trjəngw; Baxter 1992:477, 1641: *k-ljung; Mand. zhōng.There are at least two competing etymologies for this Chinese word, one relating it to Tibetan gźung ‘middle, midst’ (e.g. Bodman 1980:123 set 240, Coblin 1986:53) and the other the one proposed here (e.g. STC p. 182). Baxter (1992:525) follows Bodman, reconstructing *k-l- rather than *trj- to match Tibetan gźung and to explain the use of the character as a sound gloss for 宮 *k(r)jung, Mand. gōng in the Eastern Han.The original comparison in STC seemed suspect because of the irregular correspondence between the stop initial in Chinese and the sibilant initial in Tibeto-Burman, but the revised PTB etymon makes a good match. If we consider the possibility of reconstructing *r-tjung, as proposed in Handel 1998, then there is no mismatch in the medial.For another example of the same final correspondence, cf. #820 孔, Mand. kǒng.
[ZJH]

It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>

<par>WT <latinform>rgya</latinform>, glossed in Jäschke (p. 104) as "seal, stamp, mark, sign, token" is perhaps the origin of this ethnonym, which has somehow come to refer both to China and India, and by extension to European colonials in India. Jäschke observes (p. 106) that some scholars of his day derived it from <latinform>feringhi</latinform>, a general term for Europeans used since the Crusades, deriving ultimately from 'Frank, France, French.'</par><par>The Qiangic/rGyalrongic forms all seem to be loans from Tibetan.</par>

<par><hanform>沸</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯wət</latinform>, GSR #500q 'rushing, gushing (of a spring, the wind)'; Schuessler 2007:241 <latinform>*pət</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*put</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>fú</latinform>.</par>
