<par>This morpheme appears in cognate object constructions, e.g. Bengni <latinform>pɯ-pɯ pɯ</latinform> 'lay an egg', where the last element is the verb. The noun looks reduplicated in Bengni, but Apatani has <latinform>pa-pu</latinform> 'egg', with apparently distinct components. Similar cognate objects are characteristic of <xref ref="301">#301</xref>, e.g. Lahu <latinform>ɔ̀-u u ve</latinform> 'lay an egg', where the noun appears with a prefix.</par><par>This root may well be related to a morpheme with a more general meaning of BALL / EGG / ROUND OBJECT (<xref ref="1275">#1275 </xref>).</par>

孵 OC *pʼi̯ôg 'to hatch (eggs), incubate' ZJH; not in GSR #1233; Li 1971: (*phjəgw); Baxter 1992: (*ph(r)ju); Mand. fū.The earliest attested use of this Chinese character seems to be the Han Dynasty work Fangyan. However, it is clearly a later graphic variant of 孚, Mand. fú, which is attested writing ‘hatch’. Thus although the character may be of later development, the word itself obviously existed at the Old Chinese time period.Characters with the 孚 phonetic are generally placed in the OC 幽 Yōu rhyme group. However, some members of this phonetic series are found in the Middle Chinese 虞 Yú rhyme, which is not regularly derivable from the OC 幽 Yōu group. Neither Li nor Baxter specifically discusses the difficulties of reconstructing this set of characters. Baxter (1992:757) does however list a reconstruction for 孚 (square brackets represent irregular development): 孚 [fú] < [phju] < *ph(r)ju. It is treated as an irregular development from the OC 幽 Yōu group into the MC 虞 Yú rhyme. I have therefore provided a parallel reconstruction for 孵 in the systems of Li and Baxter.Karlgren reconstructs other characters in this phonetic series with *-ug (equivalent to the OC 侯 Hóu rhyme group), which yields a regular development into MC. However, I have provided a Karlgren-system reconstruction based on the assumption that the word belongs in the 幽 Yōu rhyme group.The OC-PTB correspondence of finals is regular. PTB *-u and *-əw phonemically differ only in length, and show identical OC correspondences. In open syllables the long vowel *-əw (which could be written /uw/ or /uː/) is more common, and so cognate sets exemplifying this correspondence usually involve that vowel. Examples include 九 ‘nine’ (TB *d-kəw, OC *kjəgw; Mand. jiǔ), 鳩 ‘dove/pigeon’ (TB *khəw, OC *kjəgw; Mand. jiū) and #1733 (elsewhere in this volume). It is interesting to note that this correspondence seems to be attested only after grave initials.As for the mismatch in aspiration of the OC and PTB initials, this raises the broader issue of voicing and aspiration within and across Chinese and TB. PTB is reconstructed with a two-way voicing contrast (e.g. *p vs. *b), while Old Chinese is reconstructed with a three-way voicing and aspiration contrast (e.g. *p vs. *ph vs. *b). Voicing and aspiration correspondences between cognates are notoriously imprecise. This is because of various complex morphological processes, not yet entirely understood, at play in word families on both sides, which can affect voicing and aspiration. On the Chinese side, it has become increasingly clear in recent decades that prefixal elements, such as *s- and various nasals, can voice or devoice root initials. (See for example Baxter and Sagart 1998, Sagart 1999, and Gong 2000.) It has also been argued that Chinese aspiration is mostly, or entirely, a secondary feature. (See Schuessler 2007:58ff for a recent articulation of this view.) Similar processes have been observed in various TB languages and posited for PTB. Because not all of these processes are fully understood, and because of the complex history of individual words and word families, it is not always possible to be sure that one is comparing etymological roots, rather than derived forms, in established OC/PTB cognate sets.For this reason the correspondence of PTB *p- with OC *ph-, for the comparison under discussion here, must be considered regular, with the assumption that aspiration in the Chinese form is a secondary development. Similarly, mismatches in voicing or aspiration will not be considered impediments in the proposal of Chinese comparanda for PTB etyma elsewhere in this volume. We assume, ultimately, that PTB voiceless initials correspond to Chinese voiceless initials, and that PTB voiced initials correspond to Chinese voiced initials, and that as our understanding of morphological processes on each side improves, these patterns of correlation will become more evident.
[ZJH]

<par>The first elements in the Bengni, Bokar, Galo, and Tagin forms are either morphemes meaning 'bird' or 'chicken' (cf. Damu <latinform>rok-pɯ</latinform>, where <latinform>rok</latinform> clearly < <reconstruction>*k-rak</reconstruction> 'chicken'), or else reduplications of the root.</par>
<par>R.S. Cook (p.c., 2002) has noted the similarity of these forms to the reflexes of <xref ref="645">#645</xref>, another 'verminous / venomous species'.</par>
<par>This root is very well attested in TB, with plausible Chinese comparanda.</par>
<par>Cf. <hanform>脅</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>xié</latinform>, comparandum for <xref ref="6186">#6186</xref>.</par>

<par>In the Southeast Asian linguistic area, yeast is used for brewing liquor rather than for baking bread. The semantic connection between SALT and YEAST can be understood as residing in the enlivening effect the two substances have on food and drink.</par><par>The nasal prefix attested overtly in many languages is also reflected by the voiced Lahu initial.</par>

<par>This root appears distinct from <xref ref="2729">#2729</xref>, which has a low vowel. Unlike Qiangic, we do not expect languages of the NE Indian Areal Group to have undergone a brightening change of <latinform>a > i</latinform>.</par>

<par>This is a rather complex word family in which all three dental suffixes /<latinform>-t, -s, -n</latinform>/ are attested. Reflexes include Kanauri <latinform>this</latinform> 'wet', Jingpho <latinform>mədìt</latinform> 'moisten sthg; wet, damp', Lalo <latinform>tíq</latinform> 'steep, soak', Kanauri <latinform>ti</latinform> 'water', Jingpho <latinform>mədī</latinform> 'moist, damp, wet', Lahu <latinform>dì</latinform> 'moisten due to sexual excitement (of a woman), ejaculate (of a man)'. The nasal prefix is reflected directly in Jingpho, and indirectly by the voiced Lahu initial.</par><par>Note the Kanauri form <latinform>thiss</latinform>, where the <latinform>-s</latinform> perhaps reflects the source of the <latinform>-t</latinform> to be found in other languages.</par><par>There is sometimes confusion between this root and <xref ref="300">#300</xref>, as in compounds of BIRD + WATER > EGG. Note the Kom Rem forms <latinform>mit ɣətui</latinform> 'eyeball' and <latinform>nəi tui</latinform> 'amniotic fluid', where the second element definitely means 'egg' in the former, but 'liquid' in the latter.</par>
<par>This root seems certainly to underlie <xref ref="2719">#2719</xref>, which shows a semantic extension towards the notion of flowing, as from a wound.</par>

<par>See STC #55 and Matisoff 1988a p. 705. STC #55, #167, and #168 should be combined as one root.</par><par>See HPTB <reconstruction>*twəy</reconstruction>, p. 194; <reconstruction>*twəy ⪤ *dwəy</reconstruction>, p. 195; <reconstruction>*twəy-n</reconstruction>, p. 451; <reconstruction>*ti(y)</reconstruction>, pp. 193, 194, 471; <reconstruction>*m-ti-s</reconstruction>, pp. 434, 351.</par><par>There are several likely Chinese comparanda, the best of which is probably <hanform>涕</hanform> OC <latinform>*t'iər</latinform> 'weep, tears', GSR #591m. In Mandarin, this word now means either 'tears' or 'snot' (<latinform>tì</latinform>). Cf. also <hanform>水</hanform> OC <latinform>*śi̯wər</latinform> 'water' GSR #576a-e, Mand. <latinform>shǔi</latinform>, and <hanform>川</hanform> 'river' Mand. <latinform>chuān</latinform>, the latter pointing to an allofam with a nasal final: <latinform>*m-twəy-n</latinform>.</par><par>This root seems independent of <xref ref="6170">#6170</xref>.</par>

涕 OC *t’iər, GSR #591m 'weep, tears'; Li 1971: (*thidx); Baxter 1992:792 *thijʔ; Mand. tì.The Middle Chinese reading would permit an Old Chinese reconstruction in either the OC 脂 Zhī rhyme group (*-id (Li)/*-ij (Baxter)) or the OC 微 Wēi rhyme group (*-əd (Li)/*-ɨj (Baxter)). Li does not reconstruct this word, but he assigns other words in GSR #591 to the 脂 Zhī group. Words in GSR #591 are reconstructed some with one vowel, some with the other by Baxter (see 1992:457ff for an explanation), but 涕 is reconstructed with *-ij because it rhymes unambiguously with a number of 脂 Zhī group words in Shījīng #203. Still, it is possible that in Baxter's system the word goes back to earlier *thɨjʔ, with vowel fronting occurring early in the dialect on which the Shījīng poem was based. Baxter proposes just such a development for other words in the phonetic series.If the OC vowel is reconstructed as *i, the vowel correspondence with the proposed TB cognate is regular (see #300 for examples). A 微 Wēi group reconstruction of *-əd (Li)/*-ɨj (Baxter) does not jeopardize the comparison, but this Chinese rhyme seems to correspond to TB *-ay more often than to TB *-əy. It may be that the correspondence with *-ay is regular while that with *-əy is irregular but not uncommon. See #519 and #1160, but note also 'hungry' 飢 OC *krjɨj (Baxter), TB *b-kri-(n/s); Mand. jī.On the aspiration mismatch in the initials, see the discussion under #1654.
[ZJH]
<par>Cf. also <xref ref="187">#187</xref>.</par>

水 OC *śi̯wər, GSR #576a-e 'water, river'; Li 1971: --; Baxter 1992 #1239: *h[l]jujʔ; Mand. shuǐ.Gong 1995 #133 reconstructs *hljədx and compares to WT chu 'water, brook, river', presumably assuming that the latter derives from an earlier form with a lateral initial. The comparison is, however, not tenable given the reconstruction of the PTB root #1631.This Chinese word is most likely related to PTB *lwi(y) 'flow, stream' (see STC #210), as proposed by Coblin (1986:158) and discussed in Handel (1998). A relationship with #298 seems unlikely because of the mismatch of initial consonants.
[ZJH]

川 OC *t̑’i̯wən, GSR #462a 'river'; Li 1971: *thjiən ?; Baxter 1992 #1126: *KHju/on; Mand. chuān.The reconstruction of this Chinese word is problematic. As Karlgren (GSR #462a) notes, the Middle Chinese form is probably irregular. The Old Chinese rhyme group is uncertain, which is why Baxter gives two possibilities for the vowel. Baxter's capital *KH indicates a velar initial that palatalized irregularly in the development of Middle Chinese. Handel (1998) reconstructs *khlun and Schuessler (2007:195) *k-hlun, because GSR #462 looks like a lateral series. Schuessler further suggests that the Chinese word is related to PTB *kluːŋ 'river / valley', which has areal connections with Austroasiatic and Tai. However, this does not explain the *-n coda in Chinese.The proposed PTB comparison in this volume depends on an Old Chinese reconstruction like Li's with a dental initial, which in Baxter's system would look like *thjun. However, the vowel correspondence is still irregular, and the comparison further requires positing an *-n suffix not found in Tibeto-Burman.
[ZJH]

愛 OC *ʔəd, GSR #508a 'love'; Li 1971: *·ədh; Baxter 1992 #337: *ʔɨts; Mand. ài.The Chinese form most closely resembles the Karen form ʔai. <par>This areal etymon has its origins in Proto Kam-Tai <latinform>*na</latinform>, 'paddy field'. In particular, it has attracted scholarly interest in its role as a toponymic morpheme, easily recognizable in place names in e.g. southern China, which has historically been a Tai-speaking area. Take, for example, <hanform>那馬</hanform> Mand. <latinform>nàmǎ</latinform>, a township south of <hanform>南寧</hanform> Nanning city in <hanform>廣西</hanform> Guangxi province, as well numerous other toponyms with <hanform>那</hanform> in the vicinity of Nanning area (note that <hanform>那</hanform> <latinform>nà</latinform> here is simply the Chinese transcription of an (originally) non-Chinese toponymic component).</par><par>Of interest also are cases in certain ST languages where <latinform>*na</latinform> seems to have become part of the lexicon as a common noun early on; for instance, Chinese <hanform>洳</hanform>, Mand. <latinform>rù</latinform>,  'marsh, bog', which Schuessler (2007:447) and others have contended is cognate (see Comparanda; <hanform>洳</hanform> <latinform>rù</latinform> is etymologically unrelated to transcriptional <hanform>那</hanform> <latinform>nà</latinform> cited above). Given the substantial phonetic alteration seen in reflexes of <latinform>*na</latinform> in modern Chinese dialects, this would imply that <latinform>*na</latinform>, "borrowed" or not, must have entered the Chinese lexicon prior to the OC stage (see Comparanda; <hanform>洳</hanform> <latinform>rù</latinform> is etymologically unrelated to transcriptional <hanform>那</hanform> <latinform>nà</latinform> cited above). The historicity of Tibetan <latinform>na</latinform> 'meadow' and Jingpho <latinform>na</latinform> 'paddy' (also noted by Schuessler (ibid.)) is less obvious; however, we see little reason to assume that reflexes of <latinform>*na</latinform> in these languages are merely recent. Kayan <latinform>nà̤</latinform> 'paddy field', possibly a borrowing from Shan, may be an exception.</par>

<par><hanform>闞</hanform> OC <latinform>*χ̯ɑ̆m</latinform>~<latinform>*χɑm</latinform>~<latinform>*χɑ̂m</latinform>, GSR #607d 'roar, growl'; Schuessler 2007:530 <latinform>*h(r)âmʔ</latinform> or <latinform>*hrə̂mʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>xiǎn</latinform> ~ <latinform>hǎn</latinform>.</par>

The independence of the two roots is demonstrated by their co-occurrence in compounds, e.g. Lahu <latinform>pâ-pi(-li)</latinform> 'dimple' and <latinform>pâ-pì-qu</latinform> 'chin'.</par><par>Karenic has separate reflexes of the two roots.</par>

蠃 OC *lwɑ̂, GSR #14b; B & S 2011: *rˁojʔ {[r]ˁo[j]ʔ}; Mand. luǒ.
蝸 OC *kwɑ, GSR #18c; B & S 2011: *k.rˁoj (dial. › kˁroj); Mand. wō.

But then where did PKC final glide come from?</par>

The Tsangla forms in <latinform>-r</latinform> are of uncertain affiliation, since <latinform>-r</latinform> and <latinform>-t</latinform> do not usually cooccur in TB word-families.</par>

HPTB reconstructs two separate roots (<latinform>*kluŋ ⪤ kluk</latinform> and <latinform>*klyoŋ</latinform>), although it suggests that the two are related. We treat them here as co-allofamic.</par><par>Chinese <latinform>kuk ~ gi̯uk</latinform> 'valley' reflects the allofam with final stop.</par><par>It seems likely that this etymon is related to <xref ref="1277">#1277</xref>.</par>

<par>STC sets up two separate roots: <xref ref="330">#330</xref> <reconstruction>*kaːŋ</reconstruction> and glossed 'roast / toast / burn' and <xref ref="331">#331</xref> <reconstruction>*kaŋ</reconstruction> glossed 'dry up'. <par><hanform>炕</hanform> OC (not in GSR #698, probably <latinform>*k’ɑ̂ng</latinform>), 'heated brick bed / dry / to roast'; Mand. <latinform>kàng</latinform>.</par>

<par>The labial prefix is attested in Kuki-Chin and Naga. The Sema/Simi forms seem to show reduplication of the root. Palatalization of the initial has occurred in Nungic and Qiangic. The Wakung and Nungic forms provide evidence for the velar nasal final.</par>

<par><hanform>睹</hanform> OC <latinform>*to</latinform>, GSR #45c' 'look; see'; Schuessler 2007:218 <latinform>*tâʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*tˁaʔ</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>dǔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>The North Assam forms with initial sibilants perhaps point to a variant with prefixal <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> which preempted the root initial.</par>

<par><hanform>孫</hanform> OC <latinform>*swən</latinform>, GSR #434a-c 'grandchild, descendant'; B & S 2011: <latinform>*sˤun {[s]ˤu[n]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>sūn</latinform>.</par>
<par>This etymon was reconstructed as <latinform>lep</latinform> in STC #351.</par><par>The velar prefix is attested in Bodo, Thulung, and Jingpho.</par>

<par>This etymon is still not well established, with only scattered reflexes in Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese, Karenic, and Baic. The final nasal is reconstructed on the basis of the vowel nasalization in some Bai dialects.</par>

<par>The fourth syllable of Jingpho <latinform>n-mun n-mau</latinform> cannot be directly assigned to this root, since the Jingpho reflex of PTB <latinform>*mul</latinform> is <latinform>mun</latinform>. Bola <latinform>ʃă³⁵ mau³⁵</latinform> might well be a borrowing from Jingpho.</par>
<par>Although a number of rGyalrongic forms apparently descend from this root, many others are better assigned to a prototype with front vowel, i.e. <xref ref="368">#368</xref>.</par>
<par>The denasalization of <latinform>m-</latinform> to <latinform>p-</latinform> in this Chepang form is undoubtedly due to assimilation to the final stop of the preceding syllable.</par>

眉 OC *mi̯ər GSR #567a-c; B & S 2011 *mrər; Mand. méi.
毛 OC *mog, GSR 1137a-b; B & S 2011 *C.mˁaw; Mand. máo.

<par><hanform>肌</hanform> OC <latinform>*ki̯ɛr</latinform>, GSR #602d; B & S 2011: <latinform>*krəj {krə[j]}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>jī</latinform>.</par>

解 OC *kĕg, GSR #861a 'cut up, divide; loosen; explain'; Schuessler 2007:313 *krêʔ; B & S 2011: *kˁreʔ; Mand. jiě.

<par><hanform>箬</hanform> OC <latinform>*ńi̯ɑk</latinform>, GSR #777g-h 'skin of bamboo'; Schuessler 2007:448 (<latinform>*nak</latinform>); B & S 2011: (<latinform>*nak</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>ruò</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>屎</hanform> OC <latinform>*śi̯ər</latinform>, GSR #561d 'excrement'; Schuessler 2007:465 <latinform>*lhiʔ</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*qʰijʔ </latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shǐ</latinform>.</par>

<par>This etymon has a range of meanings from 'small' to 'child' to 'give birth'. Most reflexes descend from a diphthongal prototype in <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction>, but occasionally from monophthongal <reconstruction>*-a</reconstruction> (e.g. Yi Dafang <latinform>a³³ ŋa⁵⁵</latinform> [see <reconstruction>*g-sow</reconstruction> above], and Bokar Lhoba <latinform>a-ŋaː</latinform>), so that this is a good candidate for a putative PTB palatal suffix, one of the functions of which is to mark diminutives. See Matisoff 1995 and DL:783-784.</par>
<par>Chinese <hanform>兒</hanform> 'child' (Mand. <latinform>ér</latinform>) is a plausible comparandum.</par>

兒 OC *ńi̯ĕg, GSR #873a-d ‘child'; Li 1971: *ngrjig; Baxter 1992 #1452: *ngje; Mand. ér.In Li’s system, *-rj- is reconstructed to account for the palatalization of the velar initial in Middle Chinese. This palatalizing medial is no longer accepted by most scholars today. In Baxter’s system, medial *-r- blocks palatalization, and must be omitted.The TB/Chinese correspondences look good. We would expect *a vocalism in Chinese, but *i (Li) / *e (Baxter) could be the result of an original *a fusing irregularly with the palatal suffix. As seen in #519 and #1160), TB *-ay may correspond to OC *-əd (Li) / *-ɨj (Baxter), and there is some evidence that OC *-ig (Li) / *-e (Baxter) also can correspond to this same TB final. Consider, for example, ‘crab’ (STC #51), TB *d-kay, which is likely cognate to 蟹 OC *grigx 'crab' (Li), Mand xiè (see STC p. 166, GSR #861d).Schuessler (2007:225) believes that this is an area word, with connections to forms in Austroasiatic and Miao-Yao.
[ZJH]

<par>This root is very likely related to <xref ref="6031">#6031</xref>, the obvious connection being the goatees of hircine animals. However, in view of the large semantic difference between the two roots, we are keeping them separate for the time being.</par><par>The nasalization in the second syllable of one of the Idu forms seems due to the effect of the initial nasal in the third syllable.</par>
<par><latinform>↭ *cuŋ/k </latinform>FLESH/MEAT.</par>

<par><hanform>嚏</hanform> OC <latinform>*tied</latinform>, GSR #415e 'sneeze'; Schuessler 2007: (<latinform>*tîth</latinform>); B & S 2011: <latinform>*tˁit-s {[t]ˁi[t]-s}</latinform> (‹<latinform>*t.lˁ-</latinform> ?); Mand. <latinform>tì</latinform>.</par>

<par><hanform>樺</hanform> OC (<latinform>*gʼwå</latinform>), (not in GSR #44) 'birch'; Schuessler 2007:283 <latinform>*(g)wrâh</latinform>; B & S 2011: (<latinform>*qʷʰˁra-s</latinform>); Mand. <latinform>huà</latinform>.</par>

<par>Lepcha has an interesting doublet: <latinform>fot</latinform> < <latinform>*wat</latinform> ⪤ <latinform>rot</latinform> < <latinform>*r-wat</latinform>.</par><par>The WB form contains the "Lolo-Burmese velar animal prefix" (see <plainlatinform>HPTB §4.4.4(3)</plainlatinform>).</par>

<par>This etymon constitutes a large set in STC (#404), where it was originally reconstructed with <reconstruction>*-a- ⪤ *-i-</reconstruction> variation at the PTB level: <reconstruction>*s-riŋ ⪤ *s-raŋ</reconstruction>. Benedict later changed the reconstruction to <reconstruction>*śriŋ</reconstruction> (n. 252, n.128), explaining the <latinform>-a-</latinform> in WB <latinform>hraŋ</latinform> as being 'conditioned by the initial cluster'. I find this revision unconvincing, and prefer to posit vocalic variation at the proto-level.  (This is similar to the <reconstruction>*-ya- ⪤* -i-</reconstruction> variation found in several roots, notably EYE and PHEASANT. See STC pp. 84-5, VSTB pp. 40-1.) There is an excellent Chinese comparandum, <hanform>生</hanform>, reconstructed in GSR #812a-d as OC <reconstruction>*sĕng</reconstruction>/MC <latinform>ṣɒng</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>shēng</latinform>. But here too Karlgren notes an irregular vocalic development from OC to MC (one would have expected MC <latinform>ṣɛng</latinform>). The semantic fit between the TB forms and Chinese is extraordinarily good.</par>
<par>This root is perhaps to be reconstructed <reconstruction>*tsiŋ</reconstruction> at the PLB level (cf. Lahu <latinform>chɛ̂</latinform>).</par>

生 OC *sĕng GSR #812a, ‘live; bear, born; fresh’; Li 1971: *sring; Baxter 1992 #130: *srjeng; Mand. shēng.For the reconstruction of *-j- in Baxter’s system, see Baxter 1992:580-581. The development to Middle Chinese is regular in Baxter’s system, while it is irregular in Li’s system.This is a long-recognized cognate (see e.g. STC #404). The Chinese initial and coda correspond to the Tibeto-Burman. In Li’s reconstruction, the main vowel *i is also a perfect match for the TB vowel. Baxter’s OC system does not permit a final *-ing reconstruction. Baxter postulates developments *-ing > *-in and *-ing > *-eng (depending on dialect) predating the Old Chinese period (1992:299,563). In this case the comparative evidence points to *-ing > *-eng, and Baxter’s reconstruction is also a perfect match for the Tibeto-Burman.Other Chinese members of the word family (such as 青 ‘green; color of living things’, Mand. qīng ) apparently reflect PST allofams with vowel *a and/or lacking medial *r. See Schuessler (2007:431, 459-460) for tables comparing Chinese and Tibeto-Burman allofams.=====================================================性 姓 生 蒼Schuessler (2007:76, 460) further argues that this etymon derives from PST *sri ‘to be, exist’.
[ZJH]A better match for TO BE / EXIST is #2608, [JAM]
<par>support for voiceless initial comes from E Tusom data (not yet imported)</par>
<par>Both the <latinform>l-</latinform> and <latinform>m-</latinform> prefixes are attested in Written Tibetan. The final nasal shows variation in position of articulation.</par>

<par>This widespread etymon is similar to <xref ref="665">#665</xref> (below), in that it typically occurs as the second morpheme in binomes referring to orifices of the body, especially EAR, NOSTRIL, and ANUS; occasionally it occurs in compounds for VAGINA.</par><par>This etymon is certainly allofamically related to <xref ref="387">#387</xref>, as in the last syllable of, e.g. Lahu <latinform>ó-qō</latinform> 'head' < PLB <reconstruction>*bʷu²-ʔgoŋ²</reconstruction>.</par><par>It is also very possible that it is allofamically related to <xref ref="3432">#3432</xref>. For remarks on velar/laryngeal interchange in TB, see <xref ref="664">#664</xref> above.</par><par>There are excellent Chinese comparanda: <hanform>空</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*k'ung</reconstruction>, GSR #1172h 'hollow, empty' and <hanform>孔</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*k'ung</reconstruction>, GSR #1174a-b 'very, greatly; empty'. Another likely Chinese relative is <hanform>肛</hanform> 'anus', Mand. <latinform>gāng</latinform> (not in GSR #1172), though this might fit better with <xref ref="219">#219</xref>. See ZJH's discussion, below.</par><par>It is often difficult to distinguish reflexes of this etymon from those of <xref ref="665">#665</xref>, especially with respect to Loloish and Qiangic. More than one allofam are evidently involved; cf. e.g. Lahu <latinform>qhɔ</latinform> 'inside; hole' ⪤ <latinform>qō</latinform> 'hollow object'.</par>

<par><hanform>空</hanform> OC <latinform>*kʼung</latinform> GSR #1172h ‘hollow, empty’; Li 1971: <latinform>*khung</latinform>; Baxter:1992:771 <latinform>*khong</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>kōng</latinform>.</par><par><hanform>孔</hanform> OC <latinform>*kʼung</latinform>, GSR #1174a ‘very, greatly; empty’; Li 1971: <reconstruction>*khungx</reconstruction>; Baxter 1992: <reconstruction>*khongʔ</reconstruction>; Mand. <latinform>kǒng</latinform>.</par><par>This OC-PTB comparison is long-recognized.  See for example Simon 1929, Gong 1995 #75, Coblin 1986:71.</par><par>The Chinese forms are a perfect match for the TB reconstruction. For another example of this final correspondence, cf. <xref ref="3447">#3447</xref>.  On the aspiration mismatch in the initial corresponce, see the discussion under <xref ref="1654">#1654</xref>.</par><par>The two Chinese forms are clearly etymological doublets.  Schuessler (2007:335) speculates that <hanform>孔</hanform> <latinform>kǒng</latinform> may be an 'endoactive' derivation meaning 'hole', from <hanform>空</hanform> <latinform>kōng</latinform> ‘hollow, empty’, lit. 'that which is hollow, empty'.</par>

肛 OC (see below) 'lower intestines/anus', GSR: not in #1172; Mand. gāng.This word is not attested until the Middle Chinese period. The Old Chinese reconstruction would be *krung (Li)/*krong (Baxter) if we assume membership in GSR #1172; however, it is possible that the Old Chinese source is *krong (Li)/*krung (Baxter), and that the phonetic element of the character was chosen after the merger of these two OC finals. (The Mandarin pronunciation of 肛 gāng is irregular; we would expect jiāng.)Schuessler (2007:251) suggests a comparison with WT gźaŋ 'anus'; however, the vowel correspondence is not good (unless a late borrowing is involved). It is certainly possible that this word is in the same family as 空 kōng and 孔 kǒng, although the function of *-r- is not clear.

<par>The stronghold of this root is Loloish, with some support from Nungish.</par>

<par><hanform>貝</hanform> OC <latinform>*pwɑ̂d</latinform>, GSR #320a-e 'cowrie shell; currency'; Schuessler 2007:159 <latinform>*pâts</latinform> < <latinform>*pops</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bèi</latinform>.</par>

The <latinform>-t</latinform> and <latinform>-n</latinform> suffixes had a nominalizing force, giving meanings like 'food', 'rice', 'deer' (see the references to #66 in the Index of STC, p. 200).</par>

咀 OC *dzi̯o, GSR #46u 'chew'; Schuessler 2007:323 *dzaʔ; B & S 2011: (*dzaʔ); Mand. jǔ.

<par>This is by far the best-attested root for VAGINA in TB, with reflexes in the NE Indian Areal Group, Himalayish, Lolo-Burmese, Karenic, and Qiangic. While most of the supporting forms point to a prefixal <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> plus <reconstruction>*t-</reconstruction> root-initial, a few reflexes seem to have undergone metathesis, e.g. Hayu <latinform>pep-tshu</latinform>, Lakher (Mara) <latinform>³tshu</latinform>, etc.</par><par>Some of the reflexes are transcribed with high front vowel <latinform>-i</latinform> instead of <latinform>-u</latinform>, but this fronting seems to be secondary. Compare, e.g. the Abor-Miri, Apatani, and Galo forms; or the Chepang, Dumi, and Bantawa reflexes, etc.</par>

<par>This etymon was formerly reconstructed as <latinform>*s-ke-k ⪤ *m-ke-k</latinform>.</par><par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. <par>The second syllable of this binome bears a fortuitous similarity to the second syllable of Thai <latinform>məlɛɛŋ-mum</latinform> 'spider', literally "corner bug."</par>

<par>The most general form of this complex word family is reconstructed as <xref ref="163">#163</xref>.</par><par>Like <xref ref="1168">#1168</xref>, some of the reflexes of <reconstruction>*pʷam</reconstruction> have initial <latinform>w-</latinform> <xref ref="2108">#2108 </xref>, while others have an initial labial stop or derivative thereof <xref ref="673">#673 </xref>. All these forms meaning 'womb / placenta / nest / belly' clearly represent the same etymon as <reconstruction>*p-wam</reconstruction> BELLY, presented separately as <xref ref="137">#137 </xref>. A further group of forms with nasal initials <xref ref="3444">#3444 </xref> may also be brought into this word family. Finally, there is a strong likelihood that <xref ref="680">#680</xref> is also allofamically related.</par><par>Some languages (e.g. Jingpho, Lashi, Zaiwa) show internal variation between stop and semivowel. The semantic association between WOMB and BELLY is too obvious to belabor. <emph>Belly</emph> was used frequently in early Mod. Eng. to mean WOMB: "As yet my wife hath not laid her belly" (<emph>Plumpton Correspondence</emph> 1549-50); "My belly did not blab, so I was still a Mayde" (William Warner, <emph>Albion’s England</emph>, 1592); "Why, she may plead her belly at worst" (John Gay, <emph>The Beggar's Opera</emph>, 1728).<footnote>In former times, a pregnant female criminal condemned to death was allowed to bring the baby to term before being executed. This practice was known as "pleading one's belly". See OED 1971:789.</footnote></par><par>Still another related form is <xref ref="3">#3</xref>, found especially in Kuki-Chin, with a meaning which goes beyond the visceral / gastric area to cover the whole body.</par><par>Cf. also <xref ref="2111">#2111</xref>.</par>
<par>The first element in this compound seems to reflect <xref ref="2125">#2125</xref>, while the second element no doubt descends from <xref ref="529">#529</xref>.</par>

<par>The nasal-final allofam of this widespread root is attested in Chepang and Nungic. Note the semantic association in Tibetan between WING and SIDE OF BODY. The velar prefix shows up in Tibetan and Qiangic.</par><par>It is sometimes difficult to distinguish reflexes of this etymon from those of <xref ref="737">#737</xref>.</par><par>There is a Chinese comparandum.</par>

<par>It is possible that this root is actually an allofam of <latinform>*k-rak</latinform>, with pre-emption by the prefix, although there is little evidence of any rhotic element, with the possible exception of the Nungish forms with retroflex <latinform>ɖ</latinform>.</par>

<par>As proposed in Matisoff 1972:72 ("The Loloish tonal split revisited"), the unique initial cluster in PLB <reconstruction>*ʔwyik</reconstruction> is to be explained by a disyllabic prototype <reconstruction>*ʔu-(y)ik</reconstruction>, where the first syllable is from <xref ref="385">#385</xref>, an honorific morpheme frequently occurring in Tibeto-Burman words for elder kinsmen.</par>

<par>Two sorts of alternations are to be found with this etymon: between open and nasal final syllables, and between open and velar final syllables. Several languages have doublets reflecting two variants, e.g. Lepcha <latinform>bi~bín</latinform>, Hpun <latinform>pè</latinform> and <latinform>pèŋ</latinform>, Lahu (Black) <latinform>pèʔ</latinform> and <latinform>pî</latinform>, and Dulong <latinform>biŋ</latinform> and <latinform>bi⁵⁵</latinform>. The Chin languages have a thorough-going morphological alternation between open syllables in Form I and velar final syllables in Form II.</par>

謬 OC *mli̯ŏg, GSR #1069m 'to lie (i.e. 'twist words')'; Schuessler 2007:389 *mriuh; B & S 2011: *mə-riw-s (dial. › *mriw-s); Mand. miù.

<par>This excellent root, with a relatively rare laryngeal proto-initial, appears in the NE Indian Areal Group, Lolo-Burmese, and Qiangic, with a range of meanings including 'be pregnant'; 'give birth'; 'bring up, rear (a child)'; 'nourish, feed', covering the whole range of parental responsibility from conception to childhood. It is reconstructed in Matisoff 1985, 'God and the Sino-Tibetan copula' (note 69, p. 38) and in Matisoff 1988, <emph>The Dictionary of Lahu</emph> (p. 1071), and discussed in the context of TB laryngeal-initial etyma in general in Matisoff 1997 "Primary and secondary laryngeal initials in TB" (section 5.3).</par>

好 OC *χôg, GSR #1044a ‘good’ ⪤ ‘love, like’This comparison is correctly cited in HPTB (p. 58), although by an unfortunate error the Chinese character 愛 is given instead of 好. [JAM]; Li 1971: *həgwx/*həgwh; Baxter 1992: *xuʔ/ *xu(ʔ)s (737); Mand. hǎo 'good' ⪤ hào 'love, like'.The word for 'love, like' is a putative form derived from 'good' through *-s suffixation. This presents some semantic difficulty for the OC-PTB comparison, since the basic meaning of the PTB root is closer to that of the derived Chinese transitive verb.Corroborated as it is by other etyma (e.g. #1168, #1733), the correspondence TB *-u with OC *-əgw (Li)/*-u (Baxter) is well attested. The initial correspondence is difficult to evaluate because there are so few proposed cognates involving words with OC *h- (Li)/*x- (Baxter) or PTB *h-. Of these several (such as Gong 1995 #142 comparing OC 鼾 *xan ‘snore’ (Mand. hān) to PTB *hal ‘snore’) are clearly onomatopoetic. In other cases Gong has derived OC *x- from earlier *skh-, facilitating comparison with Written Tibetan forms having initial velars (see Gong 2000). Nevertheless, there is no specific reason to doubt the validity of the initial correspondence.
[ZJH]

<par>The final liquid is attested directly in Kaman, Karbi, Tsangla, and Tshona, and indirectly in Manang, Thakali, Bantawa, and Chinese (all of which have a final nasal). The final <latinform>-i</latinform> in Bantawa <latinform>ŋai wa</latinform> and Chamling <latinform>ngai</latinform> is also perhaps a reflex of this final liquid. The <latinform>*r-</latinform> prefix is attested in rGyalrongic.</par><par>The Written Tibetan rhyme <latinform>-o</latinform> is a regular reflex of PTB <latinform>*-wa</latinform> (cf. <xref ref="632">#632</xref> > WT <latinform>so</latinform>).</par><par>There is no apparent relation to PTai *<latinform>hnaa-C</latinform> 'face'.</par><par>The final nasals in the Tamang and Magar forms are either to be attributed to the final <latinform>-l</latinform> or to assimilation from the nasal initial.</par><par>There is a Chinese comparandum (<hanform>顏</hanform>) reconstructed in GSR 199c with final <latinform>-n</latinform>. Again, it is possible to assign this to <xref ref="2017">#2017</xref> rather than to the present allofam.</par>

<par>GSTC #129; <latinform>⪤ *tsaːy </latinform>'property, cattle, economically valuable animal'. Same root as GSTC #143 ‘elephant, cattle’ as discussed in Matisoff 1988, ‘Universal semantics and allofamic identification... property, livestock, talent.’</par>

<par>This etymon is attested in the NE Indian Areal Group and Himalayish, with a possible cognate in Loloish (Bisu). The initial shows variation between <latinform>l-</latinform> and <latinform>r-</latinform>. This root seems to be quite distinct from <xref ref="3472">#3472</xref>, below. The final <latinform>-t</latinform> in the Tangkhul forms is unexplained. See <plainlatinform>TBRS</plainlatinform> 151.</par>

<par>This is a Mandarin loan which appears in a number of Tibeto-Burman languages. Other areal words for DUCK that have been borrowed into TB are Indo-Aryan <xref ref="5716">#5716</xref>, Nepali <xref ref="5717">#5717</xref>, and Austro-Tai <xref ref="5713">#5713</xref>.</par>

See Matisoff 1991, "The mother of all morphemes".</par><par>The present root seems to overlap somewhat with <xref ref="5595">#5595</xref> and <xref ref="1623">#1623</xref>, and further microlinguistic work will be necessary to separate them out.</par>

糲 OC *lɑ̂d ~ *li̯ɑd ~ *lɑ̂t, GSR #340g 'grain (dehusked but not polished)'; Schuessler 2007:352 *rats; B & S 2011: *rat-s {([m]ə-)rat-s}; Mand. lì.
粺 OC *b’ĕg, GSR #874t 'fine grain'; Schuessler 2007:155 *brêh; B & S 2011: *C.bˁre-s {C.[b]ˁre-s}; Mand. bài.

<par>An Austronesian connection has long been suggested for this root (cf. Malay <latinform>bəras</latinform> < Proto-Indonesian <latinform>*bəɣa/s</latinform> 'husked rice') although this etymology has been rejected by Benedict (1975:104), who prefers to relate the WT form to Chinese 飯 'cooked rice or millet' (OC <latinform>*b'i̯wăn</latinform>; <plainlatinform>GSR 262i</plainlatinform>), on the grounds that the basic meaning of the TB etymon is FRUIT / BEAR FRUIT (cf. WT <latinform>ḥbras-bu</latinform> 'fruit, corn, grain'). See Gong 2001:23.</par>
<par><hanform>波</hanform> OC <latinform>*pwɑ̂</latinform>, GSR #25l 'wave, surge'; Baxter 92:1049 <latinform>*paj</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>bō</latinform>.</par>

<par>The nasal prefix is confirmed by the voiced initials in Lahu. The <latinform>r-</latinform> and <latinform>d-</latinform> prefixes are directly attested in Tibetan. It has been shown that the semantically mysterious <latinform>r-</latinform> prefix is reflected by a syllabic nasal in Jingpho (STC p. 109), indicating that the former is more ancient in TB as a whole. The <latinform>d-</latinform> in Tibetan is not paralleled elsewhere as far as is now known.</par>

璐 OC *glɑ̂g, GSR #766r' 'beautiful jade'; Schuessler 2007: (*râkh); B & S 2011: (*Cə.rˁak-s); Mand. lù.

As an interesting sidelight, this root is attested in Romani <emph>khan(d)</emph>, where it means 'stink, stench, smell' (p.c. Terrence S. Kaufman).</par><par>The Mikir forms descend immediately from Assamese <emph>gandhaka</emph>. Cf. Turner 1966:214-5.</par>

<par>The present allofam of this complex word family has reflexes both with open <latinform>-a</latinform> and with a <latinform>-t</latinform> final. The latter variant is attested in Jingpho, Lepcha, Magar, Limbu, and Chinese.</par>

筆 OC *pli̯ət, GSR #502d 'writing pencil'; B & S 2011: *p.rut {p.[r]ut}; Mand. bǐ.Note: Cf. #107. In Chinese, 筆 bǐ has the additional sense 'write (v.)'.Also cf. 理 lǐ, comparandum for #2616.

<par>This etymon displays two well-attested patterns of variation: between <latinform>*-i-</latinform> and <latinform>*-u-</latinform>,  and between <latinform>*-i-</latinform> and <latinform>*-ya-</latinform>. Similar allofamic behavior is shown by <xref ref="363">#363</xref>.</par><par>The meanings of the reflexes range from PINCH / SQUEEZE to PRESS / OPPRESS  to SUBMERGE / SINK INTO / WEST, the common semantic link being downward motion. There is a resemblant root, <xref ref="2407">#2407</xref>, with similar meanings related to downward motion.</par><par>STC sets up two separate roots in this area, <latinform>*nup ⪤ *nip</latinform> 'sink' and <latinform>*nyap</latinform> 'pinch, squeeze', although it seems best to combine them into a single etymon.</par><par>Note the large number of Kuki-Chin allofams of this semantically highly differentiated root.</par>

<par>This etymon definitely has an onomatopoetic component (witness the English near-homophone <latinform>hawk</latinform>). There is a possible allofamic relationship with <xref ref="1795">#1795</xref> and/or <xref ref="540">#540</xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon resembles <xref ref="5575">#5575</xref>, but must be kept distinct, as witness the two Jingpho forms <latinform>myéʔ</latinform> from <xref ref="5575">#5575</xref>, and <latinform>mat</latinform> from the present root.</par><par>Some languages (e.g., Lotha, Rongmei, Tangkhul, Tsangla) have developed a dental nasal suffix instead of a dental stop.</par>

<par>There is a possible cognate to this Proto-Burmish root in Lotha Naga. If this is valid, the root should be promoted to the PTB level.</par>
<par>The scattered reflexes of this root all have dental stops, but these may well reflect an earlier fricative <reconstruction>**swar</reconstruction>. See HPTB: 31-32.</par>
<par>In addition to its Loloish reflexes, there are also probable cognates in the Naga languages. The <latinform>s-</latinform> prefix is motivated by the tones in several languages, e.g. Lahu.</par>
<par>This root seems to be specific to the Himalayan languages.</par>

<par>The semantic range of this root is interesting: the basic meaning seems to have been 'rot'; have spots of rot or mold', whence 'be spotted, mottled', thence to the notion of 'write' (perceived by preliterate people as making spots on paper). Matisoff 1972 (LTSR) treats ROT / SPOIL (#75) as a separate etymon from WRITE / MAKE SPOTS (#89).</par><par>This root resembles <xref ref="197">#197</xref>, but is best kept distinct.</par>

<par>This root is solidly established for PTB on the basis of the Jingpho and Burmese reflexes, with good confirmation from the Limbu form. It also is well attested in Qiangic, although the vocalism of some of these forms remains to be explained. The cognacy of the rGyalrongic forms is possible but not certain.</par>

<par>This etymon was already set up in Matisoff 1972a #170 as PLB <reconstruction>*r-lek</reconstruction>, on the basis of a single Loloish form (Akha) and WT <latinform>rlig-pa</latinform>. It is solidly attested in Himalayish, with possible cognates in Xixia and the NE Indian Areal Group (esp. Karbi).</par>

<par>This root is well-attested in Tibeto-Burman and Chinese, but has a fortuitous resemblance to an Indo-Aryan root (Skt. <emph>laśuna-</emph>, Hindi <emph>lahsun</emph>, Nepali <emph>lasun</emph>). In fact, many Tibeto-Burman languages (e.g. Maru <latinform>la³¹sɔn³⁵</latinform>, Chang <latinform>lasung</latinform>, Lotha <latinform>lasen</latinform>, Bantawa <latinform>losuna</latinform>, and Jingpho <latinform>la³¹son³³</latinform>) have borrowed the Indic root. In a couple of cases, a compound seems to be an Indic-TB hybrid, e.g. Achang <latinform>lɑ³¹suɑn⁵⁵</latinform>.</par><par>Compound formations with this etymon show an interesting range of semantic associations, including BITTER (e.g. Lisu), CHICKEN (e.g. Burmese), WHITE (e.g. Burmese), and SMELLY (e.g. Lahu).</par>

Proto-Waic <latinform>*lik</latinform> (Diffloth 1980:120); Lamet <latinform>lik</latinform> (Lindell et al. 1978: 17); Danaw <latinform>kǎ-lék³</latinform>; Mon <latinform>cli̱k</latinform> (Luce 1965:108) 'pig'." (Sun 1993:217).</par>

<par><hanform>軀</hanform> OC <latinform>*k’i̯u</latinform>, GSR #122g 'body'; Coblin 86: <latinform>*khjug ~ khjugh</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>qū</latinform>.</par>

<par>This root is virtually confined to Himalayish. The Konyak form with initial <latinform>h-</latinform> is also probably related. Note the two Thulung forms, which may be co-allofams; <latinform>theom</latinform> seems to fit better phonologically, despite the semantic divergence.</par>

<par><hanform>惟</hanform> OC <latinform>*d’i̯wər</latinform>, GSR #575n 'namely; <emph>copula</emph>'; Schuessler 2007:510 <latinform>*wi</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ɢʷij</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>wéi</latinform>.</par>

<par>There is variation between the fricative and affricate initials even within the same subgroup (e.g., Qiangic, Burmish). See <xref ref="2727">#2727</xref>.</par><par>This morpheme frequently occurs as the last element in compounds, with diminutive force.</par>

<par><hanform>厲</hanform> OC <latinform>*li̯ɑd</latinform>, GSR #340a 'ford'; Coblin 86:83 ST <latinform>*r̵jap</latinform> > PC <latinform>*ljabh</latinform> > OC <latinform>*ljadh</latinform>; Schuessler 2007:352 <latinform>*rats</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*rat-s {(mə-)rat-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>lì</latinform>.</par>

<par>The WT reflex for this root and a Chinese comparandum were first proposed by Coblin (1986) and were recapitulated in Schuessler (2007). As of now, primary supporting evidence is seen elsewhere than in Bodic, notably in Western Tani and Jingpho. Hopefully, additional corroborating data from spoken Tibetan dialects and Qiangic/rGyalrongic will some day become available.</par>
<par>This root seems to be restricted to TB languages of Nepal. The resemblance to Nepali <latinform>kapāl</latinform> is apparently accidental.</par>
<par>The Lepcha form <latinform>glyan̊</latinform> provides the best evidence for the allofam with the <latinform>*-yaŋ</latinform> rhyme.</par>

<par>In line with the frequently noted tendency of words meaning NECK / THROAT to have velar initials, we have reconstructed no fewer than eleven etyma with initial velars or velar clusters: <xref ref="389">#389</xref>, <xref ref="481">#481</xref>, <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="488">#488</xref>, <xref ref="489">#489</xref>, <xref ref="491">#491</xref>, <xref ref="493">#493</xref>, <xref ref="494">#494</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, <xref ref="496">#496</xref>, <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>, and <xref ref="5651">#5651</xref>.</par><par>It is highly likely that some of these etyma are allofams of each other. It is equally likely that a given supporting form may have been assigned to the wrong etymon. However, on the basis of the available data, this may be the best that can be done at this point.</par>

<par><hanform>喉</hanform> OC <latinform>*g’u</latinform>, GSR #113f 'throat'; Schuessler 2007:279 <latinform>*gô</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*ɡˁo {[ɡ]ˁ(r)o}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>hóu</latinform>.</par>
<par>It seems likely that this root is allofamically related to <xref ref="69">#69</xref>.</par>

峯/峰 OC (*p’i̯ung), GSR (not in #1197) 'peak, summit; hump (of camel)'; Schuessler 2007:238 *phoŋ; B & S 2011: (*pʰoŋ {pʰ(r)oŋ}); Mand. fēng.

<par><latinform>Comment:</latinform> Cf. the etyma <hanform>觸</hanform> <latinform>chù</latinform> 'butt, knock against', <hanform>椓</hanform> <latinform>zhuó</latinform> ‘beat, strike’, and <hanform>啄</hanform> <latinform>zhuó</latinform> 'peck', members of a larger set of comparanda for <xref ref="2709">#2709</xref>, q.v.</par>

<par><hanform>沸</hanform> OC <latinform>*pi̯wəd</latinform>, GSR #500q 'boil'; Schuessler 2007:233-4 <latinform>*pəts</latinform> < <latinform>*puts</latinform>; B & S 2011: <latinform>*Nə.put-s {Nə.p[u][t]-s}</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>fèi</latinform>.</par>

<par>The semantic range of this etymon is similar to that of English 'breast'; i.e. it can refer to the general thoracic area (like 'chest'), or specifically to a woman's mammary gland. In Southern Loloish this root typically occurs after <xref ref="3458">#3458</xref> [q.v.]. The phonological resemblance of this root to <xref ref="662">#662</xref> [q.v.] seems entirely fortuitous.</par>

<par>Chepang <latinform>nyat-</latinform> shows fronting of the final consonant to <latinform>-t</latinform>, but the doublet <latinform>nik-</latinform> preserves the original position of articulation.</par>

<par>VanBik (2009) sets up a PKC binome <latinform>may-ho(o)l</latinform>, with the first element meaning 'fire'. </par><par>This root bears some resemblance to <xref ref="694">#694</xref>, one of a large number of co-allofams of the complex word family <xref ref="6713">#6713</xref>.</par>

<par>The forms cited from Qiangic, rGyalrongic, and Nungic look like loans from Tibetan, especially since most of them closely resemble the Tibetan binome with the <latinform>-mo</latinform> suffix.</par>

<par>This root is reconstructed for Proto-Kiranti by Michailovsky (1991). The Burmese form definitely appears cognate. It is possible that this etymon should be reconstructed with the PTB rhyme <reconstruction>*-ul</reconstruction>, as suggested by apparently parallel examples: <xref ref="363">#363</xref> (> WB <latinform>mwê</latinform>); <xref ref="2623">#2623</xref> (> WB <latinform>mrwe</latinform>).</par>

<par>This is the best attested root with the meaning WING. The reflexes vary between a low central and a mid back vowel, suggesting a prototype with labial medial <reconstruction>*dwaŋ</reconstruction>. The two Black Lahu variants <latinform>tō-làʔ-qú ~ tɔ̄-làʔ-qú</latinform> reflect this: Lahu <latinform>o</latinform> frequently derives from <reconstruction>*-oŋ</reconstruction>, while Lahu <latinform>ɔ</latinform> often derives from <reconstruction>*-aŋ</reconstruction>. Similarly, Jingpho <latinform>sùm thāŋ lə phùm</latinform> 'arm' reflects <reconstruction>*-aŋ</reconstruction>, while Jingpho <latinform>dòŋ</latinform> 'cubit' comes from <reconstruction>*-oŋ</reconstruction>.</par><par>The Naxi and Zhaba forms reflect a variant with prenasalized initial.</par><par>There is an almost identical set of reflexes with the meaning CUBIT (see <xref ref="710">#710</xref>), which is undoubtedly related to the present root.</par><par>This root is to be distinguished from the phonologically similar <xref ref="439">#439</xref>.</par>

<par>This etymon was reconstructed as PTB <reconstruction>*m-dza</reconstruction> in STC #67, on the basis of forms from Written Tibetan, Jingpho, and Written Burmese (with a note that the Jingpho form actually ends in glottal stop, so that it "may be distinct"). There is in fact ample reason to set up both open-syllabled and stop-finalled allofams for this root: <reconstruction>*m-dza ⪤ *m-dzak</reconstruction>. The latter allofam is attested not only in Jingpho, but in Yi Nanhua (note the constricted vowel), and directly in NW rGyalrong (Qiangic group). The nasal prefix is directly reflected in WT, Yi (Dafang), and Jingpho.</par><par>The Hayu form has apparently acquired the <plainlatinform>-n</plainlatinform> suffix.</par>
<par>This splendid little root is well-established in Himalayish (Chepang, Dumi), with likely cognates in Barish (Lalung) and Karenic.</par>

<par>This widespread etymon seems to have an imitative component.</par>

The correspondences of initial consonant, main vowel, and final consonant are regular (including the vowel correspondence PTB <latinform>*a</latinform> : OC <latinform>*ə</latinform>).</par><par>Generally, we expect to see OC medial <latinform>*-r-</latinform> corresponding to PTB <latinform>*-r-</latinform>. <par>Note that for "mow", Jäschke 1881 and Marrison 1967 cite the Written Tibetan root <latinform>rṅa</latinform>, i.e. <latinform>rŋa</latinform>, while Coblin 1986:111 cites <latinform>rngap</latinform> , i.e. <latinform>rŋap</latinform>. Jäschke also shows <latinform>rṅab</latinform>, i.e. <latinform>rŋab</latinform> for "mow", but labels it as a "Western Tibetan" colloquial form.</par><par>We follow Coblin in treating <latinform>rngap/b</latinform> as possibly the more conservative Tibetan form, reflected in our PTB reconstruction by suffixed <latinform>-*p</latinform>. <par>This root seems basically to have meant 'ball', with natural extensions to EGG / TESTICLE and EYEBALL. According to K. P. Malla, it means literally "hot-fruits" (<latinform>si</latinform> < <xref ref="1019">#1019</xref>). <par>This newly established root seems quite solid. The Barish forms (esp. Dimasa, Kokborok) point to a diphthongal prototype, while most Loloish (including Jinuo) and Qiangic forms seem to reflect a monophthong. (<reconstruction>*-a</reconstruction> > <latinform>-i</latinform> is a common Qiangic development.) The Himalayish evidence is mixed: Bantawa has <latinform>-a</latinform>, but WT and Pattani have <latinform>-e</latinform> (presumably from <reconstruction>*-ay</reconstruction>). There is evidence from Bahing for alternation between voiced and voiceless initial stops, reflecting a distinction between simplex (‘be born’) vs. causative (‘give birth’).</par>
<par>Some reflexes of this root have front mid vowels, while others have back mid ones, suggesting a double-medial glide <latinform>*-yw-</latinform>.</par>

<par>This binome, used both for the actual animal and as a sign of the zodiac, is here set up for convenience as a PTB etymon, but it seems really to be a borrowing from Chinese to languages in several different areas of the Sinosphere. It apparently is derived from a compound meaning RABBIT-DONKEY, the point of comparison being the long ears of both species. Cf. Chinese <hanform>兔</hanform> <latinform>tù</latinform> 'rabbit', <hanform>驢</hanform> <latinform>lyú</latinform> 'donkey', and <hanform>騾</hanform> <latinform>luó</latinform> 'mule'. Some forms in TB languages are more recently borrowings from the Mandarin binome <latinform>tùzi</latinform>, with the suffixal morpheme <hanform>子</hanform> <latinform>zi</latinform> as second element.</par><par>The same semantic association between DONKEY and RABBIT is to be found in <xref ref="1432">#1432</xref>, and has been well-attested in Indo-Aryan; see <xref ref="5357">#5357</xref> and <xref ref="5358">#5358</xref>.</par><par>This morpheme was evidently borrowed into Qiangic before the "brightening" of earlier <latinform>*a</latinform> to Qiangic <latinform>i</latinform> took place.</par><par>The Tujia forms <latinform>mao³ tao²</latinform> and <latinform>mau²¹ tho⁵⁵ li⁵⁵</latinform> look as if they have incorporated Mandarin <latinform>māo</latinform> 'cat', with the tertium comparationis this time being the whiskers common to both species. On the other hand, the Achang word for 'donkey', <latinform>m̥ʑaŋ³¹ mau³¹ ɿiʔ³¹</latinform>, where the first syllable definitely means HORSE, has the same cat-like morpheme as the second syllable.</par><par>In Nungish, Bai, Lisu, and several Yi dialects, the words for DONKEY seem to be compounds consisting of this binome as first element followed by a root meaning HORSE, even though the independent words for 'horse' in these languages are somewhat different.</par><par>In Lalo (Western Loloish), the present binome means both 'rabbit' and 'donkey'. The fuller form, <latinform>haq-thoq-loq</latinform>, literally "rat-rabbit" disambiguates the word in favor of RABBIT. Alternatively, this Lalo compound might refer to a different species, the pika, as in Northern Lisu <latinform>hæ̃³⁵ thɔʔ²¹ la³³</latinform> 'Tibetan pika'.</par><par>In short, there seems to have been a good deal of interplay, not to say confusion, among morphemes referring to several different animal species. See the semantic diagrams.</par>

蚓 OC *di̯ĕn, GSR #371c ‘earthworm’; Li 1971: *rinx; Baxter 1992: *ljinʔ; Mand. yǐn.
螾 OC *di̯ən, GSR #450j ‘earthworm’; Li 1971: *rənx ?; Baxter 1992: *ljɨ/inʔ; Mand. yǐn.Benedict (STC p.37 note 121 and p. 171 note 457) argues that all three Chinese words are related and 'point... to an original initial such as *zr-'. These in turn are compared to TB *zril 'worm'.It is in footnote 121 that Benedict seems to relate TB *zril to Lushai (Mizo) til 'testicles', the TB etymon currently under discussion in this volume. Based on this and a handful of other comparisons, Benedict argues for the following developments from PST to Chinese: *zr- > *źr- > *d̑i̯ d̑i̯ is mistakenly written d'i in STC; in Karlgren's reconstruction d with an inverted breve above is distinct from d followed by an apostrophe, but as far as I can tell Benedict transcribes them identically, perhaps due to typographic limitations. varying with *zr- > *zy- > *y- > *di̯.Based on our current understanding of Old Chinese, Benedict's hypothesis is no longer sustainable, at least not in full. GSR #148 is a dental series while GSR #371 and #450 are lateral series. This and the vowel difference indicate that 蟺 shàn is not an allofam of 蚓 yǐn and 螾 yǐn.Peiros and Starostin 1996 v2:156 set 570 relate Chinese 蟺 shàn to Lushai (Mizo) tāl 'to struggle, wriggle, writhe'. See also Schuessler 2007:453. As for the latter two, the reconstruction in both Li's and Baxter's systems is difficult. The Middle Chinese forms might be descended from either OC *i or OC *ə (Li)/*ɨ (Baxter). Li and Baxter agree that GSR #371 has main vowel *i, but the reconstruction of GSR #450 is ambiguous.Schuessler 2007:574 explicitly relates 蚓 yǐn and 螾 yǐn, indicating that they are variant graphs used to write the same morpheme. He reconstructs OC *lə/inʔ or *jə/inʔ. While various attested binomial forms for 'earthworm' suggest that *i is the OC vowel, Min dialect forms point to *ə.As for the initial, it is now generally agreed that it should be *l or *j, not *r.Looking again at Benedict's comparison with PTB *zril, we note that the vowel and coda correspondences are regular. A comparison of PTB *zr- with OC *l- or *j- looks doubtful on phonetic grounds, but cannot be dismissed out of hand. Since *zr- is so rare in TB, it is difficult to establish regularity of correspondence.Whatever the fate of that comparison, Benedict's claim that these Chinese words are ultimately connected to #1181 now seems quite unlikely to be true.
[ZJH]

<par>This etymon is well-attested in the NE Indian Areal Group, with good-looking cognates in Himalayish and Loloish. Two allofams should be reconstructed, one with and one without final <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction>. The Idu and Loloish forms point to an allofam with *voiceless initial, while the Moyon form reflects an evidently secondary nasal prefix.</par>
<par>This interesting root shows lenition in Yakha. It occurs as final element in compounds with <xref ref="2012">#2012</xref>.</par>

<par>This root is abundantly attested in Lolo-Burmese and in Qiangic. It is reconstructed as PLB <reconstruction>*ʔkyak</reconstruction> in LTSR #58, with the meaning ROPE/STRING; LTSR also tentatively assigns several forms for NAVEL to this etymon.</par>

<par>There are two Chinese candidates for relationship to this etymon: <hanform>弋</hanform> <latinform>*di̯ək/i̯ək</latinform>, GSR #918a-b 'shoot with arrow with string attached; such an arrow' <plainlatinform>⪤</plainlatinform> <hanform>繳</hanform> OC <latinform>*t̑i̯ak/tśi̯ak</latinform>, GSR #1258e  [OC form not cited in GSR] 'string attached to arrow'. STC (p. 176) attempts to relate these Chinese words to PTB <latinform>*b-la</latinform> 'arrow' [STC #449], via a hypothetical intermediate form <latinform>**pli̯ak</latinform>. I consider this to be far-fetched phonologically and unsatisfying semantically. The semantic association between an umbilical cord and an arrow with string attached is irresistible. The metaphor is still alive today, e.g. in spacecraft, where the lifeline attaching a space-walking astronaut to the mother ship is commonly called an <emph>umbilical cord</emph>. For more discussion of these Chinese words, see ZJH's note below.</par>

<par><hanform>弋</hanform> OC <latinform>*di̯ək</latinform> GSR #918a, ‘shoot arrow (with string attached)’; Li 1971: <latinform>*rək</latinform>; Baxter 1992 #467: <latinform>*ljɨk</latinform>; Mand. <latinform>yì</latinform>.</par><par>There is considerable disagreement about the reconstruction of words with MC initial <latinform>j-</latinform> which appear in phonetic series with words having dental initials. While Karlgren’s <reconstruction>*d-</reconstruction> (GSR) is no longer accepted, variations of <reconstruction>*r-</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*l-</reconstruction>, and <reconstruction>*j-</reconstruction> are proposed by a number of scholars.</par><par>This Chinese word does not appear to be directly cognate to the PTB form, as there is no evidence of a velar initial.  Gong (2001) revives Benedict’s (STC p. 176) comparison with PTB <reconstruction>*b-la</reconstruction> ‘arrow’, reconstructing OC <reconstruction>*blək</reconstruction>, with the regular development <reconstruction>*bl-</reconstruction> > <reconstruction>*l-</reconstruction>.  Gong's system would also admit the possibility of reconstructing <reconstruction>*glək</reconstruction>.</par><par>A more likely candidate for cognacy is <hanform>繳</hanform> ( Mand. <latinform>zhuó</latinform>); see below.</par><par>Cf. <hanform>射</hanform> OC <latinform>*mljaks</latinform> Baxter 1992 #1357, 1393,  ‘shoot with bow’ (Mand. <latinform>shè</latinform>), with revision of <reconstruction>*L-</reconstruction> to <reconstruction>*ml-</reconstruction>. This may also be etymologically related to <hanform>弋</hanform> and/or PTB <reconstruction>*b-la</reconstruction>.</par>

繳 OC (see below), GSR #1258e ‘string attached to arrow’; Li 1971: (*krjakw?) Baxter: (*kjewk?); Mand. zhuó.This character has two Middle Chinese readings, one with velar initial and no coda, and one with palatal initial and velar coda. Based on internal Chinese evidence, the Old Chinese reconstruction cannot be determined with certainty. Karlgren placed this word in series #1258, which is not a phonetic series at all but a collection of words that Karlgren deemed unreconstructible for lack of evidence. Other characters that appear to have the same phonetic element appear in GSR #1162, all of which are reconstructed as open syllables with velar initial.The character 激 ‘dam up and cause (water) to rush up’ (Mand. jī), found in GSR #1162 and apparently sharing a phonetic element with 繳 zhuó, is reconstructed *kewk by Baxter 1992 and *kiakw by Li 1971, suggesting that in Baxter’s system, 繳 should be reconstructed *kjewk and *kewʔ to account for the two Middle Chinese pronunciations. An *a vocalism in Baxter's system cannot be completely discounted, but it would make it difficult to explain the subsequent palatalization of the initial velar in one of the Middle Chinese forms.All of the possible Old Chinese reconstructions present problems in terms of the Chinese/PTB vowel correspondence. Old Chinese coda *-kw (Li)/*-wk (Baxter) regularly corresponds to TB rounded vowels.Given the difficulty of determining the Old Chinese reconstruction of 繳, this proposed cognate set must be considered tentative.
[ZJH]
<par>See HPTB, pp. 318, 319.</par>

<par>STC #20 and HPTB p. 288 reconstruct the voiceless allofam with a long vowel because of the Lepcha vowel; a short medial PTB <latinform>-u-</latinform> tends to lower to Lepcha <latinform>-a-</latinform> while long <latinform>-uː-</latinform> remains <latinform>-u-</latinform>. Further data is needed to establish this point, however.</par>
<par>This weakly attested root appears mostly as the second syllable of Bodo-Garo compounds, with possibly a couple of cognates in Bodic.</par>
<par>Cf. the rather similar etymon <xref ref="3350">#3350</xref>.</par>
